speeches.speech: I thought the King had more affected the Duke of Albany than Cornwall.
lines: I thought the King had more affected the Duke of Albany than
Cornwall.

speeches.speech: It did always seem so to us; but now, in the division of the kingdom, it appears not which of the Dukes he values most, for equalities are so weigh'd that curiosity in neither can make choice of either's moiety.
lines: It did always seem so to us; but now, in the division of the
kingdom, it appears not which of the Dukes he values most, for
equalities are so weigh'd that curiosity in neither can make
choice of either's moiety.

speeches.speech: Is not this your son, my lord?
lines: Is not this your son, my lord?

speeches.speech: His breeding, sir, hath been at my charge. I have so often blush'd to acknowledge him that now I am braz'd to't.
lines: His breeding, sir, hath been at my charge. I have so often
blush'd to acknowledge him that now I am braz'd to't.

speeches.speech: I cannot conceive you.
lines: I cannot conceive you.

speeches.speech: Sir, this young fellow's mother could; whereupon she grew round-womb'd, and had indeed, sir, a son for her cradle ere she had a husband for her bed. Do you smell a fault?
lines: Sir, this young fellow's mother could; whereupon she grew
round-womb'd, and had indeed, sir, a son for her cradle ere she
had a husband for her bed. Do you smell a fault?

speeches.speech: I cannot wish the fault undone, the issue of it being so proper.
lines: I cannot wish the fault undone, the issue of it being so
proper.

speeches.speech: But I have, sir, a son by order of law, some year elder than this, who yet is no dearer in my account. Though this knave came something saucily into the world before he was sent for, yet was his mother fair, there was good sport at his making, and the whoreson must be acknowledged.- Do you know this noble gentleman, Edmund?
lines: But I have, sir, a son by order of law, some year elder than
this, who yet is no dearer in my account. Though this knave came
something saucily into the world before he was sent for, yet was
his mother fair, there was good sport at his making, and the
whoreson must be acknowledged.- Do you know this noble gentleman,
Edmund?

speeches.speech: [comes forward] No, my lord.
lines: [comes forward] No, my lord.

speeches.speech: My Lord of Kent. Remember him hereafter as my honourable friend.
lines: My Lord of Kent. Remember him hereafter as my honourable
friend.

speeches.speech: My services to your lordship.
lines: My services to your lordship.

speeches.speech: I must love you, and sue to know you better.
lines: I must love you, and sue to know you better.

speeches.speech: Sir, I shall study deserving.
lines: Sir, I shall study deserving.

speeches.speech: He hath been out nine years, and away he shall again. [Sound a sennet.] The King is coming.
lines: He hath been out nine years, and away he shall again.
[Sound a sennet.]
The King is coming.

speeches.speech: Attend the lords of France and Burgundy, Gloucester.
lines: Attend the lords of France and Burgundy, Gloucester.

speeches.speech: I shall, my liege.
lines: I shall, my liege.

speeches.speech: Meantime we shall express our darker purpose. Give me the map there. Know we have divided In three our kingdom; and 'tis our fast intent To shake all cares and business from our age, Conferring them on younger strengths while we Unburthen'd crawl toward death. Our son of Cornwall, And you, our no less loving son of Albany, We have this hour a constant will to publish Our daughters' several dowers, that future strife May be prevented now. The princes, France and Burgundy, Great rivals in our youngest daughter's love, Long in our court have made their amorous sojourn, And here are to be answer'd. Tell me, my daughters (Since now we will divest us both of rule, Interest of territory, cares of state), Which of you shall we say doth love us most? That we our largest bounty may extend Where nature doth with merit challenge. Goneril, Our eldest-born, speak first.
lines: Meantime we shall express our darker purpose.
Give me the map there. Know we have divided
In three our kingdom; and 'tis our fast intent
To shake all cares and business from our age,
Conferring them on younger strengths while we
Unburthen'd crawl toward death. Our son of Cornwall,
And you, our no less loving son of Albany,
We have this hour a constant will to publish
Our daughters' several dowers, that future strife
May be prevented now. The princes, France and Burgundy,
Great rivals in our youngest daughter's love,
Long in our court have made their amorous sojourn,
And here are to be answer'd. Tell me, my daughters
(Since now we will divest us both of rule,
Interest of territory, cares of state),
Which of you shall we say doth love us most?
That we our largest bounty may extend
Where nature doth with merit challenge. Goneril,
Our eldest-born, speak first.

speeches.speech: Sir, I love you more than words can wield the matter; Dearer than eyesight, space, and liberty; Beyond what can be valued, rich or rare; No less than life, with grace, health, beauty, honour; As much as child e'er lov'd, or father found; A love that makes breath poor, and speech unable. Beyond all manner of so much I love you.
lines: Sir, I love you more than words can wield the matter;
Dearer than eyesight, space, and liberty;
Beyond what can be valued, rich or rare;
No less than life, with grace, health, beauty, honour;
As much as child e'er lov'd, or father found;
A love that makes breath poor, and speech unable.
Beyond all manner of so much I love you.

speeches.speech: [aside] What shall Cordelia speak? Love, and be silent.
lines: [aside] What shall Cordelia speak? Love, and be silent.

speeches.speech: Of all these bounds, even from this line to this, With shadowy forests and with champains rich'd, With plenteous rivers and wide-skirted meads, We make thee lady. To thine and Albany's issue Be this perpetual.- What says our second daughter, Our dearest Regan, wife to Cornwall? Speak.
lines: Of all these bounds, even from this line to this,
With shadowy forests and with champains rich'd,
With plenteous rivers and wide-skirted meads,
We make thee lady. To thine and Albany's issue
Be this perpetual.- What says our second daughter,
Our dearest Regan, wife to Cornwall? Speak.

speeches.speech: Sir, I am made Of the selfsame metal that my sister is, And prize me at her worth. In my true heart I find she names my very deed of love; Only she comes too short, that I profess Myself an enemy to all other joys Which the most precious square of sense possesses, And find I am alone felicitate In your dear Highness' love.
lines: Sir, I am made
Of the selfsame metal that my sister is,
And prize me at her worth. In my true heart
I find she names my very deed of love;
Only she comes too short, that I profess
Myself an enemy to all other joys
Which the most precious square of sense possesses,
And find I am alone felicitate
In your dear Highness' love.

speeches.speech: [aside] Then poor Cordelia! And yet not so; since I am sure my love's More richer than my tongue.
lines: [aside] Then poor Cordelia!
And yet not so; since I am sure my love's
More richer than my tongue.

speeches.speech: To thee and thine hereditary ever Remain this ample third of our fair kingdom, No less in space, validity, and pleasure Than that conferr'd on Goneril.- Now, our joy, Although the last, not least; to whose young love The vines of France and milk of Burgundy Strive to be interest; what can you say to draw A third more opulent than your sisters? Speak.
lines: To thee and thine hereditary ever
Remain this ample third of our fair kingdom,
No less in space, validity, and pleasure
Than that conferr'd on Goneril.- Now, our joy,
Although the last, not least; to whose young love
The vines of France and milk of Burgundy
Strive to be interest; what can you say to draw
A third more opulent than your sisters? Speak.

speeches.speech: Nothing, my lord.
lines: Nothing, my lord.

speeches.speech: Nothing?
lines: Nothing?

speeches.speech: Nothing.
lines: Nothing.

speeches.speech: Nothing can come of nothing. Speak again.
lines: Nothing can come of nothing. Speak again.

speeches.speech: Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave My heart into my mouth. I love your Majesty According to my bond; no more nor less.
lines: Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave
My heart into my mouth. I love your Majesty
According to my bond; no more nor less.

speeches.speech: How, how, Cordelia? Mend your speech a little, Lest it may mar your fortunes.
lines: How, how, Cordelia? Mend your speech a little,
Lest it may mar your fortunes.

speeches.speech: Good my lord, You have begot me, bred me, lov'd me; I Return those duties back as are right fit, Obey you, love you, and most honour you. Why have my sisters husbands, if they say They love you all? Haply, when I shall wed, That lord whose hand must take my plight shall carry Half my love with him, half my care and duty. Sure I shall never marry like my sisters, To love my father all.
lines: Good my lord,
You have begot me, bred me, lov'd me; I
Return those duties back as are right fit,
Obey you, love you, and most honour you.
Why have my sisters husbands, if they say
They love you all? Haply, when I shall wed,
That lord whose hand must take my plight shall carry
Half my love with him, half my care and duty.
Sure I shall never marry like my sisters,
To love my father all.

speeches.speech: But goes thy heart with this?
lines: But goes thy heart with this?

speeches.speech: Ay, good my lord.
lines: Ay, good my lord.

speeches.speech: So young, and so untender?
lines: So young, and so untender?

speeches.speech: So young, my lord, and true.
lines: So young, my lord, and true.

speeches.speech: Let it be so! thy truth then be thy dower! For, by the sacred radiance of the sun, The mysteries of Hecate and the night; By all the operation of the orbs From whom we do exist and cease to be; Here I disclaim all my paternal care, Propinquity and property of blood, And as a stranger to my heart and me Hold thee from this for ever. The barbarous Scythian, Or he that makes his generation messes To gorge his appetite, shall to my bosom Be as well neighbour'd, pitied, and reliev'd, As thou my sometime daughter.
lines: Let it be so! thy truth then be thy dower!
For, by the sacred radiance of the sun,
The mysteries of Hecate and the night;
By all the operation of the orbs
From whom we do exist and cease to be;
Here I disclaim all my paternal care,
Propinquity and property of blood,
And as a stranger to my heart and me
Hold thee from this for ever. The barbarous Scythian,
Or he that makes his generation messes
To gorge his appetite, shall to my bosom
Be as well neighbour'd, pitied, and reliev'd,
As thou my sometime daughter.

speeches.speech: Good my liege-
lines: Good my liege-

speeches.speech: Peace, Kent! Come not between the dragon and his wrath. I lov'd her most, and thought to set my rest On her kind nursery.- Hence and avoid my sight!- So be my grave my peace as here I give Her father's heart from her! Call France! Who stirs? Call Burgundy! Cornwall and Albany, With my two daughters' dowers digest this third; Let pride, which she calls plainness, marry her. I do invest you jointly in my power, Preeminence, and all the large effects That troop with majesty. Ourself, by monthly course, With reservation of an hundred knights, By you to be sustain'd, shall our abode Make with you by due turns. Only we still retain The name, and all th' additions to a king. The sway, Revenue, execution of the rest, Beloved sons, be yours; which to confirm, This coronet part betwixt you.
lines: Peace, Kent!
Come not between the dragon and his wrath.
I lov'd her most, and thought to set my rest
On her kind nursery.- Hence and avoid my sight!-
So be my grave my peace as here I give
Her father's heart from her! Call France! Who stirs?
Call Burgundy! Cornwall and Albany,
With my two daughters' dowers digest this third;
Let pride, which she calls plainness, marry her.
I do invest you jointly in my power,
Preeminence, and all the large effects
That troop with majesty. Ourself, by monthly course,
With reservation of an hundred knights,
By you to be sustain'd, shall our abode
Make with you by due turns. Only we still retain
The name, and all th' additions to a king. The sway,
Revenue, execution of the rest,
Beloved sons, be yours; which to confirm,
This coronet part betwixt you.

speeches.speech: Royal Lear, Whom I have ever honour'd as my king, Lov'd as my father, as my master follow'd, As my great patron thought on in my prayers-
lines: Royal Lear,
Whom I have ever honour'd as my king,
Lov'd as my father, as my master follow'd,
As my great patron thought on in my prayers-

speeches.speech: The bow is bent and drawn; make from the shaft.
lines: The bow is bent and drawn; make from the shaft.

speeches.speech: Let it fall rather, though the fork invade The region of my heart! Be Kent unmannerly When Lear is mad. What wouldst thou do, old man? Think'st thou that duty shall have dread to speak When power to flattery bows? To plainness honour's bound When majesty falls to folly. Reverse thy doom; And in thy best consideration check This hideous rashness. Answer my life my judgment, Thy youngest daughter does not love thee least, Nor are those empty-hearted whose low sound Reverbs no hollowness.
lines: Let it fall rather, though the fork invade
The region of my heart! Be Kent unmannerly
When Lear is mad. What wouldst thou do, old man?
Think'st thou that duty shall have dread to speak
When power to flattery bows? To plainness honour's bound
When majesty falls to folly. Reverse thy doom;
And in thy best consideration check
This hideous rashness. Answer my life my judgment,
Thy youngest daughter does not love thee least,
Nor are those empty-hearted whose low sound
Reverbs no hollowness.

speeches.speech: Kent, on thy life, no more!
lines: Kent, on thy life, no more!

speeches.speech: My life I never held but as a pawn To wage against thine enemies; nor fear to lose it, Thy safety being the motive.
lines: My life I never held but as a pawn
To wage against thine enemies; nor fear to lose it,
Thy safety being the motive.

speeches.speech: Out of my sight!
lines: Out of my sight!

speeches.speech: See better, Lear, and let me still remain The true blank of thine eye.
lines: See better, Lear, and let me still remain
The true blank of thine eye.

speeches.speech: Now by Apollo-
lines: Now by Apollo-

speeches.speech: Now by Apollo, King, Thou swear'st thy gods in vain.
lines: Now by Apollo, King,
Thou swear'st thy gods in vain.

speeches.speech: O vassal! miscreant! [Lays his hand on his sword.]
lines: O vassal! miscreant! [Lays his hand on his sword.]

speeches.speech: [with Cornwall] Dear sir, forbear!
lines: [with Cornwall] Dear sir, forbear!

speeches.speech: Do! Kill thy physician, and the fee bestow Upon the foul disease. Revoke thy gift, Or, whilst I can vent clamour from my throat, I'll tell thee thou dost evil.
lines: Do!
Kill thy physician, and the fee bestow
Upon the foul disease. Revoke thy gift,
Or, whilst I can vent clamour from my throat,
I'll tell thee thou dost evil.

speeches.speech: Hear me, recreant! On thine allegiance, hear me! Since thou hast sought to make us break our vow- Which we durst never yet- and with strain'd pride To come between our sentence and our power,- Which nor our nature nor our place can bear,- Our potency made good, take thy reward. Five days we do allot thee for provision To shield thee from diseases of the world, And on the sixth to turn thy hated back Upon our kingdom. If, on the tenth day following, Thy banish'd trunk be found in our dominions, The moment is thy death. Away! By Jupiter, This shall not be revok'd.
lines: Hear me, recreant!
On thine allegiance, hear me!
Since thou hast sought to make us break our vow-
Which we durst never yet- and with strain'd pride
To come between our sentence and our power,-
Which nor our nature nor our place can bear,-
Our potency made good, take thy reward.
Five days we do allot thee for provision
To shield thee from diseases of the world,
And on the sixth to turn thy hated back
Upon our kingdom. If, on the tenth day following,
Thy banish'd trunk be found in our dominions,
The moment is thy death. Away! By Jupiter,
This shall not be revok'd.

speeches.speech: Fare thee well, King. Since thus thou wilt appear, Freedom lives hence, and banishment is here. [To Cordelia] The gods to their dear shelter take thee, maid, That justly think'st and hast most rightly said! [To Regan and Goneril] And your large speeches may your deeds approve, That good effects may spring from words of love. Thus Kent, O princes, bids you all adieu; He'll shape his old course in a country new. Exit.
lines: Fare thee well, King. Since thus thou wilt appear,
Freedom lives hence, and banishment is here.
[To Cordelia] The gods to their dear shelter take thee, maid,
That justly think'st and hast most rightly said!
[To Regan and Goneril] And your large speeches may your deeds
approve,
That good effects may spring from words of love.
Thus Kent, O princes, bids you all adieu;
He'll shape his old course in a country new. Exit.

speeches.speech: Here's France and Burgundy, my noble lord.
lines: Here's France and Burgundy, my noble lord.

speeches.speech: My Lord of Burgundy, We first address toward you, who with this king Hath rivall'd for our daughter. What in the least Will you require in present dower with her, Or cease your quest of love?
lines: My Lord of Burgundy,
We first address toward you, who with this king
Hath rivall'd for our daughter. What in the least
Will you require in present dower with her,
Or cease your quest of love?

speeches.speech: Most royal Majesty, I crave no more than hath your Highness offer'd, Nor will you tender less.
lines: Most royal Majesty,
I crave no more than hath your Highness offer'd,
Nor will you tender less.

speeches.speech: Right noble Burgundy, When she was dear to us, we did hold her so; But now her price is fall'n. Sir, there she stands. If aught within that little seeming substance, Or all of it, with our displeasure piec'd, And nothing more, may fitly like your Grace, She's there, and she is yours.
lines: Right noble Burgundy,
When she was dear to us, we did hold her so;
But now her price is fall'n. Sir, there she stands.
If aught within that little seeming substance,
Or all of it, with our displeasure piec'd,
And nothing more, may fitly like your Grace,
She's there, and she is yours.

speeches.speech: I know no answer.
lines: I know no answer.

speeches.speech: Will you, with those infirmities she owes, Unfriended, new adopted to our hate, Dow'r'd with our curse, and stranger'd with our oath, Take her, or leave her?
lines: Will you, with those infirmities she owes,
Unfriended, new adopted to our hate,
Dow'r'd with our curse, and stranger'd with our oath,
Take her, or leave her?

speeches.speech: Pardon me, royal sir. Election makes not up on such conditions.
lines: Pardon me, royal sir.
Election makes not up on such conditions.

speeches.speech: Then leave her, sir; for, by the pow'r that made me, I tell you all her wealth. [To France] For you, great King, I would not from your love make such a stray To match you where I hate; therefore beseech you T' avert your liking a more worthier way Than on a wretch whom nature is asham'd Almost t' acknowledge hers.
lines: Then leave her, sir; for, by the pow'r that made me,
I tell you all her wealth. [To France] For you, great King,
I would not from your love make such a stray
To match you where I hate; therefore beseech you
T' avert your liking a more worthier way
Than on a wretch whom nature is asham'd
Almost t' acknowledge hers.

speeches.speech: This is most strange, That she that even but now was your best object, The argument of your praise, balm of your age, Most best, most dearest, should in this trice of time Commit a thing so monstrous to dismantle So many folds of favour. Sure her offence Must be of such unnatural degree That monsters it, or your fore-vouch'd affection Fall'n into taint; which to believe of her Must be a faith that reason without miracle Should never plant in me.
lines: This is most strange,
That she that even but now was your best object,
The argument of your praise, balm of your age,
Most best, most dearest, should in this trice of time
Commit a thing so monstrous to dismantle
So many folds of favour. Sure her offence
Must be of such unnatural degree
That monsters it, or your fore-vouch'd affection
Fall'n into taint; which to believe of her
Must be a faith that reason without miracle
Should never plant in me.

speeches.speech: I yet beseech your Majesty, If for I want that glib and oily art To speak and purpose not, since what I well intend, I'll do't before I speak- that you make known It is no vicious blot, murther, or foulness, No unchaste action or dishonoured step, That hath depriv'd me of your grace and favour; But even for want of that for which I am richer- A still-soliciting eye, and such a tongue As I am glad I have not, though not to have it Hath lost me in your liking.
lines: I yet beseech your Majesty,
If for I want that glib and oily art
To speak and purpose not, since what I well intend,
I'll do't before I speak- that you make known
It is no vicious blot, murther, or foulness,
No unchaste action or dishonoured step,
That hath depriv'd me of your grace and favour;
But even for want of that for which I am richer-
A still-soliciting eye, and such a tongue
As I am glad I have not, though not to have it
Hath lost me in your liking.

speeches.speech: Better thou Hadst not been born than not t' have pleas'd me better.
lines: Better thou
Hadst not been born than not t' have pleas'd me better.

speeches.speech: Is it but this- a tardiness in nature Which often leaves the history unspoke That it intends to do? My Lord of Burgundy, What say you to the lady? Love's not love When it is mingled with regards that stands Aloof from th' entire point. Will you have her? She is herself a dowry.
lines: Is it but this- a tardiness in nature
Which often leaves the history unspoke
That it intends to do? My Lord of Burgundy,
What say you to the lady? Love's not love
When it is mingled with regards that stands
Aloof from th' entire point. Will you have her?
She is herself a dowry.

speeches.speech: Royal Lear, Give but that portion which yourself propos'd, And here I take Cordelia by the hand, Duchess of Burgundy.
lines: Royal Lear,
Give but that portion which yourself propos'd,
And here I take Cordelia by the hand,
Duchess of Burgundy.

speeches.speech: Nothing! I have sworn; I am firm.
lines: Nothing! I have sworn; I am firm.

speeches.speech: I am sorry then you have so lost a father That you must lose a husband.
lines: I am sorry then you have so lost a father
That you must lose a husband.

speeches.speech: Peace be with Burgundy! Since that respects of fortune are his love, I shall not be his wife.
lines: Peace be with Burgundy!
Since that respects of fortune are his love,
I shall not be his wife.

speeches.speech: Fairest Cordelia, that art most rich, being poor; Most choice, forsaken; and most lov'd, despis'd! Thee and thy virtues here I seize upon. Be it lawful I take up what's cast away. Gods, gods! 'tis strange that from their cold'st neglect My love should kindle to inflam'd respect. Thy dow'rless daughter, King, thrown to my chance, Is queen of us, of ours, and our fair France. Not all the dukes in wat'rish Burgundy Can buy this unpriz'd precious maid of me. Bid them farewell, Cordelia, though unkind. Thou losest here, a better where to find.
lines: Fairest Cordelia, that art most rich, being poor;
Most choice, forsaken; and most lov'd, despis'd!
Thee and thy virtues here I seize upon.
Be it lawful I take up what's cast away.
Gods, gods! 'tis strange that from their cold'st neglect
My love should kindle to inflam'd respect.
Thy dow'rless daughter, King, thrown to my chance,
Is queen of us, of ours, and our fair France.
Not all the dukes in wat'rish Burgundy
Can buy this unpriz'd precious maid of me.
Bid them farewell, Cordelia, though unkind.
Thou losest here, a better where to find.

speeches.speech: Thou hast her, France; let her be thine; for we Have no such daughter, nor shall ever see That face of hers again. Therefore be gone Without our grace, our love, our benison. Come, noble Burgundy.
lines: Thou hast her, France; let her be thine; for we
Have no such daughter, nor shall ever see
That face of hers again. Therefore be gone
Without our grace, our love, our benison.
Come, noble Burgundy.

speeches.speech: Bid farewell to your sisters.
lines: Bid farewell to your sisters.

speeches.speech: The jewels of our father, with wash'd eyes Cordelia leaves you. I know you what you are; And, like a sister, am most loath to call Your faults as they are nam'd. Use well our father. To your professed bosoms I commit him; But yet, alas, stood I within his grace, I would prefer him to a better place! So farewell to you both.
lines: The jewels of our father, with wash'd eyes
Cordelia leaves you. I know you what you are;
And, like a sister, am most loath to call
Your faults as they are nam'd. Use well our father.
To your professed bosoms I commit him;
But yet, alas, stood I within his grace,
I would prefer him to a better place!
So farewell to you both.

speeches.speech: Prescribe not us our duties.
lines: Prescribe not us our duties.

speeches.speech: Let your study Be to content your lord, who hath receiv'd you At fortune's alms. You have obedience scanted, And well are worth the want that you have wanted.
lines: Let your study
Be to content your lord, who hath receiv'd you
At fortune's alms. You have obedience scanted,
And well are worth the want that you have wanted.

speeches.speech: Time shall unfold what plighted cunning hides. Who cover faults, at last shame them derides. Well may you prosper!
lines: Time shall unfold what plighted cunning hides.
Who cover faults, at last shame them derides.
Well may you prosper!

speeches.speech: Come, my fair Cordelia.
lines: Come, my fair Cordelia.

speeches.speech: Sister, it is not little I have to say of what most nearly appertains to us both. I think our father will hence to-night.
lines: Sister, it is not little I have to say of what most nearly
appertains to us both. I think our father will hence to-night.

speeches.speech: That's most certain, and with you; next month with us.
lines: That's most certain, and with you; next month with us.

speeches.speech: You see how full of changes his age is. The observation we have made of it hath not been little. He always lov'd our sister most, and with what poor judgment he hath now cast her off appears too grossly.
lines: You see how full of changes his age is. The observation we
have made of it hath not been little. He always lov'd our
sister most, and with what poor judgment he hath now cast her
off appears too grossly.

speeches.speech: 'Tis the infirmity of his age; yet he hath ever but slenderly known himself.
lines: 'Tis the infirmity of his age; yet he hath ever but slenderly
known himself.

speeches.speech: The best and soundest of his time hath been but rash; then must we look to receive from his age, not alone the imperfections of long-ingraffed condition, but therewithal the unruly waywardness that infirm and choleric years bring with them.
lines: The best and soundest of his time hath been but rash; then
must we look to receive from his age, not alone the
imperfections of long-ingraffed condition, but therewithal
the unruly waywardness that infirm and choleric years bring with
them.

speeches.speech: Such unconstant starts are we like to have from him as this of Kent's banishment.
lines: Such unconstant starts are we like to have from him as this
of Kent's banishment.

speeches.speech: There is further compliment of leave-taking between France and him. Pray you let's hit together. If our father carry authority with such dispositions as he bears, this last surrender of his will but offend us.
lines: There is further compliment of leave-taking between France and
him. Pray you let's hit together. If our father carry authority
with such dispositions as he bears, this last surrender of his
will but offend us.

speeches.speech: We shall further think on't.
lines: We shall further think on't.

speeches.speech: We must do something, and i' th' heat.
lines: We must do something, and i' th' heat.

speeches.speech: Thou, Nature, art my goddess; to thy law My services are bound. Wherefore should I Stand in the plague of custom, and permit The curiosity of nations to deprive me, For that I am some twelve or fourteen moonshines Lag of a brother? Why bastard? wherefore base? When my dimensions are as well compact, My mind as generous, and my shape as true, As honest madam's issue? Why brand they us With base? with baseness? bastardy? base, base? Who, in the lusty stealth of nature, take More composition and fierce quality Than doth, within a dull, stale, tired bed, Go to th' creating a whole tribe of fops Got 'tween asleep and wake? Well then, Legitimate Edgar, I must have your land. Our father's love is to the bastard Edmund As to th' legitimate. Fine word- 'legitimate'! Well, my legitimate, if this letter speed, And my invention thrive, Edmund the base Shall top th' legitimate. I grow; I prosper. Now, gods, stand up for bastards!
lines: Thou, Nature, art my goddess; to thy law
My services are bound. Wherefore should I
Stand in the plague of custom, and permit
The curiosity of nations to deprive me,
For that I am some twelve or fourteen moonshines
Lag of a brother? Why bastard? wherefore base?
When my dimensions are as well compact,
My mind as generous, and my shape as true,
As honest madam's issue? Why brand they us
With base? with baseness? bastardy? base, base?
Who, in the lusty stealth of nature, take
More composition and fierce quality
Than doth, within a dull, stale, tired bed,
Go to th' creating a whole tribe of fops
Got 'tween asleep and wake? Well then,
Legitimate Edgar, I must have your land.
Our father's love is to the bastard Edmund
As to th' legitimate. Fine word- 'legitimate'!
Well, my legitimate, if this letter speed,
And my invention thrive, Edmund the base
Shall top th' legitimate. I grow; I prosper.
Now, gods, stand up for bastards!

speeches.speech: Kent banish'd thus? and France in choler parted? And the King gone to-night? subscrib'd his pow'r? Confin'd to exhibition? All this done Upon the gad? Edmund, how now? What news?
lines: Kent banish'd thus? and France in choler parted?
And the King gone to-night? subscrib'd his pow'r?
Confin'd to exhibition? All this done
Upon the gad? Edmund, how now? What news?

speeches.speech: So please your lordship, none.
lines: So please your lordship, none.

speeches.speech: Why so earnestly seek you to put up that letter?
lines: Why so earnestly seek you to put up that letter?

speeches.speech: I know no news, my lord.
lines: I know no news, my lord.

speeches.speech: What paper were you reading?
lines: What paper were you reading?

speeches.speech: Nothing, my lord.
lines: Nothing, my lord.

speeches.speech: No? What needed then that terrible dispatch of it into your pocket? The quality of nothing hath not such need to hide itself. Let's see. Come, if it be nothing, I shall not need spectacles.
lines: No? What needed then that terrible dispatch of it into your
pocket? The quality of nothing hath not such need to hide
itself. Let's see. Come, if it be nothing, I shall not need
spectacles.

speeches.speech: I beseech you, sir, pardon me. It is a letter from my brother that I have not all o'er-read; and for so much as I have perus'd, I find it not fit for your o'erlooking.
lines: I beseech you, sir, pardon me. It is a letter from my brother
that I have not all o'er-read; and for so much as I have
perus'd, I find it not fit for your o'erlooking.

speeches.speech: Give me the letter, sir.
lines: Give me the letter, sir.

speeches.speech: I shall offend, either to detain or give it. The contents, as in part I understand them, are to blame.
lines: I shall offend, either to detain or give it. The contents, as
in part I understand them, are to blame.

speeches.speech: Let's see, let's see!
lines: Let's see, let's see!

speeches.speech: I hope, for my brother's justification, he wrote this but as an essay or taste of my virtue.
lines: I hope, for my brother's justification, he wrote this but as
an essay or taste of my virtue.

speeches.speech: [reads] 'This policy and reverence of age makes the world bitter to the best of our times; keeps our fortunes from us till our oldness cannot relish them. I begin to find an idle and fond bondage in the oppression of aged tyranny, who sways, not as it hath power, but as it is suffer'd. Come to me, that of this I may speak more. If our father would sleep till I wak'd him, you should enjoy half his revenue for ever, and live the beloved of your brother, 'EDGAR.' Hum! Conspiracy? 'Sleep till I wak'd him, you should enjoy half his revenue.' My son Edgar! Had he a hand to write this? a heart and brain to breed it in? When came this to you? Who brought it?
lines: [reads] 'This policy and reverence of age makes the world
bitter to the best of our times; keeps our fortunes from us
till our oldness cannot relish them. I begin to find an idle
and fond bondage in the oppression of aged tyranny, who sways,
not as it hath power, but as it is suffer'd. Come to me, that
of this I may speak more. If our father would sleep till I
wak'd him, you should enjoy half his revenue for ever, and live
the beloved of your brother,
'EDGAR.'
Hum! Conspiracy? 'Sleep till I wak'd him, you should enjoy half
his revenue.' My son Edgar! Had he a hand to write this? a heart
and brain to breed it in? When came this to you? Who brought it?

speeches.speech: It was not brought me, my lord: there's the cunning of it. I found it thrown in at the casement of my closet.
lines: It was not brought me, my lord: there's the cunning of it. I
found it thrown in at the casement of my closet.

speeches.speech: You know the character to be your brother's?
lines: You know the character to be your brother's?

speeches.speech: If the matter were good, my lord, I durst swear it were his; but in respect of that, I would fain think it were not.
lines: If the matter were good, my lord, I durst swear it were his;
but in respect of that, I would fain think it were not.

speeches.speech: It is his.
lines: It is his.

speeches.speech: It is his hand, my lord; but I hope his heart is not in the contents.
lines: It is his hand, my lord; but I hope his heart is not in the
contents.

speeches.speech: Hath he never before sounded you in this business?
lines: Hath he never before sounded you in this business?

speeches.speech: Never, my lord. But I have heard him oft maintain it to be fit that, sons at perfect age, and fathers declining, the father should be as ward to the son, and the son manage his revenue.
lines: Never, my lord. But I have heard him oft maintain it to be fit
that, sons at perfect age, and fathers declining, the father
should be as ward to the son, and the son manage his revenue.

speeches.speech: O villain, villain! His very opinion in the letter! Abhorred villain! Unnatural, detested, brutish villain! worse than brutish! Go, sirrah, seek him. I'll apprehend him. Abominable villain! Where is he?
lines: O villain, villain! His very opinion in the letter! Abhorred
villain! Unnatural, detested, brutish villain! worse than
brutish! Go, sirrah, seek him. I'll apprehend him. Abominable
villain! Where is he?

speeches.speech: I do not well know, my lord. If it shall please you to suspend your indignation against my brother till you can derive from him better testimony of his intent, you should run a certain course; where, if you violently proceed against him, mistaking his purpose, it would make a great gap in your own honour and shake in pieces the heart of his obedience. I dare pawn down my life for him that he hath writ this to feel my affection to your honour, and to no other pretence of danger.
lines: I do not well know, my lord. If it shall please you to suspend
your indignation against my brother till you can derive from him
better testimony of his intent, you should run a certain course;
where, if you violently proceed against him, mistaking his
purpose, it would make a great gap in your own honour and shake
in pieces the heart of his obedience. I dare pawn down my life
for him that he hath writ this to feel my affection to your
honour, and to no other pretence of danger.

speeches.speech: Think you so?
lines: Think you so?

speeches.speech: If your honour judge it meet, I will place you where you shall hear us confer of this and by an auricular assurance have your satisfaction, and that without any further delay than this very evening.
lines: If your honour judge it meet, I will place you where you shall
hear us confer of this and by an auricular assurance have your
satisfaction, and that without any further delay than this very
evening.

speeches.speech: He cannot be such a monster.
lines: He cannot be such a monster.

speeches.speech: Nor is not, sure.
lines: Nor is not, sure.

speeches.speech: To his father, that so tenderly and entirely loves him. Heaven and earth! Edmund, seek him out; wind me into him, I pray you; frame the business after your own wisdom. I would unstate myself to be in a due resolution.
lines: To his father, that so tenderly and entirely loves him.
Heaven and earth! Edmund, seek him out; wind me into him, I pray
you; frame the business after your own wisdom. I would unstate
myself to be in a due resolution.

speeches.speech: I will seek him, sir, presently; convey the business as I shall find means, and acquaint you withal.
lines: I will seek him, sir, presently; convey the business as I
shall find means, and acquaint you withal.

speeches.speech: These late eclipses in the sun and moon portend no good to us. Though the wisdom of nature can reason it thus and thus, yet nature finds itself scourg'd by the sequent effects. Love cools, friendship falls off, brothers divide. In cities, mutinies; in countries, discord; in palaces, treason; and the bond crack'd 'twixt son and father. This villain of mine comes under the prediction; there's son against father: the King falls from bias of nature; there's father against child. We have seen the best of our time. Machinations, hollowness, treachery, and all ruinous disorders follow us disquietly to our graves. Find out this villain, Edmund; it shall lose thee nothing; do it carefully. And the noble and true-hearted Kent banish'd! his offence, honesty! 'Tis strange.    Exit.
lines: These late eclipses in the sun and moon portend no good to
us. Though the wisdom of nature can reason it thus and thus, yet
nature finds itself scourg'd by the sequent effects. Love cools,
friendship falls off, brothers divide. In cities, mutinies; in
countries, discord; in palaces, treason; and the bond crack'd
'twixt son and father. This villain of mine comes under the
prediction; there's son against father: the King falls from bias
of nature; there's father against child. We have seen the best
of our time. Machinations, hollowness, treachery, and all
ruinous disorders follow us disquietly to our graves. Find out
this villain, Edmund; it shall lose thee nothing; do it
carefully. And the noble and true-hearted Kent banish'd! his
offence, honesty! 'Tis strange.    Exit.

speeches.speech: This is the excellent foppery of the world, that, when we are sick in fortune, often the surfeit of our own behaviour, we make guilty of our disasters the sun, the moon, and the stars; as if we were villains on necessity; fools by heavenly compulsion; knaves, thieves, and treachers by spherical pre-dominance; drunkards, liars, and adulterers by an enforc'd obedience of planetary influence; and all that we are evil in, by a divine thrusting on. An admirable evasion of whore-master man, to lay his goatish disposition to the charge of a star! My father compounded with my mother under the Dragon's Tail, and my nativity was under Ursa Major, so that it follows I am rough and lecherous. Fut! I should have been that I am, had the maidenliest star in the firmament twinkled on my bastardizing. Edgar- [Enter Edgar.] and pat! he comes, like the catastrophe of the old comedy. My cue is villainous melancholy, with a sigh like Tom o' Bedlam. O, these eclipses do portend these divisions! Fa, sol, la, mi.
lines: This is the excellent foppery of the world, that, when we are
sick in fortune, often the surfeit of our own behaviour, we make
guilty of our disasters the sun, the moon, and the stars; as if
we were villains on necessity; fools by heavenly compulsion;
knaves, thieves, and treachers by spherical pre-dominance;
drunkards, liars, and adulterers by an enforc'd obedience of
planetary influence; and all that we are evil in, by a divine
thrusting on. An admirable evasion of whore-master man, to lay
his goatish disposition to the charge of a star! My father
compounded with my mother under the Dragon's Tail, and my
nativity was under Ursa Major, so that it follows I am rough and
lecherous. Fut! I should have been that I am, had the
maidenliest star in the firmament twinkled on my bastardizing.
Edgar-
[Enter Edgar.]
and pat! he comes, like the catastrophe of the old comedy. My
cue is villainous melancholy, with a sigh like Tom o' Bedlam.
O, these eclipses do portend these divisions! Fa, sol, la, mi.

speeches.speech: How now, brother Edmund? What serious contemplation are you in?
lines: How now, brother Edmund? What serious contemplation are you
in?

speeches.speech: I am thinking, brother, of a prediction I read this other day, what should follow these eclipses.
lines: I am thinking, brother, of a prediction I read this other day,
what should follow these eclipses.

speeches.speech: Do you busy yourself with that?
lines: Do you busy yourself with that?

speeches.speech: I promise you, the effects he writes of succeed unhappily: as of unnaturalness between the child and the parent; death, dearth, dissolutions of ancient amities; divisions in state, menaces and maledictions against king and nobles; needless diffidences, banishment of friends, dissipation of cohorts, nuptial breaches, and I know not what.
lines: I promise you, the effects he writes of succeed unhappily: as
of unnaturalness between the child and the parent; death,
dearth, dissolutions of ancient amities; divisions in state,
menaces and maledictions against king and nobles; needless
diffidences, banishment of friends, dissipation of cohorts,
nuptial breaches, and I know not what.

speeches.speech: How long have you been a sectary astronomical?
lines: How long have you been a sectary astronomical?

speeches.speech: Come, come! When saw you my father last?
lines: Come, come! When saw you my father last?

speeches.speech: The night gone by.
lines: The night gone by.

speeches.speech: Spake you with him?
lines: Spake you with him?

speeches.speech: Ay, two hours together.
lines: Ay, two hours together.

speeches.speech: Parted you in good terms? Found you no displeasure in him by word or countenance
lines: Parted you in good terms? Found you no displeasure in him by
word or countenance

speeches.speech: None at all.
lines: None at all.

speeches.speech: Bethink yourself wherein you may have offended him; and at my entreaty forbear his presence until some little time hath qualified the heat of his displeasure, which at this instant so rageth in him that with the mischief of your person it would scarcely allay.
lines: Bethink yourself wherein you may have offended him; and at my
entreaty forbear his presence until some little time hath
qualified the heat of his displeasure, which at this instant so
rageth in him that with the mischief of your person it would
scarcely allay.

speeches.speech: Some villain hath done me wrong.
lines: Some villain hath done me wrong.

speeches.speech: That's my fear. I pray you have a continent forbearance till the speed of his rage goes slower; and, as I say, retire with me to my lodging, from whence I will fitly bring you to hear my lord speak. Pray ye, go! There's my key. If you do stir abroad, go arm'd.
lines: That's my fear. I pray you have a continent forbearance till
the speed of his rage goes slower; and, as I say, retire with me
to my lodging, from whence I will fitly bring you to hear my
lord speak. Pray ye, go! There's my key. If you do stir abroad,
go arm'd.

speeches.speech: Arm'd, brother?
lines: Arm'd, brother?

speeches.speech: Brother, I advise you to the best. Go arm'd. I am no honest man if there be any good meaning toward you. I have told you what I have seen and heard; but faintly, nothing like the image and horror of it. Pray you, away!
lines: Brother, I advise you to the best. Go arm'd. I am no honest man
if there be any good meaning toward you. I have told you what I
have seen and heard; but faintly, nothing like the image and
horror of it. Pray you, away!

speeches.speech: Shall I hear from you anon?
lines: Shall I hear from you anon?

speeches.speech: I do serve you in this business. [Exit Edgar.] A credulous father! and a brother noble, Whose nature is so far from doing harms That he suspects none; on whose foolish honesty My practices ride easy! I see the business. Let me, if not by birth, have lands by wit; All with me's meet that I can fashion fit. Exit.
lines: I do serve you in this business.
[Exit Edgar.]
A credulous father! and a brother noble,
Whose nature is so far from doing harms
That he suspects none; on whose foolish honesty
My practices ride easy! I see the business.
Let me, if not by birth, have lands by wit;
All with me's meet that I can fashion fit. Exit.

speeches.speech: Did my father strike my gentleman for chiding of his fool?
lines: Did my father strike my gentleman for chiding of his fool?

speeches.speech: Ay, madam.
lines: Ay, madam.

speeches.speech: By day and night, he wrongs me! Every hour He flashes into one gross crime or other That sets us all at odds. I'll not endure it. His knights grow riotous, and himself upbraids us On every trifle. When he returns from hunting, I will not speak with him. Say I am sick. If you come slack of former services, You shall do well; the fault of it I'll answer.
lines: By day and night, he wrongs me! Every hour
He flashes into one gross crime or other
That sets us all at odds. I'll not endure it.
His knights grow riotous, and himself upbraids us
On every trifle. When he returns from hunting,
I will not speak with him. Say I am sick.
If you come slack of former services,
You shall do well; the fault of it I'll answer.

speeches.speech: He's coming, madam; I hear him.
lines: He's coming, madam; I hear him.

speeches.speech: Put on what weary negligence you please, You and your fellows. I'd have it come to question. If he distaste it, let him to our sister, Whose mind and mine I know in that are one, Not to be overrul'd. Idle old man, That still would manage those authorities That he hath given away! Now, by my life, Old fools are babes again, and must be us'd With checks as flatteries, when they are seen abus'd. Remember what I have said.
lines: Put on what weary negligence you please,
You and your fellows. I'd have it come to question.
If he distaste it, let him to our sister,
Whose mind and mine I know in that are one,
Not to be overrul'd. Idle old man,
That still would manage those authorities
That he hath given away! Now, by my life,
Old fools are babes again, and must be us'd
With checks as flatteries, when they are seen abus'd.
Remember what I have said.

speeches.speech: Very well, madam.
lines: Very well, madam.

speeches.speech: And let his knights have colder looks among you. What grows of it, no matter. Advise your fellows so. I would breed from hence occasions, and I shall, That I may speak. I'll write straight to my sister To hold my very course. Prepare for dinner.
lines: And let his knights have colder looks among you.
What grows of it, no matter. Advise your fellows so.
I would breed from hence occasions, and I shall,
That I may speak. I'll write straight to my sister
To hold my very course. Prepare for dinner.

speeches.speech: If but as well I other accents borrow, That can my speech defuse, my good intent May carry through itself to that full issue For which I raz'd my likeness. Now, banish'd Kent, If thou canst serve where thou dost stand condemn'd, So may it come, thy master, whom thou lov'st, Shall find thee full of labours. Horns within. Enter Lear, [Knights,] and Attendants.
lines: If but as well I other accents borrow,
That can my speech defuse, my good intent
May carry through itself to that full issue
For which I raz'd my likeness. Now, banish'd Kent,
If thou canst serve where thou dost stand condemn'd,
So may it come, thy master, whom thou lov'st,
Shall find thee full of labours.
Horns within. Enter Lear, [Knights,] and Attendants.

speeches.speech: Let me not stay a jot for dinner; go get it ready. [Exit an Attendant.] How now? What art thou?
lines: Let me not stay a jot for dinner; go get it ready. [Exit
an Attendant.] How now? What art thou?

speeches.speech: A man, sir.
lines: A man, sir.

speeches.speech: What dost thou profess? What wouldst thou with us?
lines: What dost thou profess? What wouldst thou with us?

speeches.speech: I do profess to be no less than I seem, to serve him truly that will put me in trust, to love him that is honest, to converse with him that is wise and says little, to fear judgment, to fight when I cannot choose, and to eat no fish.
lines: I do profess to be no less than I seem, to serve him truly
that will put me in trust, to love him that is honest, to
converse with him that is wise and says little, to fear
judgment, to fight when I cannot choose, and to eat no fish.

speeches.speech: What art thou?
lines: What art thou?

speeches.speech: A very honest-hearted fellow, and as poor as the King.
lines: A very honest-hearted fellow, and as poor as the King.

speeches.speech: If thou be'st as poor for a subject as he's for a king, thou art poor enough. What wouldst thou?
lines: If thou be'st as poor for a subject as he's for a king, thou
art poor enough. What wouldst thou?

speeches.speech: Service.
lines: Service.

speeches.speech: Who wouldst thou serve?
lines: Who wouldst thou serve?

speeches.speech: You.
lines: You.

speeches.speech: Dost thou know me, fellow?
lines: Dost thou know me, fellow?

speeches.speech: No, sir; but you have that in your countenance which I would fain call master.
lines: No, sir; but you have that in your countenance which I would
fain call master.

speeches.speech: What's that?
lines: What's that?

speeches.speech: Authority.
lines: Authority.

speeches.speech: What services canst thou do?
lines: What services canst thou do?

speeches.speech: I can keep honest counsel, ride, run, mar a curious tale in telling it and deliver a plain message bluntly. That which ordinary men are fit for, I am qualified in, and the best of me is diligence.
lines: I can keep honest counsel, ride, run, mar a curious tale in
telling it and deliver a plain message bluntly. That which
ordinary men are fit for, I am qualified in, and the best of me
is diligence.

speeches.speech: How old art thou?
lines: How old art thou?

speeches.speech: Not so young, sir, to love a woman for singing, nor so old to dote on her for anything. I have years on my back forty-eight.
lines: Not so young, sir, to love a woman for singing, nor so old to
dote on her for anything. I have years on my back forty-eight.

speeches.speech: Follow me; thou shalt serve me. If I like thee no worse after dinner, I will not part from thee yet. Dinner, ho, dinner! Where's my knave? my fool? Go you and call my fool hither. [Exit an attendant.] [Enter [Oswald the] Steward.] You, you, sirrah, where's my daughter?
lines: Follow me; thou shalt serve me. If I like thee no worse after
dinner, I will not part from thee yet. Dinner, ho, dinner!
Where's my knave? my fool? Go you and call my fool hither.
[Exit an attendant.]
[Enter [Oswald the] Steward.]
You, you, sirrah, where's my daughter?

speeches.speech: So please you-                                      Exit.
lines: So please you-                                      Exit.

speeches.speech: What says the fellow there? Call the clotpoll back. [Exit a Knight.] Where's my fool, ho? I think the world's asleep. [Enter Knight] How now? Where's that mongrel?
lines: What says the fellow there? Call the clotpoll back.
[Exit a Knight.] Where's my fool, ho? I think the world's
asleep.
[Enter Knight]
How now? Where's that mongrel?

speeches.speech: He says, my lord, your daughter is not well.
lines: He says, my lord, your daughter is not well.

speeches.speech: Why came not the slave back to me when I call'd him?
lines: Why came not the slave back to me when I call'd him?

speeches.speech: Sir, he answered me in the roundest manner, he would not.
lines: Sir, he answered me in the roundest manner, he would not.

speeches.speech: He would not?
lines: He would not?

speeches.speech: My lord, I know not what the matter is; but to my judgment your Highness is not entertain'd with that ceremonious affection as you were wont. There's a great abatement of kindness appears as well in the general dependants as in the Duke himself also and your daughter.
lines: My lord, I know not what the matter is; but to my judgment
your Highness is not entertain'd with that ceremonious affection
as you were wont. There's a great abatement of kindness appears
as well in the general dependants as in the Duke himself also
and your daughter.

speeches.speech: Ha! say'st thou so?
lines: Ha! say'st thou so?

speeches.speech: I beseech you pardon me, my lord, if I be mistaken; for my duty cannot be silent when I think your Highness wrong'd.
lines: I beseech you pardon me, my lord, if I be mistaken; for
my duty cannot be silent when I think your Highness wrong'd.

speeches.speech: Thou but rememb'rest me of mine own conception. I have perceived a most faint neglect of late, which I have rather blamed as mine own jealous curiosity than as a very pretence and purpose of unkindness. I will look further into't. But where's my fool? I have not seen him this two days.
lines: Thou but rememb'rest me of mine own conception. I have
perceived a most faint neglect of late, which I have rather
blamed as mine own jealous curiosity than as a very pretence
and purpose of unkindness. I will look further into't. But
where's my fool? I have not seen him this two days.

speeches.speech: Since my young lady's going into France, sir, the fool hath much pined away.
lines: Since my young lady's going into France, sir, the fool
hath much pined away.

speeches.speech: No more of that; I have noted it well. Go you and tell my daughter I would speak with her. [Exit Knight.] Go you, call hither my fool. [Exit an Attendant.] [Enter [Oswald the] Steward.] O, you, sir, you! Come you hither, sir. Who am I, sir?
lines: No more of that; I have noted it well. Go you and tell my
daughter I would speak with her. [Exit Knight.] Go you, call
hither my fool.
[Exit an Attendant.]
[Enter [Oswald the] Steward.]
O, you, sir, you! Come you hither, sir. Who am I, sir?

speeches.speech: My lady's father.
lines: My lady's father.

speeches.speech: 'My lady's father'? My lord's knave! You whoreson dog! you slave! you cur!
lines: 'My lady's father'? My lord's knave! You whoreson dog! you
slave! you cur!

speeches.speech: I am none of these, my lord; I beseech your pardon.
lines: I am none of these, my lord; I beseech your pardon.

speeches.speech: Do you bandy looks with me, you rascal?
lines: Do you bandy looks with me, you rascal?

speeches.speech: I'll not be strucken, my lord.
lines: I'll not be strucken, my lord.

speeches.speech: Nor tripp'd neither, you base football player?
lines: Nor tripp'd neither, you base football player?

speeches.speech: I thank thee, fellow. Thou serv'st me, and I'll love thee.
lines: I thank thee, fellow. Thou serv'st me, and I'll love thee.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, arise, away! I'll teach you differences. Away, away! If you will measure your lubber's length again, tarry; but away! Go to! Have you wisdom? So.
lines: Come, sir, arise, away! I'll teach you differences. Away,
away! If you will measure your lubber's length again, tarry; but
away! Go to! Have you wisdom? So.

speeches.speech: Now, my friendly knave, I thank thee. There's earnest of thy service.                  [Gives money.]
lines: Now, my friendly knave, I thank thee. There's earnest of thy
service.                  [Gives money.]

speeches.speech: Let me hire him too. Here's my coxcomb.
lines: Let me hire him too. Here's my coxcomb.

speeches.speech: How now, my pretty knave? How dost thou?
lines: How now, my pretty knave? How dost thou?

speeches.speech: Sirrah, you were best take my coxcomb.
lines: Sirrah, you were best take my coxcomb.

speeches.speech: Why, fool?
lines: Why, fool?

speeches.speech: Why? For taking one's part that's out of favour. Nay, an thou canst not smile as the wind sits, thou'lt catch cold shortly. There, take my coxcomb! Why, this fellow hath banish'd two on's daughters, and did the third a blessing against his will. If thou follow him, thou must needs wear my coxcomb.- How now, nuncle? Would I had two coxcombs and two daughters!
lines: Why? For taking one's part that's out of favour. Nay, an thou
canst not smile as the wind sits, thou'lt catch cold shortly.
There, take my coxcomb! Why, this fellow hath banish'd two on's
daughters, and did the third a blessing against his will. If
thou follow him, thou must needs wear my coxcomb.- How now,
nuncle? Would I had two coxcombs and two daughters!

speeches.speech: Why, my boy?
lines: Why, my boy?

speeches.speech: If I gave them all my living, I'ld keep my coxcombs myself. There's mine! beg another of thy daughters.
lines: If I gave them all my living, I'ld keep my coxcombs myself.
There's mine! beg another of thy daughters.

speeches.speech: Take heed, sirrah- the whip.
lines: Take heed, sirrah- the whip.

speeches.speech: Truth's a dog must to kennel; he must be whipp'd out, when Lady the brach may stand by th' fire and stink.
lines: Truth's a dog must to kennel; he must be whipp'd out, when
Lady the brach may stand by th' fire and stink.

speeches.speech: A pestilent gall to me!
lines: A pestilent gall to me!

speeches.speech: Sirrah, I'll teach thee a speech.
lines: Sirrah, I'll teach thee a speech.

speeches.speech: Do.
lines: Do.

speeches.speech: Mark it, nuncle. Have more than thou showest, Speak less than thou knowest, Lend less than thou owest, Ride more than thou goest, Learn more than thou trowest, Set less than thou throwest; Leave thy drink and thy whore, And keep in-a-door, And thou shalt have more Than two tens to a score.
lines: Mark it, nuncle.
Have more than thou showest,
Speak less than thou knowest,
Lend less than thou owest,
Ride more than thou goest,
Learn more than thou trowest,
Set less than thou throwest;
Leave thy drink and thy whore,
And keep in-a-door,
And thou shalt have more
Than two tens to a score.

speeches.speech: This is nothing, fool.
lines: This is nothing, fool.

speeches.speech: Then 'tis like the breath of an unfeed lawyer- you gave me nothing for't. Can you make no use of nothing, nuncle?
lines: Then 'tis like the breath of an unfeed lawyer- you gave me
nothing for't. Can you make no use of nothing, nuncle?

speeches.speech: Why, no, boy. Nothing can be made out of nothing.
lines: Why, no, boy. Nothing can be made out of nothing.

speeches.speech: [to Kent] Prithee tell him, so much the rent of his land comes to. He will not believe a fool.
lines: [to Kent] Prithee tell him, so much the rent of his land
comes to. He will not believe a fool.

speeches.speech: A bitter fool!
lines: A bitter fool!

speeches.speech: Dost thou know the difference, my boy, between a bitter fool and a sweet fool?
lines: Dost thou know the difference, my boy, between a bitter
fool and a sweet fool?

speeches.speech: No, lad; teach me.
lines: No, lad; teach me.

speeches.speech: That lord that counsell'd thee To give away thy land, Come place him here by me- Do thou for him stand. The sweet and bitter fool Will presently appear; The one in motley here, The other found out there.
lines: That lord that counsell'd thee
To give away thy land,
Come place him here by me-
Do thou for him stand.
The sweet and bitter fool
Will presently appear;
The one in motley here,
The other found out there.

speeches.speech: Dost thou call me fool, boy?
lines: Dost thou call me fool, boy?

speeches.speech: All thy other titles thou hast given away; that thou wast born with.
lines: All thy other titles thou hast given away; that thou wast
born with.

speeches.speech: This is not altogether fool, my lord.
lines: This is not altogether fool, my lord.

speeches.speech: No, faith; lords and great men will not let me. If I had a monopoly out, they would have part on't. And ladies too, they will not let me have all the fool to myself; they'll be snatching. Give me an egg, nuncle, and I'll give thee two crowns.
lines: No, faith; lords and great men will not let me. If I had a
monopoly out, they would have part on't. And ladies too, they
will not let me have all the fool to myself; they'll be
snatching. Give me an egg, nuncle, and I'll give thee two
crowns.

speeches.speech: What two crowns shall they be?
lines: What two crowns shall they be?

speeches.speech: Why, after I have cut the egg i' th' middle and eat up the meat, the two crowns of the egg. When thou clovest thy crown i' th' middle and gav'st away both parts, thou bor'st thine ass on thy back o'er the dirt. Thou hadst little wit in thy bald crown when thou gav'st thy golden one away. If I speak like myself in this, let him be whipp'd that first finds it so. [Sings]    Fools had ne'er less grace in a year, For wise men are grown foppish; They know not how their wits to wear, Their manners are so apish.
lines: Why, after I have cut the egg i' th' middle and eat up the
meat, the two crowns of the egg. When thou clovest thy crown i'
th' middle and gav'st away both parts, thou bor'st thine ass on
thy back o'er the dirt. Thou hadst little wit in thy bald crown
when thou gav'st thy golden one away. If I speak like myself in
this, let him be whipp'd that first finds it so.
[Sings]    Fools had ne'er less grace in a year,
For wise men are grown foppish;
They know not how their wits to wear,
Their manners are so apish.

speeches.speech: When were you wont to be so full of songs, sirrah?
lines: When were you wont to be so full of songs, sirrah?

speeches.speech: I have us'd it, nuncle, ever since thou mad'st thy daughters thy mother; for when thou gav'st them the rod, and put'st down thine own breeches, [Sings]    Then they for sudden joy did weep, And I for sorrow sung, That such a king should play bo-peep And go the fools among. Prithee, nuncle, keep a schoolmaster that can teach thy fool to lie. I would fain learn to lie.
lines: I have us'd it, nuncle, ever since thou mad'st thy daughters
thy mother; for when thou gav'st them the rod, and put'st down
thine own breeches,
[Sings]    Then they for sudden joy did weep,
And I for sorrow sung,
That such a king should play bo-peep
And go the fools among.
Prithee, nuncle, keep a schoolmaster that can teach thy fool to
lie. I would fain learn to lie.

speeches.speech: An you lie, sirrah, we'll have you whipp'd.
lines: An you lie, sirrah, we'll have you whipp'd.

speeches.speech: I marvel what kin thou and thy daughters are. They'll have me whipp'd for speaking true; thou'lt have me whipp'd for lying; and sometimes I am whipp'd for holding my peace. I had rather be any kind o' thing than a fool! And yet I would not be thee, nuncle. Thou hast pared thy wit o' both sides and left nothing i' th' middle. Here comes one o' the parings.
lines: I marvel what kin thou and thy daughters are. They'll have me
whipp'd for speaking true; thou'lt have me whipp'd for lying;
and sometimes I am whipp'd for holding my peace. I had rather be
any kind o' thing than a fool! And yet I would not be thee,
nuncle. Thou hast pared thy wit o' both sides and left nothing
i' th' middle. Here comes one o' the parings.

speeches.speech: How now, daughter? What makes that frontlet on? Methinks you are too much o' late i' th' frown.
lines: How now, daughter? What makes that frontlet on? Methinks you
are too much o' late i' th' frown.

speeches.speech: Thou wast a pretty fellow when thou hadst no need to care for her frowning. Now thou art an O without a figure. I am better than thou art now: I am a fool, thou art nothing. [To Goneril] Yes, forsooth, I will hold my tongue. So your face bids me, though you say nothing. Mum, mum! He that keeps nor crust nor crum, Weary of all, shall want some.- [Points at Lear] That's a sheal'd peascod.
lines: Thou wast a pretty fellow when thou hadst no need to care for
her frowning. Now thou art an O without a figure. I am better
than thou art now: I am a fool, thou art nothing.
[To Goneril] Yes, forsooth, I will hold my tongue. So your face
bids me, though you say nothing. Mum, mum!
He that keeps nor crust nor crum,
Weary of all, shall want some.-
[Points at Lear] That's a sheal'd peascod.

speeches.speech: Not only, sir, this your all-licens'd fool, But other of your insolent retinue Do hourly carp and quarrel, breaking forth In rank and not-to-be-endured riots. Sir, I had thought, by making this well known unto you, To have found a safe redress, but now grow fearful, By what yourself, too, late have spoke and done, That you protect this course, and put it on By your allowance; which if you should, the fault Would not scape censure, nor the redresses sleep, Which, in the tender of a wholesome weal, Might in their working do you that offence Which else were shame, that then necessity Must call discreet proceeding.
lines: Not only, sir, this your all-licens'd fool,
But other of your insolent retinue
Do hourly carp and quarrel, breaking forth
In rank and not-to-be-endured riots. Sir,
I had thought, by making this well known unto you,
To have found a safe redress, but now grow fearful,
By what yourself, too, late have spoke and done,
That you protect this course, and put it on
By your allowance; which if you should, the fault
Would not scape censure, nor the redresses sleep,
Which, in the tender of a wholesome weal,
Might in their working do you that offence
Which else were shame, that then necessity
Must call discreet proceeding.

speeches.speech: For you know, nuncle, The hedge-sparrow fed the cuckoo so long That it had it head bit off by it young. So out went the candle, and we were left darkling.
lines: For you know, nuncle,
The hedge-sparrow fed the cuckoo so long
That it had it head bit off by it young.
So out went the candle, and we were left darkling.

speeches.speech: Are you our daughter?
lines: Are you our daughter?

speeches.speech: Come, sir, I would you would make use of that good wisdom Whereof I know you are fraught, and put away These dispositions that of late transform you From what you rightly are.
lines: Come, sir,
I would you would make use of that good wisdom
Whereof I know you are fraught, and put away
These dispositions that of late transform you
From what you rightly are.

speeches.speech: May not an ass know when the cart draws the horse? Whoop, Jug, I love thee!
lines: May not an ass know when the cart draws the horse?
Whoop, Jug, I love thee!

speeches.speech: Doth any here know me? This is not Lear. Doth Lear walk thus? speak thus? Where are his eyes? Either his notion weakens, his discernings Are lethargied- Ha! waking? 'Tis not so! Who is it that can tell me who I am?
lines: Doth any here know me? This is not Lear.
Doth Lear walk thus? speak thus? Where are his eyes?
Either his notion weakens, his discernings
Are lethargied- Ha! waking? 'Tis not so!
Who is it that can tell me who I am?

speeches.speech: Lear's shadow.
lines: Lear's shadow.

speeches.speech: I would learn that; for, by the marks of sovereignty, Knowledge, and reason, I should be false persuaded I had daughters.
lines: I would learn that; for, by the marks of sovereignty,
Knowledge, and reason, I should be false persuaded
I had daughters.

speeches.speech: Which they will make an obedient father.
lines: Which they will make an obedient father.

speeches.speech: Your name, fair gentlewoman?
lines: Your name, fair gentlewoman?

speeches.speech: This admiration, sir, is much o' th' savour Of other your new pranks. I do beseech you To understand my purposes aright. As you are old and reverend, you should be wise. Here do you keep a hundred knights and squires; Men so disorder'd, so debosh'd, and bold That this our court, infected with their manners, Shows like a riotous inn. Epicurism and lust Make it more like a tavern or a brothel Than a grac'd palace. The shame itself doth speak For instant remedy. Be then desir'd By her that else will take the thing she begs A little to disquantity your train, And the remainder that shall still depend To be such men as may besort your age, Which know themselves, and you.
lines: This admiration, sir, is much o' th' savour
Of other your new pranks. I do beseech you
To understand my purposes aright.
As you are old and reverend, you should be wise.
Here do you keep a hundred knights and squires;
Men so disorder'd, so debosh'd, and bold
That this our court, infected with their manners,
Shows like a riotous inn. Epicurism and lust
Make it more like a tavern or a brothel
Than a grac'd palace. The shame itself doth speak
For instant remedy. Be then desir'd
By her that else will take the thing she begs
A little to disquantity your train,
And the remainder that shall still depend
To be such men as may besort your age,
Which know themselves, and you.

speeches.speech: Darkness and devils! Saddle my horses! Call my train together! Degenerate bastard, I'll not trouble thee; Yet have I left a daughter.
lines: Darkness and devils!
Saddle my horses! Call my train together!
Degenerate bastard, I'll not trouble thee;
Yet have I left a daughter.

speeches.speech: You strike my people, and your disorder'd rabble Make servants of their betters.
lines: You strike my people, and your disorder'd rabble
Make servants of their betters.

speeches.speech: Woe that too late repents!- O, sir, are you come? Is it your will? Speak, sir!- Prepare my horses. Ingratitude, thou marble-hearted fiend, More hideous when thou show'st thee in a child Than the sea-monster!
lines: Woe that too late repents!- O, sir, are you come?
Is it your will? Speak, sir!- Prepare my horses.
Ingratitude, thou marble-hearted fiend,
More hideous when thou show'st thee in a child
Than the sea-monster!

speeches.speech: Pray, sir, be patient.
lines: Pray, sir, be patient.

speeches.speech: [to Goneril] Detested kite, thou liest! My train are men of choice and rarest parts, That all particulars of duty know And in the most exact regard support The worships of their name.- O most small fault, How ugly didst thou in Cordelia show! Which, like an engine, wrench'd my frame of nature From the fix'd place; drew from my heart all love And added to the gall. O Lear, Lear, Lear! Beat at this gate that let thy folly in  [Strikes his head.] And thy dear judgment out! Go, go, my people.
lines: [to Goneril] Detested kite, thou liest!
My train are men of choice and rarest parts,
That all particulars of duty know
And in the most exact regard support
The worships of their name.- O most small fault,
How ugly didst thou in Cordelia show!
Which, like an engine, wrench'd my frame of nature
From the fix'd place; drew from my heart all love
And added to the gall. O Lear, Lear, Lear!
Beat at this gate that let thy folly in  [Strikes his head.]
And thy dear judgment out! Go, go, my people.

speeches.speech: My lord, I am guiltless, as I am ignorant Of what hath mov'd you.
lines: My lord, I am guiltless, as I am ignorant
Of what hath mov'd you.

speeches.speech: It may be so, my lord. Hear, Nature, hear! dear goddess, hear! Suspend thy purpose, if thou didst intend To make this creature fruitful. Into her womb convey sterility; Dry up in her the organs of increase; And from her derogate body never spring A babe to honour her! If she must teem, Create her child of spleen, that it may live And be a thwart disnatur'd torment to her. Let it stamp wrinkles in her brow of youth, With cadent tears fret channels in her cheeks, Turn all her mother's pains and benefits To laughter and contempt, that she may feel How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is To have a thankless child! Away, away!                Exit.
lines: It may be so, my lord.
Hear, Nature, hear! dear goddess, hear!
Suspend thy purpose, if thou didst intend
To make this creature fruitful.
Into her womb convey sterility;
Dry up in her the organs of increase;
And from her derogate body never spring
A babe to honour her! If she must teem,
Create her child of spleen, that it may live
And be a thwart disnatur'd torment to her.
Let it stamp wrinkles in her brow of youth,
With cadent tears fret channels in her cheeks,
Turn all her mother's pains and benefits
To laughter and contempt, that she may feel
How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is
To have a thankless child! Away, away!                Exit.

speeches.speech: Now, gods that we adore, whereof comes this?
lines: Now, gods that we adore, whereof comes this?

speeches.speech: Never afflict yourself to know the cause; But let his disposition have that scope That dotage gives it.
lines: Never afflict yourself to know the cause;
But let his disposition have that scope
That dotage gives it.

speeches.speech: What, fifty of my followers at a clap? Within a fortnight?
lines: What, fifty of my followers at a clap?
Within a fortnight?

speeches.speech: What's the matter, sir?
lines: What's the matter, sir?

speeches.speech: I'll tell thee. [To Goneril] Life and death! I am asham'd That thou hast power to shake my manhood thus; That these hot tears, which break from me perforce, Should make thee worth them. Blasts and fogs upon thee! Th' untented woundings of a father's curse Pierce every sense about thee!- Old fond eyes, Beweep this cause again, I'll pluck ye out, And cast you, with the waters that you lose, To temper clay. Yea, is it come to this? Let it be so. Yet have I left a daughter, Who I am sure is kind and comfortable. When she shall hear this of thee, with her nails She'll flay thy wolvish visage. Thou shalt find That I'll resume the shape which thou dost think I have cast off for ever; thou shalt, I warrant thee.
lines: I'll tell thee. [To Goneril] Life and death! I am asham'd
That thou hast power to shake my manhood thus;
That these hot tears, which break from me perforce,
Should make thee worth them. Blasts and fogs upon thee!
Th' untented woundings of a father's curse
Pierce every sense about thee!- Old fond eyes,
Beweep this cause again, I'll pluck ye out,
And cast you, with the waters that you lose,
To temper clay. Yea, is it come to this?
Let it be so. Yet have I left a daughter,
Who I am sure is kind and comfortable.
When she shall hear this of thee, with her nails
She'll flay thy wolvish visage. Thou shalt find
That I'll resume the shape which thou dost think
I have cast off for ever; thou shalt, I warrant thee.

speeches.speech: Do you mark that, my lord?
lines: Do you mark that, my lord?

speeches.speech: I cannot be so partial, Goneril, To the great love I bear you--
lines: I cannot be so partial, Goneril,
To the great love I bear you--

speeches.speech: Pray you, content.- What, Oswald, ho! [To the Fool] You, sir, more knave than fool, after your master!
lines: Pray you, content.- What, Oswald, ho!
[To the Fool] You, sir, more knave than fool, after your master!

speeches.speech: Nuncle Lear, nuncle Lear, tarry! Take the fool with thee. A fox when one has caught her, And such a daughter, Should sure to the slaughter, If my cap would buy a halter. So the fool follows after.    Exit.
lines: Nuncle Lear, nuncle Lear, tarry! Take the fool with thee.
A fox when one has caught her,
And such a daughter,
Should sure to the slaughter,
If my cap would buy a halter.
So the fool follows after.    Exit.

speeches.speech: This man hath had good counsel! A hundred knights? 'Tis politic and safe to let him keep At point a hundred knights; yes, that on every dream, Each buzz, each fancy, each complaint, dislike, He may enguard his dotage with their pow'rs And hold our lives in mercy.- Oswald, I say!
lines: This man hath had good counsel! A hundred knights?
'Tis politic and safe to let him keep
At point a hundred knights; yes, that on every dream,
Each buzz, each fancy, each complaint, dislike,
He may enguard his dotage with their pow'rs
And hold our lives in mercy.- Oswald, I say!

speeches.speech: Well, you may fear too far.
lines: Well, you may fear too far.

speeches.speech: Safer than trust too far. Let me still take away the harms I fear, Not fear still to be taken. I know his heart. What he hath utter'd I have writ my sister. If she sustain him and his hundred knights, When I have show'd th' unfitness- [Enter [Oswald the] Steward.] How now, Oswald? What, have you writ that letter to my sister?
lines: Safer than trust too far.
Let me still take away the harms I fear,
Not fear still to be taken. I know his heart.
What he hath utter'd I have writ my sister.
If she sustain him and his hundred knights,
When I have show'd th' unfitness- [Enter [Oswald the] Steward.]
How now, Oswald?
What, have you writ that letter to my sister?

speeches.speech: Yes, madam.
lines: Yes, madam.

speeches.speech: Take you some company, and away to horse! Inform her full of my particular fear, And thereto add such reasons of your own As may compact it more. Get you gone, And hasten your return. [Exit Oswald.] No, no, my lord! This milky gentleness and course of yours, Though I condemn it not, yet, under pardon, You are much more at task for want of wisdom Than prais'd for harmful mildness.
lines: Take you some company, and away to horse!
Inform her full of my particular fear,
And thereto add such reasons of your own
As may compact it more. Get you gone,
And hasten your return. [Exit Oswald.] No, no, my lord!
This milky gentleness and course of yours,
Though I condemn it not, yet, under pardon,
You are much more at task for want of wisdom
Than prais'd for harmful mildness.

speeches.speech: How far your eyes may pierce I cannot tell. Striving to better, oft we mar what's well.
lines: How far your eyes may pierce I cannot tell.
Striving to better, oft we mar what's well.

speeches.speech: Nay then-
lines: Nay then-

speeches.speech: Well, well; th' event.         Exeunt.
lines: Well, well; th' event.         Exeunt.

speeches.speech: Go you before to Gloucester with these letters. Acquaint my daughter no further with anything you know than comes from her demand out of the letter. If your diligence be not speedy, I shall be there afore you.
lines: Go you before to Gloucester with these letters. Acquaint my
daughter no further with anything you know than comes from her
demand out of the letter. If your diligence be not speedy, I
shall be there afore you.

speeches.speech: I will not sleep, my lord, till I have delivered your letter. Exit.
lines: I will not sleep, my lord, till I have delivered your letter. Exit.

speeches.speech: If a man's brains were in's heels, were't not in danger of kibes?
lines: If a man's brains were in's heels, were't not in danger of
kibes?

speeches.speech: Ay, boy.
lines: Ay, boy.

speeches.speech: Then I prithee be merry. Thy wit shall ne'er go slip-shod.
lines: Then I prithee be merry. Thy wit shall ne'er go slip-shod.

speeches.speech: Ha, ha, ha!
