speeches.speech: Prithee go in thyself; seek thine own ease. This tempest will not give me leave to ponder On things would hurt me more. But I'll go in. [To the Fool] In, boy; go first.- You houseless poverty- Nay, get thee in. I'll pray, and then I'll sleep. [Exit Fool] Poor naked wretches, wheresoe'er you are, That bide the pelting of this pitiless storm, How shall your houseless heads and unfed sides, Your loop'd and window'd raggedness, defend you From seasons such as these? O, I have ta'en Too little care of this! Take physic, pomp; Expose thyself to feel what wretches feel, That thou mayst shake the superflux to them And show the heavens more just.
lines: Prithee go in thyself; seek thine own ease.
This tempest will not give me leave to ponder
On things would hurt me more. But I'll go in.
[To the Fool] In, boy; go first.- You houseless poverty-
Nay, get thee in. I'll pray, and then I'll sleep. [Exit Fool]
Poor naked wretches, wheresoe'er you are,
That bide the pelting of this pitiless storm,
How shall your houseless heads and unfed sides,
Your loop'd and window'd raggedness, defend you
From seasons such as these? O, I have ta'en
Too little care of this! Take physic, pomp;
Expose thyself to feel what wretches feel,
That thou mayst shake the superflux to them
And show the heavens more just.

speeches.speech: [within] Fathom and half, fathom and half! Poor Tom!
lines: [within] Fathom and half, fathom and half! Poor Tom!

speeches.speech: Come not in here, nuncle, here's a spirit. Help me, help me!
lines: Come not in here, nuncle, here's a spirit. Help me, help me!

speeches.speech: Give me thy hand. Who's there?
lines: Give me thy hand. Who's there?

speeches.speech: A spirit, a spirit! He says his name's poor Tom.
lines: A spirit, a spirit! He says his name's poor Tom.

speeches.speech: What art thou that dost grumble there i' th' straw? Come forth.
lines: What art thou that dost grumble there i' th' straw?
Come forth.

speeches.speech: Away! the foul fiend follows me! Through the sharp hawthorn blows the cold wind. Humh! go to thy cold bed, and warm thee.
lines: Away! the foul fiend follows me! Through the sharp hawthorn
blows the cold wind. Humh! go to thy cold bed, and warm thee.

speeches.speech: Hast thou given all to thy two daughters, and art thou come to this?
lines: Hast thou given all to thy two daughters, and art thou come
to this?

speeches.speech: Who gives anything to poor Tom? whom the foul fiend hath led through fire and through flame, through ford and whirlpool, o'er bog and quagmire; that hath laid knives under his pillow and halters in his pew, set ratsbane by his porridge, made him proud of heart, to ride on a bay trotting horse over four-inch'd bridges, to course his own shadow for a traitor. Bless thy five wits! Tom 's acold. O, do de, do de, do de. Bless thee from whirlwinds, star-blasting, and taking! Do poor Tom some charity, whom the foul fiend vexes. There could I have him now- and there- and there again- and there!
lines: Who gives anything to poor Tom? whom the foul fiend hath led
through fire and through flame, through ford and whirlpool, o'er
bog and quagmire; that hath laid knives under his pillow and
halters in his pew, set ratsbane by his porridge, made him proud
of heart, to ride on a bay trotting horse over four-inch'd
bridges, to course his own shadow for a traitor. Bless thy five
wits! Tom 's acold. O, do de, do de, do de. Bless thee from
whirlwinds, star-blasting, and taking! Do poor Tom some charity,
whom the foul fiend vexes. There could I have him now- and there-
and there again- and there!

speeches.speech: What, have his daughters brought him to this pass? Couldst thou save nothing? Didst thou give 'em all?
lines: What, have his daughters brought him to this pass?
Couldst thou save nothing? Didst thou give 'em all?

speeches.speech: Nay, he reserv'd a blanket, else we had been all sham'd.
lines: Nay, he reserv'd a blanket, else we had been all sham'd.

speeches.speech: Now all the plagues that in the pendulous air Hang fated o'er men's faults light on thy daughters!
lines: Now all the plagues that in the pendulous air
Hang fated o'er men's faults light on thy daughters!

speeches.speech: He hath no daughters, sir.
lines: He hath no daughters, sir.

speeches.speech: Death, traitor! nothing could have subdu'd nature To such a lowness but his unkind daughters. Is it the fashion that discarded fathers Should have thus little mercy on their flesh? Judicious punishment! 'Twas this flesh begot Those pelican daughters.
lines: Death, traitor! nothing could have subdu'd nature
To such a lowness but his unkind daughters.
Is it the fashion that discarded fathers
Should have thus little mercy on their flesh?
Judicious punishment! 'Twas this flesh begot
Those pelican daughters.

speeches.speech: Pillicock sat on Pillicock's Hill. 'Allow, 'allow, loo, loo!
lines: Pillicock sat on Pillicock's Hill. 'Allow, 'allow, loo, loo!

speeches.speech: This cold night will turn us all to fools and madmen.
lines: This cold night will turn us all to fools and madmen.

speeches.speech: Take heed o' th' foul fiend; obey thy parents: keep thy word justly; swear not; commit not with man's sworn spouse; set not thy sweet heart on proud array. Tom 's acold.
lines: Take heed o' th' foul fiend; obey thy parents: keep thy word
justly; swear not; commit not with man's sworn spouse; set not
thy sweet heart on proud array. Tom 's acold.

speeches.speech: What hast thou been?
lines: What hast thou been?

speeches.speech: A servingman, proud in heart and mind; that curl'd my hair, wore gloves in my cap; serv'd the lust of my mistress' heart and did the act of darkness with her; swore as many oaths as I spake words, and broke them in the sweet face of heaven; one that slept in the contriving of lust, and wak'd to do it. Wine lov'd I deeply, dice dearly; and in woman out-paramour'd the Turk. False of heart, light of ear, bloody of hand; hog in sloth, fox in stealth, wolf in greediness, dog in madness, lion in prey. Let not the creaking of shoes nor the rustling of silks betray thy poor heart to woman. Keep thy foot out of brothel, thy hand out of placket, thy pen from lender's book, and defy the foul fiend. Still through the hawthorn blows the cold wind; says suum, mun, hey, no, nonny. Dolphin my boy, my boy, sessa! let him trot by.
lines: A servingman, proud in heart and mind; that curl'd my hair,
wore gloves in my cap; serv'd the lust of my mistress' heart and
did the act of darkness with her; swore as many oaths as I spake
words, and broke them in the sweet face of heaven; one that
slept in the contriving of lust, and wak'd to do it. Wine lov'd
I deeply, dice dearly; and in woman out-paramour'd the Turk.
False of heart, light of ear, bloody of hand; hog in sloth, fox
in stealth, wolf in greediness, dog in madness, lion in prey.
Let not the creaking of shoes nor the rustling of silks betray
thy poor heart to woman. Keep thy foot out of brothel, thy hand
out of placket, thy pen from lender's book, and defy the foul
fiend. Still through the hawthorn blows the cold wind; says
suum, mun, hey, no, nonny. Dolphin my boy, my boy, sessa! let
him trot by.

speeches.speech: Why, thou wert better in thy grave than to answer with thy uncover'd body this extremity of the skies. Is man no more than this? Consider him well. Thou ow'st the worm no silk, the beast no hide, the sheep no wool, the cat no perfume. Ha! Here's three on's are sophisticated! Thou art the thing itself; unaccommodated man is no more but such a poor, bare, forked animal as thou art. Off, off, you lendings! Come, unbutton here.
lines: Why, thou wert better in thy grave than to answer with thy
uncover'd body this extremity of the skies. Is man no more than
this? Consider him well. Thou ow'st the worm no silk, the beast
no hide, the sheep no wool, the cat no perfume. Ha! Here's three
on's are sophisticated! Thou art the thing itself;
unaccommodated man is no more but such a poor, bare, forked
animal as thou art. Off, off, you lendings! Come, unbutton
here.

speeches.speech: Prithee, nuncle, be contented! 'Tis a naughty night to swim in. Now a little fire in a wild field were like an old lecher's heart- a small spark, all the rest on's body cold. Look, here comes a walking fire.
lines: Prithee, nuncle, be contented! 'Tis a naughty night to swim
in. Now a little fire in a wild field were like an old lecher's
heart- a small spark, all the rest on's body cold. Look, here
comes a walking fire.

speeches.speech: This is the foul fiend Flibbertigibbet. He begins at curfew, and walks till the first cock. He gives the web and the pin, squints the eye, and makes the harelip; mildews the white wheat, and hurts the poor creature of earth. Saint Withold footed thrice the 'old; He met the nightmare, and her nine fold; Bid her alight And her troth plight, And aroint thee, witch, aroint thee!
lines: This is the foul fiend Flibbertigibbet. He begins at curfew,
and walks till the first cock. He gives the web and the pin,
squints the eye, and makes the harelip; mildews the white wheat,
and hurts the poor creature of earth.
Saint Withold footed thrice the 'old;
He met the nightmare, and her nine fold;
Bid her alight
And her troth plight,
And aroint thee, witch, aroint thee!

speeches.speech: How fares your Grace?
lines: How fares your Grace?

speeches.speech: What's he?
lines: What's he?

speeches.speech: Who's there? What is't you seek?
lines: Who's there? What is't you seek?

speeches.speech: What are you there? Your names?
lines: What are you there? Your names?

speeches.speech: Poor Tom, that eats the swimming frog, the toad, the todpole, the wall-newt and the water; that in the fury of his heart, when the foul fiend rages, eats cow-dung for sallets, swallows the old rat and the ditch-dog, drinks the green mantle of the standing pool; who is whipp'd from tithing to tithing, and stock-punish'd and imprison'd; who hath had three suits to his back, six shirts to his body, horse to ride, and weapons to wear; But mice and rats, and such small deer, Have been Tom's food for seven long year. Beware my follower. Peace, Smulkin! peace, thou fiend!
lines: Poor Tom, that eats the swimming frog, the toad, the todpole,
the wall-newt and the water; that in the fury of his heart, when
the foul fiend rages, eats cow-dung for sallets, swallows the
old rat and the ditch-dog, drinks the green mantle of the
standing pool; who is whipp'd from tithing to tithing, and
stock-punish'd and imprison'd; who hath had three suits to his
back, six shirts to his body, horse to ride, and weapons to
wear;
But mice and rats, and such small deer,
Have been Tom's food for seven long year.
Beware my follower. Peace, Smulkin! peace, thou fiend!

speeches.speech: What, hath your Grace no better company?
lines: What, hath your Grace no better company?

speeches.speech: The prince of darkness is a gentleman! Modo he's call'd, and Mahu.
lines: The prince of darkness is a gentleman!
Modo he's call'd, and Mahu.

speeches.speech: Our flesh and blood is grown so vile, my lord, That it doth hate what gets it.
lines: Our flesh and blood is grown so vile, my lord,
That it doth hate what gets it.

speeches.speech: Poor Tom 's acold.
lines: Poor Tom 's acold.

speeches.speech: Go in with me. My duty cannot suffer T' obey in all your daughters' hard commands. Though their injunction be to bar my doors And let this tyrannous night take hold upon you, Yet have I ventur'd to come seek you out And bring you where both fire and food is ready.
lines: Go in with me. My duty cannot suffer
T' obey in all your daughters' hard commands.
Though their injunction be to bar my doors
And let this tyrannous night take hold upon you,
Yet have I ventur'd to come seek you out
And bring you where both fire and food is ready.

speeches.speech: First let me talk with this philosopher. What is the cause of thunder?
lines: First let me talk with this philosopher.
What is the cause of thunder?

speeches.speech: Good my lord, take his offer; go into th' house.
lines: Good my lord, take his offer; go into th' house.

speeches.speech: I'll talk a word with this same learned Theban. What is your study?
lines: I'll talk a word with this same learned Theban.
What is your study?

speeches.speech: How to prevent the fiend and to kill vermin.
lines: How to prevent the fiend and to kill vermin.

speeches.speech: Let me ask you one word in private.
lines: Let me ask you one word in private.

speeches.speech: Importune him once more to go, my lord. His wits begin t' unsettle.
lines: Importune him once more to go, my lord.
His wits begin t' unsettle.

speeches.speech: Canst thou blame him? [Storm still.] His daughters seek his death. Ah, that good Kent! He said it would be thus- poor banish'd man! Thou say'st the King grows mad: I'll tell thee, friend, I am almost mad myself. I had a son, Now outlaw'd from my blood. He sought my life But lately, very late. I lov'd him, friend- No father his son dearer. True to tell thee, The grief hath craz'd my wits. What a night 's this! I do beseech your Grace-
lines: Canst thou blame him? [Storm still.]
His daughters seek his death. Ah, that good Kent!
He said it would be thus- poor banish'd man!
Thou say'st the King grows mad: I'll tell thee, friend,
I am almost mad myself. I had a son,
Now outlaw'd from my blood. He sought my life
But lately, very late. I lov'd him, friend-
No father his son dearer. True to tell thee,
The grief hath craz'd my wits. What a night 's this!
I do beseech your Grace-

speeches.speech: O, cry you mercy, sir. Noble philosopher, your company.
lines: O, cry you mercy, sir.
Noble philosopher, your company.

speeches.speech: Tom's acold.
lines: Tom's acold.

speeches.speech: In, fellow, there, into th' hovel; keep thee warm.
lines: In, fellow, there, into th' hovel; keep thee warm.

speeches.speech: Come, let's in all.
lines: Come, let's in all.

speeches.speech: This way, my lord.
lines: This way, my lord.

speeches.speech: With him! I will keep still with my philosopher.
lines: With him!
I will keep still with my philosopher.

speeches.speech: Good my lord, soothe him; let him take the fellow.
lines: Good my lord, soothe him; let him take the fellow.

speeches.speech: Take him you on.
lines: Take him you on.

speeches.speech: Sirrah, come on; go along with us.
lines: Sirrah, come on; go along with us.

speeches.speech: Come, good Athenian.
lines: Come, good Athenian.

speeches.speech: No words, no words! hush.
lines: No words, no words! hush.

speeches.speech: Child Rowland to the dark tower came; His word was still Fie, foh, and fum! I smell the blood of a British man.
lines: Child Rowland to the dark tower came;
His word was still
Fie, foh, and fum!
I smell the blood of a British man.

speeches.speech: I will have my revenge ere I depart his house.
lines: I will have my revenge ere I depart his house.

speeches.speech: How, my lord, I may be censured, that nature thus gives way to loyalty, something fears me to think of.
lines: How, my lord, I may be censured, that nature thus gives way to
loyalty, something fears me to think of.

speeches.speech: I now perceive it was not altogether your brother's evil disposition made him seek his death; but a provoking merit, set awork by a reproveable badness in himself.
lines: I now perceive it was not altogether your brother's evil
disposition made him seek his death; but a provoking merit, set
awork by a reproveable badness in himself.

speeches.speech: How malicious is my fortune that I must repent to be just! This is the letter he spoke of, which approves him an intelligent party to the advantages of France. O heavens! that this treason were not- or not I the detector!
lines: How malicious is my fortune that I must repent to be just!
This is the letter he spoke of, which approves him an
intelligent party to the advantages of France. O heavens! that
this treason were not- or not I the detector!

speeches.speech: Go with me to the Duchess.
lines: Go with me to the Duchess.

speeches.speech: If the matter of this paper be certain, you have mighty business in hand.
lines: If the matter of this paper be certain, you have mighty
business in hand.

speeches.speech: True or false, it hath made thee Earl of Gloucester. Seek out where thy father is, that he may be ready for our apprehension.
lines: True or false, it hath made thee Earl of Gloucester.
Seek out where thy father is, that he may be ready for our
apprehension.

speeches.speech: [aside] If I find him comforting the King, it will stuff his suspicion more fully.- I will persever in my course of loyalty, though the conflict be sore between that and my blood.
lines: [aside] If I find him comforting the King, it will stuff his
suspicion more fully.- I will persever in my course of loyalty,
though the conflict be sore between that and my blood.

speeches.speech: I will lay trust upon thee, and thou shalt find a dearer father in my love.
lines: I will lay trust upon thee, and thou shalt find a dearer
father in my love.

speeches.speech: Here is better than the open air; take it thankfully. I will piece out the comfort with what addition I can. I will not be long from you.
lines: Here is better than the open air; take it thankfully. I will
piece out the comfort with what addition I can. I will not be
long from you.

speeches.speech: All the power of his wits have given way to his impatience. The gods reward your kindness!
lines: All the power of his wits have given way to his impatience.
The gods reward your kindness!

speeches.speech: Frateretto calls me, and tells me Nero is an angler in the lake of darkness. Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend.
lines: Frateretto calls me, and tells me Nero is an angler in the
lake of darkness. Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend.

speeches.speech: Prithee, nuncle, tell me whether a madman be a gentleman or a yeoman.
lines: Prithee, nuncle, tell me whether a madman be a gentleman or a
yeoman.

speeches.speech: A king, a king!
lines: A king, a king!

speeches.speech: No, he's a yeoman that has a gentleman to his son; for he's a mad yeoman that sees his son a gentleman before him.
lines: No, he's a yeoman that has a gentleman to his son; for he's a
mad yeoman that sees his son a gentleman before him.

speeches.speech: To have a thousand with red burning spits Come hizzing in upon 'em-
lines: To have a thousand with red burning spits
Come hizzing in upon 'em-

speeches.speech: The foul fiend bites my back.
lines: The foul fiend bites my back.

speeches.speech: He's mad that trusts in the tameness of a wolf, a horse's health, a boy's love, or a whore's oath.
lines: He's mad that trusts in the tameness of a wolf, a horse's
health, a boy's love, or a whore's oath.

speeches.speech: It shall be done; I will arraign them straight. [To Edgar] Come, sit thou here, most learned justicer. [To the Fool] Thou, sapient sir, sit here. Now, you she-foxes!
lines: It shall be done; I will arraign them straight.
[To Edgar] Come, sit thou here, most learned justicer.
[To the Fool] Thou, sapient sir, sit here. Now, you she-foxes!

speeches.speech: Look, where he stands and glares! Want'st thou eyes at trial, madam? Come o'er the bourn, Bessy, to me.
lines: Look, where he stands and glares! Want'st thou eyes at trial,
madam?
Come o'er the bourn, Bessy, to me.

speeches.speech: Her boat hath a leak, And she must not speak Why she dares not come over to thee.
lines: Her boat hath a leak,
And she must not speak
Why she dares not come over to thee.

speeches.speech: The foul fiend haunts poor Tom in the voice of a nightingale. Hoppedance cries in Tom's belly for two white herring. Croak not, black angel; I have no food for thee.
lines: The foul fiend haunts poor Tom in the voice of a nightingale.
Hoppedance cries in Tom's belly for two white herring. Croak
not, black angel; I have no food for thee.

speeches.speech: How do you, sir? Stand you not so amaz'd. Will you lie down and rest upon the cushions?
lines: How do you, sir? Stand you not so amaz'd.
Will you lie down and rest upon the cushions?

speeches.speech: I'll see their trial first. Bring in their evidence. [To Edgar] Thou, robed man of justice, take thy place. [To the Fool] And thou, his yokefellow of equity, Bench by his side. [To Kent] You are o' th' commission, Sit you too.
lines: I'll see their trial first. Bring in their evidence.
[To Edgar] Thou, robed man of justice, take thy place.
[To the Fool] And thou, his yokefellow of equity,
Bench by his side. [To Kent] You are o' th' commission,
Sit you too.

speeches.speech: Let us deal justly. Sleepest or wakest thou, jolly shepherd? Thy sheep be in the corn; And for one blast of thy minikin mouth Thy sheep shall take no harm. Purr! the cat is gray.
lines: Let us deal justly.
Sleepest or wakest thou, jolly shepherd?
Thy sheep be in the corn;
And for one blast of thy minikin mouth
Thy sheep shall take no harm.
Purr! the cat is gray.

speeches.speech: Arraign her first. 'Tis Goneril. I here take my oath before this honourable assembly, she kicked the poor King her father.
lines: Arraign her first. 'Tis Goneril. I here take my oath before
this honourable assembly, she kicked the poor King her father.

speeches.speech: Come hither, mistress. Is your name Goneril?
lines: Come hither, mistress. Is your name Goneril?

speeches.speech: She cannot deny it.
lines: She cannot deny it.

speeches.speech: Cry you mercy, I took you for a joint-stool.
lines: Cry you mercy, I took you for a joint-stool.

speeches.speech: And here's another, whose warp'd looks proclaim What store her heart is made on. Stop her there! Arms, arms! sword! fire! Corruption in the place! False justicer, why hast thou let her scape?
lines: And here's another, whose warp'd looks proclaim
What store her heart is made on. Stop her there!
Arms, arms! sword! fire! Corruption in the place!
False justicer, why hast thou let her scape?

speeches.speech: Bless thy five wits!
lines: Bless thy five wits!

speeches.speech: O pity! Sir, where is the patience now That you so oft have boasted to retain?
lines: O pity! Sir, where is the patience now
That you so oft have boasted to retain?

speeches.speech: [aside] My tears begin to take his part so much They'll mar my counterfeiting.
lines: [aside] My tears begin to take his part so much
They'll mar my counterfeiting.

speeches.speech: The little dogs and all, Tray, Blanch, and Sweetheart, see, they bark at me.
lines: The little dogs and all,
Tray, Blanch, and Sweetheart, see, they bark at me.

speeches.speech: Tom will throw his head at them. Avaunt, you curs! Be thy mouth or black or white, Tooth that poisons if it bite; Mastiff, greyhound, mongrel grim, Hound or spaniel, brach or lym, Bobtail tyke or trundle-tail- Tom will make them weep and wail; For, with throwing thus my head, Dogs leap the hatch, and all are fled. Do de, de, de. Sessa! Come, march to wakes and fairs and market towns. Poor Tom, thy horn is dry.
lines: Tom will throw his head at them. Avaunt, you curs!
Be thy mouth or black or white,
Tooth that poisons if it bite;
Mastiff, greyhound, mongrel grim,
Hound or spaniel, brach or lym,
Bobtail tyke or trundle-tail-
Tom will make them weep and wail;
For, with throwing thus my head,
Dogs leap the hatch, and all are fled.
Do de, de, de. Sessa! Come, march to wakes and fairs and market
towns. Poor Tom, thy horn is dry.

speeches.speech: Then let them anatomize Regan. See what breeds about her heart. Is there any cause in nature that makes these hard hearts? [To Edgar] You, sir- I entertain you for one of my hundred; only I do not like the fashion of your garments. You'll say they are Persian attire; but let them be chang'd.
lines: Then let them anatomize Regan. See what breeds about her
heart. Is there any cause in nature that makes these hard
hearts? [To Edgar] You, sir- I entertain you for one of my
hundred; only I do not like the fashion of your garments. You'll
say they are Persian attire; but let them be chang'd.

speeches.speech: Now, good my lord, lie here and rest awhile.
lines: Now, good my lord, lie here and rest awhile.

speeches.speech: Make no noise, make no noise; draw the curtains. So, so, so. We'll go to supper i' th' morning. So, so, so.
lines: Make no noise, make no noise; draw the curtains.
So, so, so. We'll go to supper i' th' morning. So, so, so.

speeches.speech: And I'll go to bed at noon.
lines: And I'll go to bed at noon.

speeches.speech: Come hither, friend. Where is the King my master?
lines: Come hither, friend. Where is the King my master?

speeches.speech: Here, sir; but trouble him not; his wits are gone.
lines: Here, sir; but trouble him not; his wits are gone.

speeches.speech: Good friend, I prithee take him in thy arms. I have o'erheard a plot of death upon him. There is a litter ready; lay him in't And drive towards Dover, friend, where thou shalt meet Both welcome and protection. Take up thy master. If thou shouldst dally half an hour, his life, With thine, and all that offer to defend him, Stand in assured loss. Take up, take up! And follow me, that will to some provision Give thee quick conduct.
lines: Good friend, I prithee take him in thy arms.
I have o'erheard a plot of death upon him.
There is a litter ready; lay him in't
And drive towards Dover, friend, where thou shalt meet
Both welcome and protection. Take up thy master.
If thou shouldst dally half an hour, his life,
With thine, and all that offer to defend him,
Stand in assured loss. Take up, take up!
And follow me, that will to some provision
Give thee quick conduct.

speeches.speech: Oppressed nature sleeps. This rest might yet have balm'd thy broken senses, Which, if convenience will not allow, Stand in hard cure. [To the Fool] Come, help to bear thy master. Thou must not stay behind.
lines: Oppressed nature sleeps.
This rest might yet have balm'd thy broken senses,
Which, if convenience will not allow,
Stand in hard cure. [To the Fool] Come, help to bear thy master.
Thou must not stay behind.

speeches.speech: Come, come, away!
lines: Come, come, away!

speeches.speech: When we our betters see bearing our woes, We scarcely think our miseries our foes. Who alone suffers suffers most i' th' mind, Leaving free things and happy shows behind; But then the mind much sufferance doth o'erskip When grief hath mates, and bearing fellowship. How light and portable my pain seems now, When that which makes me bend makes the King bow, He childed as I fathered! Tom, away! Mark the high noises, and thyself bewray When false opinion, whose wrong thought defiles thee, In thy just proof repeals and reconciles thee. What will hap more to-night, safe scape the King! Lurk, lurk.                      [Exit.]
lines: When we our betters see bearing our woes,
We scarcely think our miseries our foes.
Who alone suffers suffers most i' th' mind,
Leaving free things and happy shows behind;
But then the mind much sufferance doth o'erskip
When grief hath mates, and bearing fellowship.
How light and portable my pain seems now,
When that which makes me bend makes the King bow,
He childed as I fathered! Tom, away!
Mark the high noises, and thyself bewray
When false opinion, whose wrong thought defiles thee,
In thy just proof repeals and reconciles thee.
What will hap more to-night, safe scape the King!
Lurk, lurk.                      [Exit.]

speeches.speech: [to Goneril] Post speedily to my lord your husband, show him this letter. The army of France is landed.- Seek out the traitor Gloucester.
lines: [to Goneril] Post speedily to my lord your husband, show him
this letter. The army of France is landed.- Seek out the traitor
Gloucester.

speeches.speech: Hang him instantly.
lines: Hang him instantly.

speeches.speech: Pluck out his eyes.
lines: Pluck out his eyes.

speeches.speech: Leave him to my displeasure. Edmund, keep you our sister company. The revenges we are bound to take upon your traitorous father are not fit for your beholding. Advise the Duke where you are going, to a most festinate preparation. We are bound to the like. Our posts shall be swift and intelligent betwixt us. Farewell, dear sister; farewell, my Lord of Gloucester. [Enter Oswald the Steward.] How now? Where's the King?
lines: Leave him to my displeasure. Edmund, keep you our sister
company. The revenges we are bound to take upon your traitorous
father are not fit for your beholding. Advise the Duke where you
are going, to a most festinate preparation. We are bound to the
like. Our posts shall be swift and intelligent betwixt us.
Farewell, dear sister; farewell, my Lord of Gloucester. [Enter Oswald the Steward.]
How now? Where's the King?

speeches.speech: My Lord of Gloucester hath convey'd him hence. Some five or six and thirty of his knights, Hot questrists after him, met him at gate; Who, with some other of the lord's dependants, Are gone with him towards Dover, where they boast To have well-armed friends.
lines: My Lord of Gloucester hath convey'd him hence.
Some five or six and thirty of his knights,
Hot questrists after him, met him at gate;
Who, with some other of the lord's dependants,
Are gone with him towards Dover, where they boast
To have well-armed friends.

speeches.speech: Get horses for your mistress.
lines: Get horses for your mistress.

speeches.speech: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.
lines: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.

speeches.speech: Edmund, farewell. [Exeunt Goneril, Edmund, and Oswald.] Go seek the traitor Gloucester, Pinion him like a thief, bring him before us. [Exeunt other Servants.] Though well we may not pass upon his life Without the form of justice, yet our power Shall do a court'sy to our wrath, which men May blame, but not control. [Enter Gloucester, brought in by two or three.] Who's there? the traitor?
lines: Edmund, farewell. [Exeunt Goneril, Edmund, and Oswald.]
Go seek the traitor Gloucester,
Pinion him like a thief, bring him before us. [Exeunt other Servants.]
Though well we may not pass upon his life
Without the form of justice, yet our power
Shall do a court'sy to our wrath, which men
May blame, but not control. [Enter Gloucester, brought in by two or three.]
Who's there? the traitor?

speeches.speech: Ingrateful fox! 'tis he.
lines: Ingrateful fox! 'tis he.

speeches.speech: Bind fast his corky arms.
lines: Bind fast his corky arms.

speeches.speech: What mean, your Graces? Good my friends, consider You are my guests. Do me no foul play, friends.
lines: What mean, your Graces? Good my friends, consider
You are my guests. Do me no foul play, friends.

speeches.speech: Bind him, I say.
lines: Bind him, I say.

speeches.speech: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!
lines: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!

speeches.speech: Unmerciful lady as you are, I am none.
lines: Unmerciful lady as you are, I am none.

speeches.speech: To this chair bind him. Villain, thou shalt find-
lines: To this chair bind him. Villain, thou shalt find-

speeches.speech: By the kind gods, 'tis most ignobly done To pluck me by the beard.
lines: By the kind gods, 'tis most ignobly done
To pluck me by the beard.

speeches.speech: So white, and such a traitor!
lines: So white, and such a traitor!

speeches.speech: Naughty lady, These hairs which thou dost ravish from my chin Will quicken, and accuse thee. I am your host. With robber's hands my hospitable favours You should not ruffle thus. What will you do?
lines: Naughty lady,
These hairs which thou dost ravish from my chin
Will quicken, and accuse thee. I am your host.
With robber's hands my hospitable favours
You should not ruffle thus. What will you do?

speeches.speech: Come, sir, what letters had you late from France?
lines: Come, sir, what letters had you late from France?

speeches.speech: Be simple-answer'd, for we know the truth.
lines: Be simple-answer'd, for we know the truth.

speeches.speech: And what confederacy have you with the traitors Late footed in the kingdom?
lines: And what confederacy have you with the traitors
Late footed in the kingdom?

speeches.speech: To whose hands have you sent the lunatic King? Speak.
lines: To whose hands have you sent the lunatic King?
Speak.

speeches.speech: I have a letter guessingly set down, Which came from one that's of a neutral heart, And not from one oppos'd.
lines: I have a letter guessingly set down,
Which came from one that's of a neutral heart,
And not from one oppos'd.

speeches.speech: Cunning.
lines: Cunning.

speeches.speech: And false.
lines: And false.

speeches.speech: Where hast thou sent the King?
lines: Where hast thou sent the King?

speeches.speech: To Dover.
lines: To Dover.

speeches.speech: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charg'd at peril-
lines: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charg'd at peril-

speeches.speech: Wherefore to Dover? Let him first answer that.
lines: Wherefore to Dover? Let him first answer that.

speeches.speech: I am tied to th' stake, and I must stand the course.
lines: I am tied to th' stake, and I must stand the course.

speeches.speech: Wherefore to Dover, sir?
lines: Wherefore to Dover, sir?

speeches.speech: Because I would not see thy cruel nails Pluck out his poor old eyes; nor thy fierce sister In his anointed flesh stick boarish fangs. The sea, with such a storm as his bare head In hell-black night endur'd, would have buoy'd up And quench'd the steeled fires. Yet, poor old heart, he holp the heavens to rain. If wolves had at thy gate howl'd that stern time, Thou shouldst have said, 'Good porter, turn the key.' All cruels else subscrib'd. But I shall see The winged vengeance overtake such children.
lines: Because I would not see thy cruel nails
Pluck out his poor old eyes; nor thy fierce sister
In his anointed flesh stick boarish fangs.
The sea, with such a storm as his bare head
In hell-black night endur'd, would have buoy'd up
And quench'd the steeled fires.
Yet, poor old heart, he holp the heavens to rain.
If wolves had at thy gate howl'd that stern time,
Thou shouldst have said, 'Good porter, turn the key.'
All cruels else subscrib'd. But I shall see
The winged vengeance overtake such children.

speeches.speech: See't shalt thou never. Fellows, hold the chair. Upon these eyes of thine I'll set my foot.
lines: See't shalt thou never. Fellows, hold the chair.
Upon these eyes of thine I'll set my foot.

speeches.speech: He that will think to live till he be old, Give me some help!- O cruel! O ye gods!
lines: He that will think to live till he be old,
Give me some help!- O cruel! O ye gods!

speeches.speech: One side will mock another. Th' other too!
lines: One side will mock another. Th' other too!

speeches.speech: If you see vengeance-
lines: If you see vengeance-

speeches.speech: Hold your hand, my lord! I have serv'd you ever since I was a child; But better service have I never done you Than now to bid you hold.
lines: Hold your hand, my lord!
I have serv'd you ever since I was a child;
But better service have I never done you
Than now to bid you hold.

speeches.speech: How now, you dog?
lines: How now, you dog?

speeches.speech: If you did wear a beard upon your chin, I'ld shake it on this quarrel.
lines: If you did wear a beard upon your chin,
I'ld shake it on this quarrel.

speeches.speech: What do you mean?
lines: What do you mean?

speeches.speech: My villain!                               Draw and fight.
lines: My villain!                               Draw and fight.

speeches.speech: Nay, then, come on, and take the chance of anger.
lines: Nay, then, come on, and take the chance of anger.

speeches.speech: Give me thy sword. A peasant stand up thus? She takes a sword and runs at him behind.
lines: Give me thy sword. A peasant stand up thus?
She takes a sword and runs at him behind.

speeches.speech: O, I am slain! My lord, you have one eye left To see some mischief on him. O!                     He dies.
lines: O, I am slain! My lord, you have one eye left
To see some mischief on him. O!                     He dies.

speeches.speech: Lest it see more, prevent it. Out, vile jelly! Where is thy lustre now?
lines: Lest it see more, prevent it. Out, vile jelly!
Where is thy lustre now?

speeches.speech: All dark and comfortless! Where's my son Edmund? Edmund, enkindle all the sparks of nature To quit this horrid act.
lines: All dark and comfortless! Where's my son Edmund?
Edmund, enkindle all the sparks of nature
To quit this horrid act.

speeches.speech: Out, treacherous villain! Thou call'st on him that hates thee. It was he That made the overture of thy treasons to us; Who is too good to pity thee.
lines: Out, treacherous villain!
Thou call'st on him that hates thee. It was he
That made the overture of thy treasons to us;
Who is too good to pity thee.

speeches.speech: O my follies! Then Edgar was abus'd. Kind gods, forgive me that, and prosper him!
lines: O my follies! Then Edgar was abus'd.
Kind gods, forgive me that, and prosper him!

speeches.speech: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell His way to Dover. [Exit one with Gloucester.] How is't, my lord? How look you?
lines: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell
His way to Dover. [Exit one with Gloucester.]
How is't, my lord? How look you?

speeches.speech: I have receiv'd a hurt. Follow me, lady. Turn out that eyeless villain. Throw this slave Upon the dunghill. Regan, I bleed apace. Untimely comes this hurt. Give me your arm.
lines: I have receiv'd a hurt. Follow me, lady.
Turn out that eyeless villain. Throw this slave
Upon the dunghill. Regan, I bleed apace.
Untimely comes this hurt. Give me your arm.

speeches.speech: I'll never care what wickedness I do, If this man come to good.
lines: I'll never care what wickedness I do,
If this man come to good.

speeches.speech: If she live long, And in the end meet the old course of death, Women will all turn monsters.
lines: If she live long,
And in the end meet the old course of death,
Women will all turn monsters.

speeches.speech: Let's follow the old Earl, and get the bedlam To lead him where he would. His roguish madness Allows itself to anything.
lines: Let's follow the old Earl, and get the bedlam
To lead him where he would. His roguish madness
Allows itself to anything.

speeches.speech: Go thou. I'll fetch some flax and whites of eggs To apply to his bleeding face. Now heaven help him!
lines: Go thou. I'll fetch some flax and whites of eggs
To apply to his bleeding face. Now heaven help him!

speeches.speech: Yet better thus, and known to be contemn'd, Than still contemn'd and flatter'd. To be worst, The lowest and most dejected thing of fortune, Stands still in esperance, lives not in fear. The lamentable change is from the best; The worst returns to laughter. Welcome then, Thou unsubstantial air that I embrace! The wretch that thou hast blown unto the worst Owes nothing to thy blasts. [Enter Gloucester, led by an Old Man.] But who comes here? My father, poorly led? World, world, O world! But that thy strange mutations make us hate thee, Life would not yield to age.
lines: Yet better thus, and known to be contemn'd,
Than still contemn'd and flatter'd. To be worst,
The lowest and most dejected thing of fortune,
Stands still in esperance, lives not in fear.
The lamentable change is from the best;
The worst returns to laughter. Welcome then,
Thou unsubstantial air that I embrace!
The wretch that thou hast blown unto the worst
Owes nothing to thy blasts.
[Enter Gloucester, led by an Old Man.]
But who comes here?
My father, poorly led? World, world, O world!
But that thy strange mutations make us hate thee,
Life would not yield to age.

speeches.speech: O my good lord, I have been your tenant, and your father's tenant, These fourscore years.
lines: O my good lord,
I have been your tenant, and your father's tenant,
These fourscore years.

speeches.speech: Away, get thee away! Good friend, be gone. Thy comforts can do me no good at all; Thee they may hurt.
lines: Away, get thee away! Good friend, be gone.
Thy comforts can do me no good at all;
Thee they may hurt.

speeches.speech: You cannot see your way.
lines: You cannot see your way.

speeches.speech: I have no way, and therefore want no eyes; I stumbled when I saw. Full oft 'tis seen Our means secure us, and our mere defects Prove our commodities. Ah dear son Edgar, The food of thy abused father's wrath! Might I but live to see thee in my touch, I'ld say I had eyes again!
lines: I have no way, and therefore want no eyes;
I stumbled when I saw. Full oft 'tis seen
Our means secure us, and our mere defects
Prove our commodities. Ah dear son Edgar,
The food of thy abused father's wrath!
Might I but live to see thee in my touch,
I'ld say I had eyes again!

speeches.speech: How now? Who's there?
lines: How now? Who's there?

speeches.speech: [aside] O gods! Who is't can say 'I am at the worst'? I am worse than e'er I was.
lines: [aside] O gods! Who is't can say 'I am at the worst'?
I am worse than e'er I was.

speeches.speech: 'Tis poor mad Tom.
lines: 'Tis poor mad Tom.

speeches.speech: [aside] And worse I may be yet. The worst is not So long as we can say 'This is the worst.'
lines: [aside] And worse I may be yet. The worst is not
So long as we can say 'This is the worst.'

speeches.speech: Fellow, where goest?
lines: Fellow, where goest?

speeches.speech: Is it a beggarman?
lines: Is it a beggarman?

speeches.speech: Madman and beggar too.
lines: Madman and beggar too.

speeches.speech: He has some reason, else he could not beg. I' th' last night's storm I such a fellow saw, Which made me think a man a worm. My son Came then into my mind, and yet my mind Was then scarce friends with him. I have heard more since. As flies to wanton boys are we to th' gods. They kill us for their sport.
lines: He has some reason, else he could not beg.
I' th' last night's storm I such a fellow saw,
Which made me think a man a worm. My son
Came then into my mind, and yet my mind
Was then scarce friends with him. I have heard more since.
As flies to wanton boys are we to th' gods.
They kill us for their sport.

speeches.speech: [aside] How should this be? Bad is the trade that must play fool to sorrow, Ang'ring itself and others.- Bless thee, master!
lines: [aside] How should this be?
Bad is the trade that must play fool to sorrow,
Ang'ring itself and others.- Bless thee, master!

speeches.speech: Is that the naked fellow?
lines: Is that the naked fellow?

speeches.speech: Ay, my lord.
lines: Ay, my lord.

speeches.speech: Then prithee get thee gone. If for my sake Thou wilt o'ertake us hence a mile or twain I' th' way toward Dover, do it for ancient love; And bring some covering for this naked soul, Who I'll entreat to lead me.
lines: Then prithee get thee gone. If for my sake
Thou wilt o'ertake us hence a mile or twain
I' th' way toward Dover, do it for ancient love;
And bring some covering for this naked soul,
Who I'll entreat to lead me.

speeches.speech: Alack, sir, he is mad!
lines: Alack, sir, he is mad!

speeches.speech: 'Tis the time's plague when madmen lead the blind. Do as I bid thee, or rather do thy pleasure. Above the rest, be gone.
lines: 'Tis the time's plague when madmen lead the blind.
Do as I bid thee, or rather do thy pleasure.
Above the rest, be gone.

speeches.speech: I'll bring him the best 'parel that I have, Come on't what will.               Exit.
lines: I'll bring him the best 'parel that I have,
Come on't what will.               Exit.

speeches.speech: Sirrah naked fellow-
lines: Sirrah naked fellow-

speeches.speech: Poor Tom's acold. [Aside] I cannot daub it further.
lines: Poor Tom's acold. [Aside] I cannot daub it further.

speeches.speech: Come hither, fellow.
lines: Come hither, fellow.

speeches.speech: [aside] And yet I must.- Bless thy sweet eyes, they bleed.
lines: [aside] And yet I must.- Bless thy sweet eyes, they bleed.

speeches.speech: Know'st thou the way to Dover?
lines: Know'st thou the way to Dover?

speeches.speech: Both stile and gate, horseway and footpath. Poor Tom hath been scar'd out of his good wits. Bless thee, good man's son, from the foul fiend! Five fiends have been in poor Tom at once: of lust, as Obidicut; Hobbididence, prince of dumbness; Mahu, of stealing; Modo, of murder; Flibbertigibbet, of mopping and mowing, who since possesses chambermaids and waiting women. So, bless thee, master!
lines: Both stile and gate, horseway and footpath. Poor Tom hath been
scar'd out of his good wits. Bless thee, good man's son, from
the foul fiend! Five fiends have been in poor Tom at once: of
lust, as Obidicut; Hobbididence, prince of dumbness; Mahu, of
stealing; Modo, of murder; Flibbertigibbet, of mopping and
mowing, who since possesses chambermaids and waiting women. So,
bless thee, master!

speeches.speech: Here, take this purse, thou whom the heavens' plagues Have humbled to all strokes. That I am wretched Makes thee the happier. Heavens, deal so still! Let the superfluous and lust-dieted man, That slaves your ordinance, that will not see Because he does not feel, feel your pow'r quickly; So distribution should undo excess, And each man have enough. Dost thou know Dover?
lines: Here, take this purse, thou whom the heavens' plagues
Have humbled to all strokes. That I am wretched
Makes thee the happier. Heavens, deal so still!
Let the superfluous and lust-dieted man,
That slaves your ordinance, that will not see
Because he does not feel, feel your pow'r quickly;
So distribution should undo excess,
And each man have enough. Dost thou know Dover?

speeches.speech: Ay, master.
lines: Ay, master.

speeches.speech: There is a cliff, whose high and bending head Looks fearfully in the confined deep. Bring me but to the very brim of it, And I'll repair the misery thou dost bear With something rich about me. From that place I shall no leading need.
lines: There is a cliff, whose high and bending head
Looks fearfully in the confined deep.
Bring me but to the very brim of it,
And I'll repair the misery thou dost bear
With something rich about me. From that place
I shall no leading need.

speeches.speech: Give me thy arm. Poor Tom shall lead thee.
lines: Give me thy arm.
Poor Tom shall lead thee.

speeches.speech: Welcome, my lord. I marvel our mild husband Not met us on the way. [Enter Oswald the Steward.] Now, where's your master?
lines: Welcome, my lord. I marvel our mild husband
Not met us on the way. [Enter Oswald the Steward.]
Now, where's your master?

speeches.speech: Madam, within, but never man so chang'd. I told him of the army that was landed: He smil'd at it. I told him you were coming: His answer was, 'The worse.' Of Gloucester's treachery And of the loyal service of his son When I inform'd him, then he call'd me sot And told me I had turn'd the wrong side out. What most he should dislike seems pleasant to him; What like, offensive.
lines: Madam, within, but never man so chang'd.
I told him of the army that was landed:
He smil'd at it. I told him you were coming:
His answer was, 'The worse.' Of Gloucester's treachery
And of the loyal service of his son
When I inform'd him, then he call'd me sot
And told me I had turn'd the wrong side out.
What most he should dislike seems pleasant to him;
What like, offensive.

speeches.speech: [to Edmund] Then shall you go no further. It is the cowish terror of his spirit, That dares not undertake. He'll not feel wrongs Which tie him to an answer. Our wishes on the way May prove effects. Back, Edmund, to my brother. Hasten his musters and conduct his pow'rs. I must change arms at home and give the distaff Into my husband's hands. This trusty servant Shall pass between us. Ere long you are like to hear (If you dare venture in your own behalf) A mistress's command. Wear this. [Gives a favour.] Spare speech. Decline your head. This kiss, if it durst speak, Would stretch thy spirits up into the air. Conceive, and fare thee well.
lines: [to Edmund] Then shall you go no further.
It is the cowish terror of his spirit,
That dares not undertake. He'll not feel wrongs
Which tie him to an answer. Our wishes on the way
May prove effects. Back, Edmund, to my brother.
Hasten his musters and conduct his pow'rs.
I must change arms at home and give the distaff
Into my husband's hands. This trusty servant
Shall pass between us. Ere long you are like to hear
(If you dare venture in your own behalf)
A mistress's command. Wear this. [Gives a favour.]
Spare speech.
Decline your head. This kiss, if it durst speak,
Would stretch thy spirits up into the air.
Conceive, and fare thee well.

speeches.speech: Yours in the ranks of death!                        Exit.
lines: Yours in the ranks of death!                        Exit.

speeches.speech: My most dear Gloucester! O, the difference of man and man! To thee a woman's services are due; My fool usurps my body.
lines: My most dear Gloucester!
O, the difference of man and man!
To thee a woman's services are due;
My fool usurps my body.

speeches.speech: Madam, here comes my lord.       Exit.
lines: Madam, here comes my lord.       Exit.

speeches.speech: I have been worth the whistle.
lines: I have been worth the whistle.

speeches.speech: O Goneril, You are not worth the dust which the rude wind Blows in your face! I fear your disposition. That nature which contemns it origin Cannot be bordered certain in itself. She that herself will sliver and disbranch From her material sap, perforce must wither And come to deadly use.
lines: O Goneril,
You are not worth the dust which the rude wind
Blows in your face! I fear your disposition.
That nature which contemns it origin
Cannot be bordered certain in itself.
She that herself will sliver and disbranch
From her material sap, perforce must wither
And come to deadly use.

speeches.speech: No more! The text is foolish.
lines: No more! The text is foolish.

speeches.speech: Wisdom and goodness to the vile seem vile; Filths savour but themselves. What have you done? Tigers, not daughters, what have you perform'd? A father, and a gracious aged man, Whose reverence even the head-lugg'd bear would lick, Most barbarous, most degenerate, have you madded. Could my good brother suffer you to do it? A man, a prince, by him so benefited! If that the heavens do not their visible spirits Send quickly down to tame these vile offences, It will come, Humanity must perforce prey on itself, Like monsters of the deep.
lines: Wisdom and goodness to the vile seem vile;
Filths savour but themselves. What have you done?
Tigers, not daughters, what have you perform'd?
A father, and a gracious aged man,
Whose reverence even the head-lugg'd bear would lick,
Most barbarous, most degenerate, have you madded.
Could my good brother suffer you to do it?
A man, a prince, by him so benefited!
If that the heavens do not their visible spirits
Send quickly down to tame these vile offences,
It will come,
Humanity must perforce prey on itself,
Like monsters of the deep.

speeches.speech: Milk-liver'd man! That bear'st a cheek for blows, a head for wrongs; Who hast not in thy brows an eye discerning Thine honour from thy suffering; that not know'st Fools do those villains pity who are punish'd Ere they have done their mischief. Where's thy drum? France spreads his banners in our noiseless land, With plumed helm thy state begins to threat, Whiles thou, a moral fool, sit'st still, and criest 'Alack, why does he so?'
lines: Milk-liver'd man!
That bear'st a cheek for blows, a head for wrongs;
Who hast not in thy brows an eye discerning
Thine honour from thy suffering; that not know'st
Fools do those villains pity who are punish'd
Ere they have done their mischief. Where's thy drum?
France spreads his banners in our noiseless land,
With plumed helm thy state begins to threat,
Whiles thou, a moral fool, sit'st still, and criest
'Alack, why does he so?'

speeches.speech: See thyself, devil! Proper deformity seems not in the fiend So horrid as in woman.
lines: See thyself, devil!
Proper deformity seems not in the fiend
So horrid as in woman.

speeches.speech: O vain fool!
lines: O vain fool!

speeches.speech: Thou changed and self-cover'd thing, for shame! Bemonster not thy feature! Were't my fitness To let these hands obey my blood, They are apt enough to dislocate and tear Thy flesh and bones. Howe'er thou art a fiend, A woman's shape doth shield thee.
lines: Thou changed and self-cover'd thing, for shame!
Bemonster not thy feature! Were't my fitness
To let these hands obey my blood,
They are apt enough to dislocate and tear
Thy flesh and bones. Howe'er thou art a fiend,
A woman's shape doth shield thee.

speeches.speech: Marry, your manhood mew!
lines: Marry, your manhood mew!

speeches.speech: What news?
lines: What news?

speeches.speech: O, my good lord, the Duke of Cornwall 's dead, Slain by his servant, going to put out The other eye of Gloucester.
lines: O, my good lord, the Duke of Cornwall 's dead,
Slain by his servant, going to put out
The other eye of Gloucester.

speeches.speech: Gloucester's eyes?
lines: Gloucester's eyes?

speeches.speech: A servant that he bred, thrill'd with remorse, Oppos'd against the act, bending his sword To his great master; who, thereat enrag'd, Flew on him, and amongst them fell'd him dead; But not without that harmful stroke which since Hath pluck'd him after.
lines: A servant that he bred, thrill'd with remorse,
Oppos'd against the act, bending his sword
To his great master; who, thereat enrag'd,
Flew on him, and amongst them fell'd him dead;
But not without that harmful stroke which since
Hath pluck'd him after.

speeches.speech: This shows you are above, You justicers, that these our nether crimes So speedily can venge! But O poor Gloucester! Lose he his other eye?
lines: This shows you are above,
You justicers, that these our nether crimes
So speedily can venge! But O poor Gloucester!
Lose he his other eye?

speeches.speech: Both, both, my lord. This letter, madam, craves a speedy answer. 'Tis from your sister.
lines: Both, both, my lord.
This letter, madam, craves a speedy answer.
'Tis from your sister.

speeches.speech: [aside] One way I like this well; But being widow, and my Gloucester with her, May all the building in my fancy pluck Upon my hateful life. Another way The news is not so tart.- I'll read, and answer. Exit.
lines: [aside] One way I like this well;
But being widow, and my Gloucester with her,
May all the building in my fancy pluck
Upon my hateful life. Another way
The news is not so tart.- I'll read, and answer. Exit.

speeches.speech: Where was his son when they did take his eyes?
lines: Where was his son when they did take his eyes?

speeches.speech: Come with my lady hither.
lines: Come with my lady hither.

speeches.speech: He is not here.
lines: He is not here.

speeches.speech: No, my good lord; I met him back again.
lines: No, my good lord; I met him back again.

speeches.speech: Knows he the wickedness?
lines: Knows he the wickedness?

speeches.speech: Ay, my good lord. 'Twas he inform'd against him, And quit the house on purpose, that their punishment Might have the freer course.
lines: Ay, my good lord. 'Twas he inform'd against him,
And quit the house on purpose, that their punishment
Might have the freer course.

speeches.speech: Gloucester, I live To thank thee for the love thou show'dst the King, And to revenge thine eyes. Come hither, friend. Tell me what more thou know'st.
lines: Gloucester, I live
To thank thee for the love thou show'dst the King,
And to revenge thine eyes. Come hither, friend.
Tell me what more thou know'st.

speeches.speech: Why the King of France is so suddenly gone back know you the reason?
lines: Why the King of France is so suddenly gone back know you the
reason?

speeches.speech: Something he left imperfect in the state, which since his coming forth is thought of, which imports to the kingdom so much fear and danger that his personal return was most required and necessary.
lines: Something he left imperfect in the state, which since his
coming forth is thought of, which imports to the kingdom so much
fear and danger that his personal return was most required and
necessary.

speeches.speech: Who hath he left behind him general?
lines: Who hath he left behind him general?

speeches.speech: The Marshal of France, Monsieur La Far.
lines: The Marshal of France, Monsieur La Far.

speeches.speech: Did your letters pierce the Queen to any demonstration of grief?
lines: Did your letters pierce the Queen to any demonstration of
grief?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir. She took them, read them in my presence, And now and then an ample tear trill'd down Her delicate cheek. It seem'd she was a queen Over her passion, who, most rebel-like, Sought to be king o'er her.
lines: Ay, sir. She took them, read them in my presence,
And now and then an ample tear trill'd down
Her delicate cheek. It seem'd she was a queen
Over her passion, who, most rebel-like,
Sought to be king o'er her.

speeches.speech: O, then it mov'd her?
lines: O, then it mov'd her?

speeches.speech: Not to a rage. Patience and sorrow strove Who should express her goodliest. You have seen Sunshine and rain at once: her smiles and tears Were like, a better way. Those happy smilets That play'd on her ripe lip seem'd not to know What guests were in her eyes, which parted thence As pearls from diamonds dropp'd. In brief, Sorrow would be a rarity most belov'd, If all could so become it.
lines: Not to a rage. Patience and sorrow strove
Who should express her goodliest. You have seen
Sunshine and rain at once: her smiles and tears
Were like, a better way. Those happy smilets
That play'd on her ripe lip seem'd not to know
What guests were in her eyes, which parted thence
As pearls from diamonds dropp'd. In brief,
Sorrow would be a rarity most belov'd,
If all could so become it.

speeches.speech: Made she no verbal question?
lines: Made she no verbal question?

speeches.speech: Faith, once or twice she heav'd the name of father Pantingly forth, as if it press'd her heart; Cried 'Sisters, sisters! Shame of ladies! Sisters! Kent! father! sisters! What, i' th' storm? i' th' night? Let pity not be believ'd!' There she shook The holy water from her heavenly eyes, And clamour moisten'd. Then away she started To deal with grief alone.
lines: Faith, once or twice she heav'd the name of father
Pantingly forth, as if it press'd her heart;
Cried 'Sisters, sisters! Shame of ladies! Sisters!
Kent! father! sisters! What, i' th' storm? i' th' night?
Let pity not be believ'd!' There she shook
The holy water from her heavenly eyes,
And clamour moisten'd. Then away she started
To deal with grief alone.

speeches.speech: It is the stars, The stars above us, govern our conditions; Else one self mate and mate could not beget Such different issues. You spoke not with her since?
lines: It is the stars,
The stars above us, govern our conditions;
Else one self mate and mate could not beget
Such different issues. You spoke not with her since?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: Was this before the King return'd?
lines: Was this before the King return'd?

speeches.speech: No, since.
lines: No, since.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, the poor distressed Lear's i' th' town; Who sometime, in his better tune, remembers What we are come about, and by no means Will yield to see his daughter.
lines: Well, sir, the poor distressed Lear's i' th' town;
Who sometime, in his better tune, remembers
What we are come about, and by no means
Will yield to see his daughter.

speeches.speech: Why, good sir?
lines: Why, good sir?

speeches.speech: A sovereign shame so elbows him; his own unkindness, That stripp'd her from his benediction, turn'd her To foreign casualties, gave her dear rights To his dog-hearted daughters- these things sting His mind so venomously that burning shame Detains him from Cordelia.
lines: A sovereign shame so elbows him; his own unkindness,
That stripp'd her from his benediction, turn'd her
To foreign casualties, gave her dear rights
To his dog-hearted daughters- these things sting
His mind so venomously that burning shame
Detains him from Cordelia.

speeches.speech: Alack, poor gentleman!
lines: Alack, poor gentleman!

speeches.speech: Of Albany's and Cornwall's powers you heard not?
lines: Of Albany's and Cornwall's powers you heard not?

speeches.speech: 'Tis so; they are afoot.
lines: 'Tis so; they are afoot.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, I'll bring you to our master Lear And leave you to attend him. Some dear cause Will in concealment wrap me up awhile. When I am known aright, you shall not grieve Lending me this acquaintance. I pray you go Along with me.                   Exeunt.
lines: Well, sir, I'll bring you to our master Lear
And leave you to attend him. Some dear cause
Will in concealment wrap me up awhile.
When I am known aright, you shall not grieve
Lending me this acquaintance. I pray you go
Along with me.                   Exeunt.

speeches.speech: Alack, 'tis he! Why, he was met even now As mad as the vex'd sea, singing aloud, Crown'd with rank fumiter and furrow weeds, With harlocks, hemlock, nettles, cuckoo flow'rs, Darnel, and all the idle weeds that grow In our sustaining corn. A century send forth. Search every acre in the high-grown field And bring him to our eye. [Exit an Officer.] What can man's wisdom In the restoring his bereaved sense? He that helps him take all my outward worth.
lines: Alack, 'tis he! Why, he was met even now
As mad as the vex'd sea, singing aloud,
Crown'd with rank fumiter and furrow weeds,
With harlocks, hemlock, nettles, cuckoo flow'rs,
Darnel, and all the idle weeds that grow
In our sustaining corn. A century send forth.
Search every acre in the high-grown field
And bring him to our eye. [Exit an Officer.] What can man's
wisdom
In the restoring his bereaved sense?
He that helps him take all my outward worth.

speeches.speech: There is means, madam. Our foster nurse of nature is repose, The which he lacks. That to provoke in him Are many simples operative, whose power Will close the eye of anguish.
lines: There is means, madam.
Our foster nurse of nature is repose,
The which he lacks. That to provoke in him
Are many simples operative, whose power
Will close the eye of anguish.

speeches.speech: All blest secrets, All you unpublish'd virtues of the earth, Spring with my tears! be aidant and remediate In the good man's distress! Seek, seek for him! Lest his ungovern'd rage dissolve the life That wants the means to lead it.
lines: All blest secrets,
All you unpublish'd virtues of the earth,
Spring with my tears! be aidant and remediate
In the good man's distress! Seek, seek for him!
Lest his ungovern'd rage dissolve the life
That wants the means to lead it.

speeches.speech: News, madam. The British pow'rs are marching hitherward.
lines: News, madam.
The British pow'rs are marching hitherward.

speeches.speech: 'Tis known before. Our preparation stands In expectation of them. O dear father, It is thy business that I go about. Therefore great France My mourning and important tears hath pitied. No blown ambition doth our arms incite, But love, dear love, and our ag'd father's right. Soon may I hear and see him!
lines: 'Tis known before. Our preparation stands
In expectation of them. O dear father,
It is thy business that I go about.
Therefore great France
My mourning and important tears hath pitied.
No blown ambition doth our arms incite,
But love, dear love, and our ag'd father's right.
Soon may I hear and see him!

speeches.speech: But are my brother's pow'rs set forth?
lines: But are my brother's pow'rs set forth?

speeches.speech: Ay, madam.
lines: Ay, madam.

speeches.speech: Himself in person there?
lines: Himself in person there?

speeches.speech: Madam, with much ado. Your sister is the better soldier.
lines: Madam, with much ado.
Your sister is the better soldier.

speeches.speech: Lord Edmund spake not with your lord at home?
lines: Lord Edmund spake not with your lord at home?

speeches.speech: No, madam.
lines: No, madam.

speeches.speech: What might import my sister's letter to him?
lines: What might import my sister's letter to him?

speeches.speech: I know not, lady.
lines: I know not, lady.

speeches.speech: Faith, he is posted hence on serious matter. It was great ignorance, Gloucester's eyes being out, To let him live. Where he arrives he moves All hearts against us. Edmund, I think, is gone, In pity of his misery, to dispatch His nighted life; moreover, to descry The strength o' th' enemy.
lines: Faith, he is posted hence on serious matter.
It was great ignorance, Gloucester's eyes being out,
To let him live. Where he arrives he moves
All hearts against us. Edmund, I think, is gone,
In pity of his misery, to dispatch
His nighted life; moreover, to descry
The strength o' th' enemy.

speeches.speech: I must needs after him, madam, with my letter.
lines: I must needs after him, madam, with my letter.

speeches.speech: Our troops set forth to-morrow. Stay with us. The ways are dangerous.
lines: Our troops set forth to-morrow. Stay with us.
The ways are dangerous.

speeches.speech: I may not, madam. My lady charg'd my duty in this business.
lines: I may not, madam.
My lady charg'd my duty in this business.

speeches.speech: Why should she write to Edmund? Might not you Transport her purposes by word? Belike, Something- I know not what- I'll love thee much- Let me unseal the letter.
lines: Why should she write to Edmund? Might not you
Transport her purposes by word? Belike,
Something- I know not what- I'll love thee much-
Let me unseal the letter.

speeches.speech: Madam, I had rather-
lines: Madam, I had rather-

speeches.speech: I know your lady does not love her husband; I am sure of that; and at her late being here She gave strange eyeliads and most speaking looks To noble Edmund. I know you are of her bosom.
lines: I know your lady does not love her husband;
I am sure of that; and at her late being here
She gave strange eyeliads and most speaking looks
To noble Edmund. I know you are of her bosom.

speeches.speech: I, madam?
lines: I, madam?

speeches.speech: I speak in understanding. Y'are! I know't. Therefore I do advise you take this note. My lord is dead; Edmund and I have talk'd, And more convenient is he for my hand Than for your lady's. You may gather more. If you do find him, pray you give him this; And when your mistress hears thus much from you, I pray desire her call her wisdom to her. So farewell. If you do chance to hear of that blind traitor, Preferment falls on him that cuts him off.
lines: I speak in understanding. Y'are! I know't.
Therefore I do advise you take this note.
My lord is dead; Edmund and I have talk'd,
And more convenient is he for my hand
Than for your lady's. You may gather more.
If you do find him, pray you give him this;
And when your mistress hears thus much from you,
I pray desire her call her wisdom to her.
So farewell.
If you do chance to hear of that blind traitor,
Preferment falls on him that cuts him off.

speeches.speech: Would I could meet him, madam! I should show What party I do follow.
lines: Would I could meet him, madam! I should show
What party I do follow.

speeches.speech: Fare thee well.                Exeunt.
lines: Fare thee well.                Exeunt.

speeches.speech: When shall I come to th' top of that same hill?
lines: When shall I come to th' top of that same hill?

speeches.speech: You do climb up it now. Look how we labour.
lines: You do climb up it now. Look how we labour.

speeches.speech: Methinks the ground is even.
lines: Methinks the ground is even.

speeches.speech: Horrible steep. Hark, do you hear the sea?
lines: Horrible steep.
Hark, do you hear the sea?

speeches.speech: No, truly.
lines: No, truly.

speeches.speech: Why, then, your other senses grow imperfect By your eyes' anguish.
lines: Why, then, your other senses grow imperfect
By your eyes' anguish.

speeches.speech: So may it be indeed. Methinks thy voice is alter'd, and thou speak'st In better phrase and matter than thou didst.
lines: So may it be indeed.
Methinks thy voice is alter'd, and thou speak'st
In better phrase and matter than thou didst.

speeches.speech: Y'are much deceiv'd. In nothing am I chang'd But in my garments.
lines: Y'are much deceiv'd. In nothing am I chang'd
But in my garments.

speeches.speech: Methinks y'are better spoken.
lines: Methinks y'are better spoken.

speeches.speech: Come on, sir; here's the place. Stand still. How fearful And dizzy 'tis to cast one's eyes so low! The crows and choughs that wing the midway air Show scarce so gross as beetles. Halfway down Hangs one that gathers sampire- dreadful trade! Methinks he seems no bigger than his head. The fishermen that walk upon the beach Appear like mice; and yond tall anchoring bark, Diminish'd to her cock; her cock, a buoy Almost too small for sight. The murmuring surge That on th' unnumb'red idle pebble chafes Cannot be heard so high. I'll look no more, Lest my brain turn, and the deficient sight Topple down headlong.
lines: Come on, sir; here's the place. Stand still. How fearful
And dizzy 'tis to cast one's eyes so low!
The crows and choughs that wing the midway air
Show scarce so gross as beetles. Halfway down
Hangs one that gathers sampire- dreadful trade!
Methinks he seems no bigger than his head.
The fishermen that walk upon the beach
Appear like mice; and yond tall anchoring bark,
Diminish'd to her cock; her cock, a buoy
Almost too small for sight. The murmuring surge
That on th' unnumb'red idle pebble chafes
Cannot be heard so high. I'll look no more,
Lest my brain turn, and the deficient sight
Topple down headlong.

speeches.speech: Set me where you stand.
lines: Set me where you stand.

speeches.speech: Give me your hand. You are now within a foot Of th' extreme verge. For all beneath the moon Would I not leap upright.
lines: Give me your hand. You are now within a foot
Of th' extreme verge. For all beneath the moon
Would I not leap upright.

speeches.speech: Let go my hand. Here, friend, is another purse; in it a jewel Well worth a poor man's taking. Fairies and gods Prosper it with thee! Go thou further off; Bid me farewell, and let me hear thee going.
lines: Let go my hand.
Here, friend, is another purse; in it a jewel
Well worth a poor man's taking. Fairies and gods
Prosper it with thee! Go thou further off;
Bid me farewell, and let me hear thee going.

speeches.speech: Now fare ye well, good sir.
lines: Now fare ye well, good sir.

speeches.speech: With all my heart.
lines: With all my heart.

speeches.speech: [aside]. Why I do trifle thus with his despair Is done to cure it.
lines: [aside]. Why I do trifle thus with his despair
Is done to cure it.

speeches.speech: O you mighty gods!                            He kneels. This world I do renounce, and, in your sights, Shake patiently my great affliction off. If I could bear it longer and not fall To quarrel with your great opposeless wills, My snuff and loathed part of nature should Burn itself out. If Edgar live, O, bless him! Now, fellow, fare thee well. He falls [forward and swoons].
lines: O you mighty gods!                            He kneels.
This world I do renounce, and, in your sights,
Shake patiently my great affliction off.
If I could bear it longer and not fall
To quarrel with your great opposeless wills,
My snuff and loathed part of nature should
Burn itself out. If Edgar live, O, bless him!
Now, fellow, fare thee well.
He falls [forward and swoons].

speeches.speech: Gone, sir, farewell.- And yet I know not how conceit may rob The treasury of life when life itself Yields to the theft. Had he been where he thought, By this had thought been past.- Alive or dead? Ho you, sir! friend! Hear you, sir? Speak!- Thus might he pass indeed. Yet he revives. What are you, sir?
lines: Gone, sir, farewell.-
And yet I know not how conceit may rob
The treasury of life when life itself
Yields to the theft. Had he been where he thought,
By this had thought been past.- Alive or dead?
Ho you, sir! friend! Hear you, sir? Speak!-
Thus might he pass indeed. Yet he revives.
What are you, sir?

speeches.speech: Away, and let me die.
lines: Away, and let me die.

speeches.speech: Hadst thou been aught but gossamer, feathers, air, So many fadom down precipitating, Thou'dst shiver'd like an egg; but thou dost breathe; Hast heavy substance; bleed'st not; speak'st; art sound. Ten masts at each make not the altitude Which thou hast perpendicularly fell. Thy life is a miracle. Speak yet again.
lines: Hadst thou been aught but gossamer, feathers, air,
So many fadom down precipitating,
Thou'dst shiver'd like an egg; but thou dost breathe;
Hast heavy substance; bleed'st not; speak'st; art sound.
Ten masts at each make not the altitude
Which thou hast perpendicularly fell.
Thy life is a miracle. Speak yet again.

speeches.speech: But have I fall'n, or no?
lines: But have I fall'n, or no?

speeches.speech: From the dread summit of this chalky bourn. Look up a-height. The shrill-gorg'd lark so far Cannot be seen or heard. Do but look up.
lines: From the dread summit of this chalky bourn.
Look up a-height. The shrill-gorg'd lark so far
Cannot be seen or heard. Do but look up.

speeches.speech: Alack, I have no eyes! Is wretchedness depriv'd that benefit To end itself by death? 'Twas yet some comfort When misery could beguile the tyrant's rage And frustrate his proud will.
lines: Alack, I have no eyes!
Is wretchedness depriv'd that benefit
To end itself by death? 'Twas yet some comfort
When misery could beguile the tyrant's rage
And frustrate his proud will.

speeches.speech: Give me your arm. Up- so. How is't? Feel you your legs? You stand.
lines: Give me your arm.
Up- so. How is't? Feel you your legs? You stand.

speeches.speech: Too well, too well.
lines: Too well, too well.

speeches.speech: This is above all strangeness. Upon the crown o' th' cliff what thing was that Which parted from you?
lines: This is above all strangeness.
Upon the crown o' th' cliff what thing was that
Which parted from you?

speeches.speech: A poor unfortunate beggar.
lines: A poor unfortunate beggar.

speeches.speech: As I stood here below, methought his eyes Were two full moons; he had a thousand noses,Horns whelk'd and wav'd like the enridged sea. It was some fiend. Therefore, thou happy father, Think that the clearest gods, who make them honours Of men's impossibility, have preserv'd thee.
lines: As I stood here below, methought his eyes
Were two full moons; he had a thousand noses,Horns whelk'd and wav'd like the enridged sea.
It was some fiend. Therefore, thou happy father,
Think that the clearest gods, who make them honours
Of men's impossibility, have preserv'd thee.

speeches.speech: I do remember now. Henceforth I'll bear Affliction till it do cry out itself 'Enough, enough,' and die. That thing you speak of, I took it for a man. Often 'twould say 'The fiend, the fiend'- he led me to that place.
lines: I do remember now. Henceforth I'll bear
Affliction till it do cry out itself
'Enough, enough,' and die. That thing you speak of,
I took it for a man. Often 'twould say
'The fiend, the fiend'- he led me to that place.

speeches.speech: Bear free and patient thoughts. Enter Lear, mad, [fantastically dressed with weeds]. But who comes here? The safer sense will ne'er accommodate His master thus.
lines: Bear free and patient thoughts.
Enter Lear, mad, [fantastically dressed with weeds].
But who comes here?
The safer sense will ne'er accommodate
His master thus.

speeches.speech: No, they cannot touch me for coming; I am the King himself.
lines: No, they cannot touch me for coming;
I am the King himself.

speeches.speech: O thou side-piercing sight!
lines: O thou side-piercing sight!

speeches.speech: Nature 's above art in that respect. There's your press money. That fellow handles his bow like a crow-keeper. Draw me a clothier's yard. Look, look, a mouse! Peace, peace; this piece of toasted cheese will do't. There's my gauntlet; I'll prove it on a giant. Bring up the brown bills. O, well flown, bird! i' th' clout, i' th' clout! Hewgh! Give the word.
lines: Nature 's above art in that respect. There's your press
money. That fellow handles his bow like a crow-keeper. Draw me
a clothier's yard. Look, look, a mouse! Peace, peace; this piece
of toasted cheese will do't. There's my gauntlet; I'll prove it
on a giant. Bring up the brown bills. O, well flown, bird! i'
th' clout, i' th' clout! Hewgh! Give the word.

speeches.speech: Sweet marjoram.
lines: Sweet marjoram.

speeches.speech: Pass.
lines: Pass.

speeches.speech: I know that voice.
lines: I know that voice.

speeches.speech: Ha! Goneril with a white beard? They flatter'd me like a dog, and told me I had white hairs in my beard ere the black ones were there. To say 'ay' and 'no' to everything I said! 'Ay' and 'no' too was no good divinity. When the rain came to wet me once, and the wind to make me chatter; when the thunder would not peace at my bidding; there I found 'em, there I smelt 'em out. Go to, they are not men o' their words! They told me I was everything. 'Tis a lie- I am not ague-proof.
lines: Ha! Goneril with a white beard? They flatter'd me like a dog,
and told me I had white hairs in my beard ere the black ones
were there. To say 'ay' and 'no' to everything I said! 'Ay' and
'no' too was no good divinity. When the rain came to wet me
once, and the wind to make me chatter; when the thunder would
not peace at my bidding; there I found 'em, there I smelt 'em
out. Go to, they are not men o' their words! They told me I was
everything. 'Tis a lie- I am not ague-proof.

speeches.speech: The trick of that voice I do well remember. Is't not the King?
lines: The trick of that voice I do well remember.
Is't not the King?

speeches.speech: Ay, every inch a king! When I do stare, see how the subject quakes. I pardon that man's life. What was thy cause? Adultery? Thou shalt not die. Die for adultery? No. The wren goes to't, and the small gilded fly Does lecher in my sight. Let copulation thrive; for Gloucester's bastard son Was kinder to his father than my daughters Got 'tween the lawful sheets. To't, luxury, pell-mell! for I lack soldiers. Behold yond simp'ring dame, Whose face between her forks presageth snow, That minces virtue, and does shake the head To hear of pleasure's name. The fitchew nor the soiled horse goes to't With a more riotous appetite. Down from the waist they are Centaurs, Though women all above. But to the girdle do the gods inherit, Beneath is all the fiend's. There's hell, there's darkness, there's the sulphurous pit; burning, scalding, stench, consumption. Fie, fie, fie! pah, pah! Give me an ounce of civet, good apothecary, to sweeten my imagination. There's money for thee.
lines: Ay, every inch a king!
When I do stare, see how the subject quakes.
I pardon that man's life. What was thy cause?
Adultery?
Thou shalt not die. Die for adultery? No.
The wren goes to't, and the small gilded fly
Does lecher in my sight.
Let copulation thrive; for Gloucester's bastard son
Was kinder to his father than my daughters
Got 'tween the lawful sheets.
To't, luxury, pell-mell! for I lack soldiers.
Behold yond simp'ring dame,
Whose face between her forks presageth snow,
That minces virtue, and does shake the head
To hear of pleasure's name.
The fitchew nor the soiled horse goes to't
With a more riotous appetite.
Down from the waist they are Centaurs,
Though women all above.
But to the girdle do the gods inherit,
Beneath is all the fiend's.
There's hell, there's darkness, there's the sulphurous pit;
burning, scalding, stench, consumption. Fie, fie, fie! pah, pah!
Give me an ounce of civet, good apothecary, to sweeten my
imagination. There's money for thee.

speeches.speech: O, let me kiss that hand!
lines: O, let me kiss that hand!

speeches.speech: Let me wipe it first; it smells of mortality.
lines: Let me wipe it first; it smells of mortality.
