speeches.speech: O ruin'd piece of nature! This great world Shall so wear out to naught. Dost thou know me?
lines: O ruin'd piece of nature! This great world
Shall so wear out to naught. Dost thou know me?

speeches.speech: I remember thine eyes well enough. Dost thou squiny at me? No, do thy worst, blind Cupid! I'll not love. Read thou this challenge; mark but the penning of it.
lines: I remember thine eyes well enough. Dost thou squiny at me?
No, do thy worst, blind Cupid! I'll not love. Read thou this
challenge; mark but the penning of it.

speeches.speech: Were all the letters suns, I could not see one.
lines: Were all the letters suns, I could not see one.

speeches.speech: [aside] I would not take this from report. It is, And my heart breaks at it.
lines: [aside] I would not take this from report. It is,
And my heart breaks at it.

speeches.speech: Read.
lines: Read.

speeches.speech: What, with the case of eyes?
lines: What, with the case of eyes?

speeches.speech: O, ho, are you there with me? No eyes in your head, nor no money in your purse? Your eyes are in a heavy case, your purse in a light. Yet you see how this world goes.
lines: O, ho, are you there with me? No eyes in your head, nor no
money in your purse? Your eyes are in a heavy case, your purse
in a light. Yet you see how this world goes.

speeches.speech: I see it feelingly.
lines: I see it feelingly.

speeches.speech: What, art mad? A man may see how the world goes with no eyes. Look with thine ears. See how yond justice rails upon yond simple thief. Hark in thine ear. Change places and, handy-dandy, which is the justice, which is the thief? Thou hast seen a farmer's dog bark at a beggar?
lines: What, art mad? A man may see how the world goes with no eyes.
Look with thine ears. See how yond justice rails upon yond
simple thief. Hark in thine ear. Change places and, handy-dandy,
which is the justice, which is the thief? Thou hast seen a
farmer's dog bark at a beggar?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir.
lines: Ay, sir.

speeches.speech: And the creature run from the cur? There thou mightst behold the great image of authority: a dog's obeyed in office. Thou rascal beadle, hold thy bloody hand! Why dost thou lash that whore? Strip thine own back. Thou hotly lusts to use her in that kind For which thou whip'st her. The usurer hangs the cozener. Through tatter'd clothes small vices do appear; Robes and furr'd gowns hide all. Plate sin with gold, And the strong lance of justice hurtless breaks; Arm it in rags, a pygmy's straw does pierce it. None does offend, none- I say none! I'll able 'em. Take that of me, my friend, who have the power To seal th' accuser's lips. Get thee glass eyes And, like a scurvy politician, seem To see the things thou dost not. Now, now, now, now! Pull off my boots. Harder, harder! So.
lines: And the creature run from the cur? There thou mightst behold
the great image of authority: a dog's obeyed in office.
Thou rascal beadle, hold thy bloody hand!
Why dost thou lash that whore? Strip thine own back.
Thou hotly lusts to use her in that kind
For which thou whip'st her. The usurer hangs the cozener.
Through tatter'd clothes small vices do appear;
Robes and furr'd gowns hide all. Plate sin with gold,
And the strong lance of justice hurtless breaks;
Arm it in rags, a pygmy's straw does pierce it.
None does offend, none- I say none! I'll able 'em.
Take that of me, my friend, who have the power
To seal th' accuser's lips. Get thee glass eyes
And, like a scurvy politician, seem
To see the things thou dost not. Now, now, now, now!
Pull off my boots. Harder, harder! So.

speeches.speech: O, matter and impertinency mix'd! Reason, in madness!
lines: O, matter and impertinency mix'd!
Reason, in madness!

speeches.speech: If thou wilt weep my fortunes, take my eyes. I know thee well enough; thy name is Gloucester. Thou must be patient. We came crying hither; Thou know'st, the first time that we smell the air We wawl and cry. I will preach to thee. Mark.
lines: If thou wilt weep my fortunes, take my eyes.
I know thee well enough; thy name is Gloucester.
Thou must be patient. We came crying hither;
Thou know'st, the first time that we smell the air
We wawl and cry. I will preach to thee. Mark.

speeches.speech: Alack, alack the day!
lines: Alack, alack the day!

speeches.speech: When we are born, we cry that we are come To this great stage of fools. This' a good block. It were a delicate stratagem to shoe A troop of horse with felt. I'll put't in proof, And when I have stol'n upon these sons-in-law, Then kill, kill, kill, kill, kill, kill!
lines: When we are born, we cry that we are come
To this great stage of fools. This' a good block.
It were a delicate stratagem to shoe
A troop of horse with felt. I'll put't in proof,
And when I have stol'n upon these sons-in-law,
Then kill, kill, kill, kill, kill, kill!

speeches.speech: O, here he is! Lay hand upon him.- Sir, Your most dear daughter-
lines: O, here he is! Lay hand upon him.- Sir,
Your most dear daughter-

speeches.speech: No rescue? What, a prisoner? I am even The natural fool of fortune. Use me well; You shall have ransom. Let me have a surgeon; I am cut to th' brains.
lines: No rescue? What, a prisoner? I am even
The natural fool of fortune. Use me well;
You shall have ransom. Let me have a surgeon;
I am cut to th' brains.

speeches.speech: You shall have anything.
lines: You shall have anything.

speeches.speech: No seconds? All myself? Why, this would make a man a man of salt, To use his eyes for garden waterpots, Ay, and laying autumn's dust.
lines: No seconds? All myself?
Why, this would make a man a man of salt,
To use his eyes for garden waterpots,
Ay, and laying autumn's dust.

speeches.speech: Good sir-
lines: Good sir-

speeches.speech: I will die bravely, like a smug bridegroom. What! I will be jovial. Come, come, I am a king; My masters, know you that?
lines: I will die bravely, like a smug bridegroom. What!
I will be jovial. Come, come, I am a king;
My masters, know you that?

speeches.speech: You are a royal one, and we obey you.
lines: You are a royal one, and we obey you.

speeches.speech: Then there's life in't. Nay, an you get it, you shall get it by running. Sa, sa, sa, sa!
lines: Then there's life in't. Nay, an you get it, you shall get it
by running. Sa, sa, sa, sa!

speeches.speech: A sight most pitiful in the meanest wretch, Past speaking of in a king! Thou hast one daughter Who redeems nature from the general curse Which twain have brought her to.
lines: A sight most pitiful in the meanest wretch,
Past speaking of in a king! Thou hast one daughter
Who redeems nature from the general curse
Which twain have brought her to.

speeches.speech: Hail, gentle sir.
lines: Hail, gentle sir.

speeches.speech: Sir, speed you. What's your will?
lines: Sir, speed you. What's your will?

speeches.speech: Do you hear aught, sir, of a battle toward?
lines: Do you hear aught, sir, of a battle toward?

speeches.speech: Most sure and vulgar. Every one hears that Which can distinguish sound.
lines: Most sure and vulgar. Every one hears that
Which can distinguish sound.

speeches.speech: But, by your favour, How near's the other army?
lines: But, by your favour,
How near's the other army?

speeches.speech: Near and on speedy foot. The main descry Stands on the hourly thought.
lines: Near and on speedy foot. The main descry
Stands on the hourly thought.

speeches.speech: I thank you sir. That's all.
lines: I thank you sir. That's all.

speeches.speech: Though that the Queen on special cause is here, Her army is mov'd on.
lines: Though that the Queen on special cause is here,
Her army is mov'd on.

speeches.speech: I thank you, sir
lines: I thank you, sir

speeches.speech: You ever-gentle gods, take my breath from me; Let not my worser spirit tempt me again To die before you please!
lines: You ever-gentle gods, take my breath from me;
Let not my worser spirit tempt me again
To die before you please!

speeches.speech: Well pray you, father.
lines: Well pray you, father.

speeches.speech: Now, good sir, what are you?
lines: Now, good sir, what are you?

speeches.speech: A most poor man, made tame to fortune's blows, Who, by the art of known and feeling sorrows, Am pregnant to good pity. Give me your hand; I'll lead you to some biding.
lines: A most poor man, made tame to fortune's blows,
Who, by the art of known and feeling sorrows,
Am pregnant to good pity. Give me your hand;
I'll lead you to some biding.

speeches.speech: Hearty thanks. The bounty and the benison of heaven To boot, and boot!
lines: Hearty thanks.
The bounty and the benison of heaven
To boot, and boot!

speeches.speech: A proclaim'd prize! Most happy! That eyeless head of thine was first fram'd flesh To raise my fortunes. Thou old unhappy traitor, Briefly thyself remember. The sword is out That must destroy thee.
lines: A proclaim'd prize! Most happy!
That eyeless head of thine was first fram'd flesh
To raise my fortunes. Thou old unhappy traitor,
Briefly thyself remember. The sword is out
That must destroy thee.

speeches.speech: Now let thy friendly hand Put strength enough to't.
lines: Now let thy friendly hand
Put strength enough to't.

speeches.speech: Wherefore, bold peasant, Dar'st thou support a publish'd traitor? Hence! Lest that th' infection of his fortune take Like hold on thee. Let go his arm.
lines: Wherefore, bold peasant,
Dar'st thou support a publish'd traitor? Hence!
Lest that th' infection of his fortune take
Like hold on thee. Let go his arm.

speeches.speech: Chill not let go, zir, without vurther 'cagion.
lines: Chill not let go, zir, without vurther 'cagion.

speeches.speech: Let go, slave, or thou diest!
lines: Let go, slave, or thou diest!

speeches.speech: Good gentleman, go your gait, and let poor voke pass. An chud ha' bin zwagger'd out of my life, 'twould not ha' bin zo long as 'tis by a vortnight. Nay, come not near th' old man. Keep out, che vore ye, or Ise try whether your costard or my ballow be the harder. Chill be plain with you.
lines: Good gentleman, go your gait, and let poor voke pass. An chud
ha' bin zwagger'd out of my life, 'twould not ha' bin zo long as
'tis by a vortnight. Nay, come not near th' old man. Keep out,
che vore ye, or Ise try whether your costard or my ballow be the
harder. Chill be plain with you.

speeches.speech: Out, dunghill!
lines: Out, dunghill!

speeches.speech: Chill pick your teeth, zir. Come! No matter vor your foins.
lines: Chill pick your teeth, zir. Come! No matter vor your foins.

speeches.speech: Slave, thou hast slain me. Villain, take my purse. If ever thou wilt thrive, bury my body, And give the letters which thou find'st about me To Edmund Earl of Gloucester. Seek him out Upon the British party. O, untimely death! Death!
lines: Slave, thou hast slain me. Villain, take my purse.
If ever thou wilt thrive, bury my body,
And give the letters which thou find'st about me
To Edmund Earl of Gloucester. Seek him out
Upon the British party. O, untimely death! Death!

speeches.speech: I know thee well. A serviceable villain, As duteous to the vices of thy mistress As badness would desire.
lines: I know thee well. A serviceable villain,
As duteous to the vices of thy mistress
As badness would desire.

speeches.speech: What, is he dead?
lines: What, is he dead?

speeches.speech: Sit you down, father; rest you. Let's see his pockets; these letters that he speaks of May be my friends. He's dead. I am only sorry He had no other deathsman. Let us see. Leave, gentle wax; and, manners, blame us not. To know our enemies' minds, we'ld rip their hearts; Their papers, is more lawful. Reads the letter. 'Let our reciprocal vows be rememb'red. You have many opportunities to cut him off. If your will want not, time and place will be fruitfully offer'd. There is nothing done, if he return the conqueror. Then am I the prisoner, and his bed my jail; from the loathed warmth whereof deliver me, and supply the place for your labour. 'Your (wife, so I would say) affectionate servant, 'Goneril.' O indistinguish'd space of woman's will! A plot upon her virtuous husband's life, And the exchange my brother! Here in the sands Thee I'll rake up, the post unsanctified Of murtherous lechers; and in the mature time With this ungracious paper strike the sight Of the death-practis'd Duke, For him 'tis well That of thy death and business I can tell.
lines: Sit you down, father; rest you.
Let's see his pockets; these letters that he speaks of
May be my friends. He's dead. I am only sorry
He had no other deathsman. Let us see.
Leave, gentle wax; and, manners, blame us not.
To know our enemies' minds, we'ld rip their hearts;
Their papers, is more lawful. Reads the letter.
'Let our reciprocal vows be rememb'red. You have many
opportunities to cut him off. If your will want not, time and
place will be fruitfully offer'd. There is nothing done, if he
return the conqueror. Then am I the prisoner, and his bed my
jail; from the loathed warmth whereof deliver me, and supply the
place for your labour.
'Your (wife, so I would say) affectionate servant, 'Goneril.'
O indistinguish'd space of woman's will!
A plot upon her virtuous husband's life,
And the exchange my brother! Here in the sands
Thee I'll rake up, the post unsanctified
Of murtherous lechers; and in the mature time
With this ungracious paper strike the sight
Of the death-practis'd Duke, For him 'tis well
That of thy death and business I can tell.

speeches.speech: The King is mad. How stiff is my vile sense, That I stand up, and have ingenious feeling Of my huge sorrows! Better I were distract. So should my thoughts be sever'd from my griefs, And woes by wrong imaginations lose The knowledge of themselves.
lines: The King is mad. How stiff is my vile sense,
That I stand up, and have ingenious feeling
Of my huge sorrows! Better I were distract.
So should my thoughts be sever'd from my griefs,
And woes by wrong imaginations lose
The knowledge of themselves.

speeches.speech: Give me your hand. Far off methinks I hear the beaten drum. Come, father, I'll bestow you with a friend. Exeunt.
lines: Give me your hand.
Far off methinks I hear the beaten drum.
Come, father, I'll bestow you with a friend. Exeunt.

speeches.speech: O thou good Kent, how shall I live and work To match thy goodness? My life will be too short And every measure fail me.
lines: O thou good Kent, how shall I live and work
To match thy goodness? My life will be too short
And every measure fail me.

speeches.speech: To be acknowledg'd, madam, is o'erpaid. All my reports go with the modest truth; Nor more nor clipp'd, but so.
lines: To be acknowledg'd, madam, is o'erpaid.
All my reports go with the modest truth;
Nor more nor clipp'd, but so.

speeches.speech: Be better suited. These weeds are memories of those worser hours. I prithee put them off.
lines: Be better suited.
These weeds are memories of those worser hours.
I prithee put them off.

speeches.speech: Pardon, dear madam. Yet to be known shortens my made intent. My boon I make it that you know me not Till time and I think meet.
lines: Pardon, dear madam.
Yet to be known shortens my made intent.
My boon I make it that you know me not
Till time and I think meet.

speeches.speech: Then be't so, my good lord. [To the Doctor] How, does the King?
lines: Then be't so, my good lord. [To the Doctor] How, does the King?

speeches.speech: Madam, sleeps still.
lines: Madam, sleeps still.

speeches.speech: O you kind gods, Cure this great breach in his abused nature! Th' untun'd and jarring senses, O, wind up Of this child-changed father!
lines: O you kind gods,
Cure this great breach in his abused nature!
Th' untun'd and jarring senses, O, wind up
Of this child-changed father!

speeches.speech: So please your Majesty That we may wake the King? He hath slept long.
lines: So please your Majesty
That we may wake the King? He hath slept long.

speeches.speech: Be govern'd by your knowledge, and proceed I' th' sway of your own will. Is he array'd?
lines: Be govern'd by your knowledge, and proceed
I' th' sway of your own will. Is he array'd?

speeches.speech: Ay, madam. In the heaviness of sleep We put fresh garments on him.
lines: Ay, madam. In the heaviness of sleep
We put fresh garments on him.

speeches.speech: Be by, good madam, when we do awake him. I doubt not of his temperance.
lines: Be by, good madam, when we do awake him.
I doubt not of his temperance.

speeches.speech: Very well.
lines: Very well.

speeches.speech: Please you draw near. Louder the music there!
lines: Please you draw near. Louder the music there!

speeches.speech: O my dear father, restoration hang Thy medicine on my lips, and let this kiss Repair those violent harms that my two sisters Have in thy reverence made!
lines: O my dear father, restoration hang
Thy medicine on my lips, and let this kiss
Repair those violent harms that my two sisters
Have in thy reverence made!

speeches.speech: Kind and dear princess!
lines: Kind and dear princess!

speeches.speech: Had you not been their father, these white flakes Had challeng'd pity of them. Was this a face To be oppos'd against the warring winds? To stand against the deep dread-bolted thunder? In the most terrible and nimble stroke Of quick cross lightning? to watch- poor perdu!- With this thin helm? Mine enemy's dog, Though he had bit me, should have stood that night Against my fire; and wast thou fain, poor father, To hovel thee with swine and rogues forlorn, In short and musty straw? Alack, alack! 'Tis wonder that thy life and wits at once Had not concluded all.- He wakes. Speak to him.
lines: Had you not been their father, these white flakes
Had challeng'd pity of them. Was this a face
To be oppos'd against the warring winds?
To stand against the deep dread-bolted thunder?
In the most terrible and nimble stroke
Of quick cross lightning? to watch- poor perdu!-
With this thin helm? Mine enemy's dog,
Though he had bit me, should have stood that night
Against my fire; and wast thou fain, poor father,
To hovel thee with swine and rogues forlorn,
In short and musty straw? Alack, alack!
'Tis wonder that thy life and wits at once
Had not concluded all.- He wakes. Speak to him.

speeches.speech: Madam, do you; 'tis fittest.
lines: Madam, do you; 'tis fittest.

speeches.speech: How does my royal lord? How fares your Majesty?
lines: How does my royal lord? How fares your Majesty?

speeches.speech: You do me wrong to take me out o' th' grave. Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears Do scald like molten lead.
lines: You do me wrong to take me out o' th' grave.
Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound
Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears
Do scald like molten lead.

speeches.speech: Sir, do you know me?
lines: Sir, do you know me?

speeches.speech: You are a spirit, I know. When did you die?
lines: You are a spirit, I know. When did you die?

speeches.speech: Still, still, far wide!
lines: Still, still, far wide!

speeches.speech: He's scarce awake. Let him alone awhile.
lines: He's scarce awake. Let him alone awhile.

speeches.speech: Where have I been? Where am I? Fair daylight, I am mightily abus'd. I should e'en die with pity, To see another thus. I know not what to say. I will not swear these are my hands. Let's see. I feel this pin prick. Would I were assur'd Of my condition!
lines: Where have I been? Where am I? Fair daylight,
I am mightily abus'd. I should e'en die with pity,
To see another thus. I know not what to say.
I will not swear these are my hands. Let's see.
I feel this pin prick. Would I were assur'd
Of my condition!

speeches.speech: O, look upon me, sir, And hold your hands in benediction o'er me. No, sir, you must not kneel.
lines: O, look upon me, sir,
And hold your hands in benediction o'er me.
No, sir, you must not kneel.

speeches.speech: Pray, do not mock me. I am a very foolish fond old man, Fourscore and upward, not an hour more nor less; And, to deal plainly, I fear I am not in my perfect mind. Methinks I should know you, and know this man; Yet I am doubtful; for I am mainly ignorant What place this is; and all the skill I have Remembers not these garments; nor I know not Where I did lodge last night. Do not laugh at me; For (as I am a man) I think this lady To be my child Cordelia.
lines: Pray, do not mock me.
I am a very foolish fond old man,
Fourscore and upward, not an hour more nor less;
And, to deal plainly,
I fear I am not in my perfect mind.
Methinks I should know you, and know this man;
Yet I am doubtful; for I am mainly ignorant
What place this is; and all the skill I have
Remembers not these garments; nor I know not
Where I did lodge last night. Do not laugh at me;
For (as I am a man) I think this lady
To be my child Cordelia.

speeches.speech: And so I am! I am!
lines: And so I am! I am!

speeches.speech: Be your tears wet? Yes, faith. I pray weep not. If you have poison for me, I will drink it. I know you do not love me; for your sisters Have, as I do remember, done me wrong. You have some cause, they have not.
lines: Be your tears wet? Yes, faith. I pray weep not.
If you have poison for me, I will drink it.
I know you do not love me; for your sisters
Have, as I do remember, done me wrong.
You have some cause, they have not.

speeches.speech: No cause, no cause.
lines: No cause, no cause.

speeches.speech: Am I in France?
lines: Am I in France?

speeches.speech: In your own kingdom, sir.
lines: In your own kingdom, sir.

speeches.speech: Do not abuse me.
lines: Do not abuse me.

speeches.speech: Be comforted, good madam. The great rage You see is kill'd in him; and yet it is danger To make him even o'er the time he has lost. Desire him to go in. Trouble him no more Till further settling.
lines: Be comforted, good madam. The great rage
You see is kill'd in him; and yet it is danger
To make him even o'er the time he has lost.
Desire him to go in. Trouble him no more
Till further settling.

speeches.speech: Will't please your Highness walk?
lines: Will't please your Highness walk?

speeches.speech: You must bear with me. Pray you now, forget and forgive. I am old and foolish.
lines: You must bear with me.
Pray you now, forget and forgive. I am old and foolish.

speeches.speech: Holds it true, sir, that the Duke of Cornwall was so slain?
lines: Holds it true, sir, that the Duke of Cornwall was so slain?

speeches.speech: Most certain, sir.
lines: Most certain, sir.

speeches.speech: Who is conductor of his people?
lines: Who is conductor of his people?

speeches.speech: As 'tis said, the bastard son of Gloucester.
lines: As 'tis said, the bastard son of Gloucester.

speeches.speech: They say Edgar, his banish'd son, is with the Earl of Kent in Germany.
lines: They say Edgar, his banish'd son, is with the Earl of Kent
in Germany.

speeches.speech: Report is changeable. 'Tis time to look about; the powers of the kingdom approach apace.
lines: Report is changeable. 'Tis time to look about; the powers of
the kingdom approach apace.

speeches.speech: The arbitrement is like to be bloody. Fare you well, sir.              [Exit.]
lines: The arbitrement is like to be bloody.
Fare you well, sir.              [Exit.]

speeches.speech: My point and period will be throughly wrought, Or well or ill, as this day's battle's fought. Exit.
lines: My point and period will be throughly wrought,
Or well or ill, as this day's battle's fought. Exit.

speeches.speech: Know of the Duke if his last purpose hold, Or whether since he is advis'd by aught To change the course. He's full of alteration And self-reproving. Bring his constant pleasure.
lines: Know of the Duke if his last purpose hold,
Or whether since he is advis'd by aught
To change the course. He's full of alteration
And self-reproving. Bring his constant pleasure.

speeches.speech: Our sister's man is certainly miscarried.
lines: Our sister's man is certainly miscarried.

speeches.speech: Tis to be doubted, madam.
lines: Tis to be doubted, madam.

speeches.speech: Now, sweet lord, You know the goodness I intend upon you. Tell me- but truly- but then speak the truth- Do you not love my sister?
lines: Now, sweet lord,
You know the goodness I intend upon you.
Tell me- but truly- but then speak the truth-
Do you not love my sister?

speeches.speech: In honour'd love.
lines: In honour'd love.

speeches.speech: But have you never found my brother's way To the forfended place?
lines: But have you never found my brother's way
To the forfended place?

speeches.speech: That thought abuses you.
lines: That thought abuses you.

speeches.speech: I am doubtful that you have been conjunct And bosom'd with her, as far as we call hers.
lines: I am doubtful that you have been conjunct
And bosom'd with her, as far as we call hers.

speeches.speech: No, by mine honour, madam.
lines: No, by mine honour, madam.

speeches.speech: I never shall endure her. Dear my lord, Be not familiar with her.
lines: I never shall endure her. Dear my lord,
Be not familiar with her.

speeches.speech: Fear me not. She and the Duke her husband! Enter, with Drum and Colours, Albany, Goneril, Soldiers.
lines: Fear me not.
She and the Duke her husband!
Enter, with Drum and Colours, Albany, Goneril, Soldiers.

speeches.speech: [aside] I had rather lose the battle than that sister Should loosen him and me.
lines: [aside] I had rather lose the battle than that sister
Should loosen him and me.

speeches.speech: Our very loving sister, well bemet. Sir, this I hear: the King is come to his daughter, With others whom the rigour of our state Forc'd to cry out. Where I could not be honest, I never yet was valiant. For this business, It toucheth us as France invades our land, Not bolds the King, with others whom, I fear, Most just and heavy causes make oppose.
lines: Our very loving sister, well bemet.
Sir, this I hear: the King is come to his daughter,
With others whom the rigour of our state
Forc'd to cry out. Where I could not be honest,
I never yet was valiant. For this business,
It toucheth us as France invades our land,
Not bolds the King, with others whom, I fear,
Most just and heavy causes make oppose.

speeches.speech: Sir, you speak nobly.
lines: Sir, you speak nobly.

speeches.speech: Why is this reason'd?
lines: Why is this reason'd?

speeches.speech: Combine together 'gainst the enemy; For these domestic and particular broils Are not the question here.
lines: Combine together 'gainst the enemy;
For these domestic and particular broils
Are not the question here.

speeches.speech: Let's then determine With th' ancient of war on our proceeding.
lines: Let's then determine
With th' ancient of war on our proceeding.

speeches.speech: I shall attend you presently at your tent.
lines: I shall attend you presently at your tent.

speeches.speech: Sister, you'll go with us?
lines: Sister, you'll go with us?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: 'Tis most convenient. Pray you go with us.
lines: 'Tis most convenient. Pray you go with us.

speeches.speech: [aside] O, ho, I know the riddle.- I will go. [As they are going out,] enter Edgar [disguised].
lines: [aside] O, ho, I know the riddle.- I will go.
[As they are going out,] enter Edgar [disguised].

speeches.speech: If e'er your Grace had speech with man so poor, Hear me one word.
lines: If e'er your Grace had speech with man so poor,
Hear me one word.

speeches.speech: I'll overtake you.- Speak.
lines: I'll overtake you.- Speak.

speeches.speech: Before you fight the battle, ope this letter. If you have victory, let the trumpet sound For him that brought it. Wretched though I seem, I can produce a champion that will prove What is avouched there. If you miscarry, Your business of the world hath so an end, And machination ceases. Fortune love you!
lines: Before you fight the battle, ope this letter.
If you have victory, let the trumpet sound
For him that brought it. Wretched though I seem,
I can produce a champion that will prove
What is avouched there. If you miscarry,
Your business of the world hath so an end,
And machination ceases. Fortune love you!

speeches.speech: Stay till I have read the letter.
lines: Stay till I have read the letter.

speeches.speech: I was forbid it. When time shall serve, let but the herald cry, And I'll appear again.
lines: I was forbid it.
When time shall serve, let but the herald cry,
And I'll appear again.

speeches.speech: Why, fare thee well. I will o'erlook thy paper.
lines: Why, fare thee well. I will o'erlook thy paper.

speeches.speech: The enemy 's in view; draw up your powers. Here is the guess of their true strength and forces By diligent discovery; but your haste Is now urg'd on you.
lines: The enemy 's in view; draw up your powers.
Here is the guess of their true strength and forces
By diligent discovery; but your haste
Is now urg'd on you.

speeches.speech: We will greet the time.          Exit.
lines: We will greet the time.          Exit.

speeches.speech: To both these sisters have I sworn my love; Each jealous of the other, as the stung Are of the adder. Which of them shall I take? Both? one? or neither? Neither can be enjoy'd, If both remain alive. To take the widow Exasperates, makes mad her sister Goneril; And hardly shall I carry out my side, Her husband being alive. Now then, we'll use His countenance for the battle, which being done, Let her who would be rid of him devise His speedy taking off. As for the mercy Which he intends to Lear and to Cordelia- The battle done, and they within our power, Shall never see his pardon; for my state Stands on me to defend, not to debate. Exit.
lines: To both these sisters have I sworn my love;
Each jealous of the other, as the stung
Are of the adder. Which of them shall I take?
Both? one? or neither? Neither can be enjoy'd,
If both remain alive. To take the widow
Exasperates, makes mad her sister Goneril;
And hardly shall I carry out my side,
Her husband being alive. Now then, we'll use
His countenance for the battle, which being done,
Let her who would be rid of him devise
His speedy taking off. As for the mercy
Which he intends to Lear and to Cordelia-
The battle done, and they within our power,
Shall never see his pardon; for my state
Stands on me to defend, not to debate. Exit.

speeches.speech: Here, father, take the shadow of this tree For your good host. Pray that the right may thrive. If ever I return to you again, I'll bring you comfort.
lines: Here, father, take the shadow of this tree
For your good host. Pray that the right may thrive.
If ever I return to you again,
I'll bring you comfort.

speeches.speech: Grace go with you, sir!
lines: Grace go with you, sir!

speeches.speech: Away, old man! give me thy hand! away! King Lear hath lost, he and his daughter ta'en. Give me thy hand! come on!
lines: Away, old man! give me thy hand! away!
King Lear hath lost, he and his daughter ta'en.
Give me thy hand! come on!

speeches.speech: No further, sir. A man may rot even here.
lines: No further, sir. A man may rot even here.

speeches.speech: What, in ill thoughts again? Men must endure Their going hence, even as their coming hither; Ripeness is all. Come on.
lines: What, in ill thoughts again? Men must endure
Their going hence, even as their coming hither;
Ripeness is all. Come on.

speeches.speech: And that's true too.          Exeunt.
lines: And that's true too.          Exeunt.

speeches.speech: Some officers take them away. Good guard Until their greater pleasures first be known That are to censure them.
lines: Some officers take them away. Good guard
Until their greater pleasures first be known
That are to censure them.

speeches.speech: We are not the first Who with best meaning have incurr'd the worst. For thee, oppressed king, am I cast down; Myself could else outfrown false Fortune's frown. Shall we not see these daughters and these sisters?
lines: We are not the first
Who with best meaning have incurr'd the worst.
For thee, oppressed king, am I cast down;
Myself could else outfrown false Fortune's frown.
Shall we not see these daughters and these sisters?

speeches.speech: No, no, no, no! Come, let's away to prison. We two alone will sing like birds i' th' cage. When thou dost ask me blessing, I'll kneel down And ask of thee forgiveness. So we'll live, And pray, and sing, and tell old tales, and laugh At gilded butterflies, and hear poor rogues Talk of court news; and we'll talk with them too- Who loses and who wins; who's in, who's out- And take upon 's the mystery of things, As if we were God's spies; and we'll wear out, In a wall'd prison, packs and sects of great ones That ebb and flow by th' moon.
lines: No, no, no, no! Come, let's away to prison.
We two alone will sing like birds i' th' cage.
When thou dost ask me blessing, I'll kneel down
And ask of thee forgiveness. So we'll live,
And pray, and sing, and tell old tales, and laugh
At gilded butterflies, and hear poor rogues
Talk of court news; and we'll talk with them too-
Who loses and who wins; who's in, who's out-
And take upon 's the mystery of things,
As if we were God's spies; and we'll wear out,
In a wall'd prison, packs and sects of great ones
That ebb and flow by th' moon.

speeches.speech: Take them away.
lines: Take them away.

speeches.speech: Upon such sacrifices, my Cordelia, The gods themselves throw incense. Have I caught thee? He that parts us shall bring a brand from heaven And fire us hence like foxes. Wipe thine eyes. The goodyears shall devour 'em, flesh and fell, Ere they shall make us weep! We'll see 'em starv'd first. Come. Exeunt [Lear and Cordelia, guarded].
lines: Upon such sacrifices, my Cordelia,
The gods themselves throw incense. Have I caught thee?
He that parts us shall bring a brand from heaven
And fire us hence like foxes. Wipe thine eyes.
The goodyears shall devour 'em, flesh and fell,
Ere they shall make us weep! We'll see 'em starv'd first.
Come. Exeunt [Lear and Cordelia, guarded].

speeches.speech: Come hither, Captain; hark. Take thou this note [gives a paper]. Go follow them to prison. One step I have advanc'd thee. If thou dost As this instructs thee, thou dost make thy way To noble fortunes. Know thou this, that men Are as the time is. To be tender-minded Does not become a sword. Thy great employment Will not bear question. Either say thou'lt do't, Or thrive by other means.
lines: Come hither, Captain; hark.
Take thou this note [gives a paper]. Go follow them to prison.
One step I have advanc'd thee. If thou dost
As this instructs thee, thou dost make thy way
To noble fortunes. Know thou this, that men
Are as the time is. To be tender-minded
Does not become a sword. Thy great employment
Will not bear question. Either say thou'lt do't,
Or thrive by other means.

speeches.speech: I'll do't, my lord.
lines: I'll do't, my lord.

speeches.speech: About it! and write happy when th' hast done. Mark- I say, instantly; and carry it so As I have set it down.
lines: About it! and write happy when th' hast done.
Mark- I say, instantly; and carry it so
As I have set it down.

speeches.speech: I cannot draw a cart, nor eat dried oats; If it be man's work, I'll do't.    Exit.
lines: I cannot draw a cart, nor eat dried oats;
If it be man's work, I'll do't.    Exit.

speeches.speech: Sir, you have show'd to-day your valiant strain, And fortune led you well. You have the captives Who were the opposites of this day's strife. We do require them of you, so to use them As we shall find their merits and our safety May equally determine.
lines: Sir, you have show'd to-day your valiant strain,
And fortune led you well. You have the captives
Who were the opposites of this day's strife.
We do require them of you, so to use them
As we shall find their merits and our safety
May equally determine.

speeches.speech: Sir, I thought it fit To send the old and miserable King To some retention and appointed guard; Whose age has charms in it, whose title more, To pluck the common bosom on his side And turn our impress'd lances in our eyes Which do command them. With him I sent the Queen, My reason all the same; and they are ready To-morrow, or at further space, t' appear Where you shall hold your session. At this time We sweat and bleed: the friend hath lost his friend; And the best quarrels, in the heat, are curs'd By those that feel their sharpness. The question of Cordelia and her father Requires a fitter place.
lines: Sir, I thought it fit
To send the old and miserable King
To some retention and appointed guard;
Whose age has charms in it, whose title more,
To pluck the common bosom on his side
And turn our impress'd lances in our eyes
Which do command them. With him I sent the Queen,
My reason all the same; and they are ready
To-morrow, or at further space, t' appear
Where you shall hold your session. At this time
We sweat and bleed: the friend hath lost his friend;
And the best quarrels, in the heat, are curs'd
By those that feel their sharpness.
The question of Cordelia and her father
Requires a fitter place.

speeches.speech: Sir, by your patience, I hold you but a subject of this war, Not as a brother.
lines: Sir, by your patience,
I hold you but a subject of this war,
Not as a brother.

speeches.speech: That's as we list to grace him. Methinks our pleasure might have been demanded Ere you had spoke so far. He led our powers, Bore the commission of my place and person, The which immediacy may well stand up And call itself your brother.
lines: That's as we list to grace him.
Methinks our pleasure might have been demanded
Ere you had spoke so far. He led our powers,
Bore the commission of my place and person,
The which immediacy may well stand up
And call itself your brother.

speeches.speech: Not so hot! In his own grace he doth exalt himself More than in your addition.
lines: Not so hot!
In his own grace he doth exalt himself
More than in your addition.

speeches.speech: In my rights By me invested, he compeers the best.
lines: In my rights
By me invested, he compeers the best.

speeches.speech: That were the most if he should husband you.
lines: That were the most if he should husband you.

speeches.speech: Jesters do oft prove prophets.
lines: Jesters do oft prove prophets.

speeches.speech: Holla, holla! That eye that told you so look'd but asquint.
lines: Holla, holla!
That eye that told you so look'd but asquint.

speeches.speech: Lady, I am not well; else I should answer From a full-flowing stomach. General, Take thou my soldiers, prisoners, patrimony; Dispose of them, of me; the walls are thine. Witness the world that I create thee here My lord and master.
lines: Lady, I am not well; else I should answer
From a full-flowing stomach. General,
Take thou my soldiers, prisoners, patrimony;
Dispose of them, of me; the walls are thine.
Witness the world that I create thee here
My lord and master.

speeches.speech: Mean you to enjoy him?
lines: Mean you to enjoy him?

speeches.speech: The let-alone lies not in your good will.
lines: The let-alone lies not in your good will.

speeches.speech: Nor in thine, lord.
lines: Nor in thine, lord.

speeches.speech: Half-blooded fellow, yes.
lines: Half-blooded fellow, yes.

speeches.speech: [to Edmund] Let the drum strike, and prove my title thine.
lines: [to Edmund] Let the drum strike, and prove my title thine.

speeches.speech: Stay yet; hear reason. Edmund, I arrest thee On capital treason; and, in thine attaint, This gilded serpent [points to Goneril]. For your claim, fair sister, I bar it in the interest of my wife. 'Tis she is subcontracted to this lord, And I, her husband, contradict your banes. If you will marry, make your loves to me; My lady is bespoke.
lines: Stay yet; hear reason. Edmund, I arrest thee
On capital treason; and, in thine attaint,
This gilded serpent [points to Goneril]. For your claim, fair
sister,
I bar it in the interest of my wife.
'Tis she is subcontracted to this lord,
And I, her husband, contradict your banes.
If you will marry, make your loves to me;
My lady is bespoke.

speeches.speech: An interlude!
lines: An interlude!

speeches.speech: Thou art arm'd, Gloucester. Let the trumpet sound. If none appear to prove upon thy person Thy heinous, manifest, and many treasons, There is my pledge [throws down a glove]! I'll prove it on thy heart, Ere I taste bread, thou art in nothing less Than I have here proclaim'd thee.
lines: Thou art arm'd, Gloucester. Let the trumpet sound.
If none appear to prove upon thy person
Thy heinous, manifest, and many treasons,
There is my pledge [throws down a glove]! I'll prove it on thy
heart,
Ere I taste bread, thou art in nothing less
Than I have here proclaim'd thee.

speeches.speech: Sick, O, sick!
lines: Sick, O, sick!

speeches.speech: [aside] If not, I'll ne'er trust medicine.
lines: [aside] If not, I'll ne'er trust medicine.

speeches.speech: There's my exchange [throws down a glove]. What in the world he is That names me traitor, villain-like he lies. Call by thy trumpet. He that dares approach, On him, on you, who not? I will maintain My truth and honour firmly.
lines: There's my exchange [throws down a glove]. What in the world
he is
That names me traitor, villain-like he lies.
Call by thy trumpet. He that dares approach,
On him, on you, who not? I will maintain
My truth and honour firmly.

speeches.speech: A herald, ho!
lines: A herald, ho!

speeches.speech: A herald, ho, a herald!
lines: A herald, ho, a herald!

speeches.speech: Trust to thy single virtue; for thy soldiers, All levied in my name, have in my name Took their discharge.
lines: Trust to thy single virtue; for thy soldiers,
All levied in my name, have in my name
Took their discharge.

speeches.speech: My sickness grows upon me.
lines: My sickness grows upon me.

speeches.speech: She is not well. Convey her to my tent. [Exit Regan, led. Enter a Herald.] Come hither, herald. Let the trumpet sound, And read out this.
lines: She is not well. Convey her to my tent.
[Exit Regan, led. Enter a Herald.]
Come hither, herald. Let the trumpet sound,
And read out this.

speeches.speech: Sound, trumpet!                        A trumpet sounds.
lines: Sound, trumpet!                        A trumpet sounds.

speeches.speech: [reads] 'If any man of quality or degree within the lists of the army will maintain upon Edmund, supposed Earl of Gloucester, that he is a manifold traitor, let him appear by the third sound of the trumpet. He is bold in his defence.'
lines: [reads] 'If any man of quality or degree within the lists of
the army will maintain upon Edmund, supposed Earl of Gloucester,
that he is a manifold traitor, let him appear by the third sound
of the trumpet. He is bold in his defence.'

speeches.speech: Sound!                                     First trumpet.
lines: Sound!                                     First trumpet.

speeches.speech: Again!                                    Second trumpet.
lines: Again!                                    Second trumpet.

speeches.speech: Again!                                     Third trumpet.
lines: Again!                                     Third trumpet.

speeches.speech: Ask him his purposes, why he appears Upon this call o' th' trumpet.
lines: Ask him his purposes, why he appears
Upon this call o' th' trumpet.

speeches.speech: What are you? Your name, your quality? and why you answer This present summons?
lines: What are you?
Your name, your quality? and why you answer
This present summons?

speeches.speech: Know my name is lost; By treason's tooth bare-gnawn and canker-bit. Yet am I noble as the adversary I come to cope.
lines: Know my name is lost;
By treason's tooth bare-gnawn and canker-bit.
Yet am I noble as the adversary
I come to cope.

speeches.speech: Which is that adversary?
lines: Which is that adversary?

speeches.speech: What's he that speaks for Edmund Earl of Gloucester?
lines: What's he that speaks for Edmund Earl of Gloucester?

speeches.speech: Himself. What say'st thou to him?
lines: Himself. What say'st thou to him?

speeches.speech: Draw thy sword, That, if my speech offend a noble heart, Thy arm may do thee justice. Here is mine. Behold, it is the privilege of mine honours, My oath, and my profession. I protest- Maugre thy strength, youth, place, and eminence, Despite thy victor sword and fire-new fortune, Thy valour and thy heart- thou art a traitor; False to thy gods, thy brother, and thy father; Conspirant 'gainst this high illustrious prince; And from th' extremest upward of thy head To the descent and dust beneath thy foot, A most toad-spotted traitor. Say thou 'no,' This sword, this arm, and my best spirits are bent To prove upon thy heart, whereto I speak, Thou liest.
lines: Draw thy sword,
That, if my speech offend a noble heart,
Thy arm may do thee justice. Here is mine.
Behold, it is the privilege of mine honours,
My oath, and my profession. I protest-
Maugre thy strength, youth, place, and eminence,
Despite thy victor sword and fire-new fortune,
Thy valour and thy heart- thou art a traitor;
False to thy gods, thy brother, and thy father;
Conspirant 'gainst this high illustrious prince;
And from th' extremest upward of thy head
To the descent and dust beneath thy foot,
A most toad-spotted traitor. Say thou 'no,'
This sword, this arm, and my best spirits are bent
To prove upon thy heart, whereto I speak,
Thou liest.

speeches.speech: In wisdom I should ask thy name; But since thy outside looks so fair and warlike, And that thy tongue some say of breeding breathes, What safe and nicely I might well delay By rule of knighthood, I disdain and spurn. Back do I toss those treasons to thy head; With the hell-hated lie o'erwhelm thy heart; Which- for they yet glance by and scarcely bruise- This sword of mine shall give them instant way Where they shall rest for ever. Trumpets, speak!
lines: In wisdom I should ask thy name;
But since thy outside looks so fair and warlike,
And that thy tongue some say of breeding breathes,
What safe and nicely I might well delay
By rule of knighthood, I disdain and spurn.
Back do I toss those treasons to thy head;
With the hell-hated lie o'erwhelm thy heart;
Which- for they yet glance by and scarcely bruise-
This sword of mine shall give them instant way
Where they shall rest for ever. Trumpets, speak!

speeches.speech: Save him, save him!
lines: Save him, save him!

speeches.speech: This is mere practice, Gloucester. By th' law of arms thou wast not bound to answer An unknown opposite. Thou art not vanquish'd, But cozen'd and beguil'd.
lines: This is mere practice, Gloucester.
By th' law of arms thou wast not bound to answer
An unknown opposite. Thou art not vanquish'd,
But cozen'd and beguil'd.

speeches.speech: Shut your mouth, dame, Or with this paper shall I stop it. [Shows her her letter to Edmund.]- [To Edmund]. Hold, sir. [To Goneril] Thou worse than any name, read thine own evil. No tearing, lady! I perceive you know it.
lines: Shut your mouth, dame,
Or with this paper shall I stop it. [Shows her her letter to
Edmund.]- [To Edmund]. Hold, sir.
[To Goneril] Thou worse than any name, read thine own evil.
No tearing, lady! I perceive you know it.

speeches.speech: Say if I do- the laws are mine, not thine. Who can arraign me for't?
lines: Say if I do- the laws are mine, not thine.
Who can arraign me for't?

speeches.speech: Most monstrous! Know'st thou this paper?
lines: Most monstrous!
Know'st thou this paper?

speeches.speech: Ask me not what I know.          Exit.
lines: Ask me not what I know.          Exit.

speeches.speech: Go after her. She's desperate; govern her.
lines: Go after her. She's desperate; govern her.

speeches.speech: What, you have charg'd me with, that have I done, And more, much more. The time will bring it out. 'Tis past, and so am I.- But what art thou That hast this fortune on me? If thou'rt noble, I do forgive thee.
lines: What, you have charg'd me with, that have I done,
And more, much more. The time will bring it out.
'Tis past, and so am I.- But what art thou
That hast this fortune on me? If thou'rt noble,
I do forgive thee.

speeches.speech: Let's exchange charity. I am no less in blood than thou art, Edmund; If more, the more th' hast wrong'd me. My name is Edgar and thy father's son. The gods are just, and of our pleasant vices Make instruments to scourge us. The dark and vicious place where thee he got Cost him his eyes.
lines: Let's exchange charity.
I am no less in blood than thou art, Edmund;
If more, the more th' hast wrong'd me.
My name is Edgar and thy father's son.
The gods are just, and of our pleasant vices
Make instruments to scourge us.
The dark and vicious place where thee he got
Cost him his eyes.

speeches.speech: Th' hast spoken right; 'tis true. The wheel is come full circle; I am here.
lines: Th' hast spoken right; 'tis true.
The wheel is come full circle; I am here.

speeches.speech: Methought thy very gait did prophesy A royal nobleness. I must embrace thee. Let sorrow split my heart if ever I Did hate thee, or thy father!
lines: Methought thy very gait did prophesy
A royal nobleness. I must embrace thee.
Let sorrow split my heart if ever I
Did hate thee, or thy father!

speeches.speech: Worthy prince, I know't.
lines: Worthy prince, I know't.

speeches.speech: Where have you hid yourself? How have you known the miseries of your father?
lines: Where have you hid yourself?
How have you known the miseries of your father?

speeches.speech: By nursing them, my lord. List a brief tale; And when 'tis told, O that my heart would burst! The bloody proclamation to escape That follow'd me so near (O, our lives' sweetness! That with the pain of death would hourly die Rather than die at once!) taught me to shift Into a madman's rags, t' assume a semblance That very dogs disdain'd; and in this habit Met I my father with his bleeding rings, Their precious stones new lost; became his guide, Led him, begg'd for him, sav'd him from despair; Never (O fault!) reveal'd myself unto him Until some half hour past, when I was arm'd, Not sure, though hoping of this good success, I ask'd his blessing, and from first to last Told him my pilgrimage. But his flaw'd heart (Alack, too weak the conflict to support!) 'Twixt two extremes of passion, joy and grief, Burst smilingly.
lines: By nursing them, my lord. List a brief tale;
And when 'tis told, O that my heart would burst!
The bloody proclamation to escape
That follow'd me so near (O, our lives' sweetness!
That with the pain of death would hourly die
Rather than die at once!) taught me to shift
Into a madman's rags, t' assume a semblance
That very dogs disdain'd; and in this habit
Met I my father with his bleeding rings,
Their precious stones new lost; became his guide,
Led him, begg'd for him, sav'd him from despair;
Never (O fault!) reveal'd myself unto him
Until some half hour past, when I was arm'd,
Not sure, though hoping of this good success,
I ask'd his blessing, and from first to last
Told him my pilgrimage. But his flaw'd heart
(Alack, too weak the conflict to support!)
'Twixt two extremes of passion, joy and grief,
Burst smilingly.

speeches.speech: This speech of yours hath mov'd me, And shall perchance do good; but speak you on; You look as you had something more to say.
lines: This speech of yours hath mov'd me,
And shall perchance do good; but speak you on;
You look as you had something more to say.

speeches.speech: If there be more, more woful, hold it in; For I am almost ready to dissolve, Hearing of this.
lines: If there be more, more woful, hold it in;
For I am almost ready to dissolve,
Hearing of this.

speeches.speech: This would have seem'd a period To such as love not sorrow; but another, To amplify too much, would make much more, And top extremity. Whilst I was big in clamour, came there a man, Who, having seen me in my worst estate, Shunn'd my abhorr'd society; but then, finding Who 'twas that so endur'd, with his strong arms He fastened on my neck, and bellowed out As he'd burst heaven; threw him on my father; Told the most piteous tale of Lear and him That ever ear receiv'd; which in recounting His grief grew puissant, and the strings of life Began to crack. Twice then the trumpets sounded, And there I left him tranc'd.
lines: This would have seem'd a period
To such as love not sorrow; but another,
To amplify too much, would make much more,
And top extremity.
Whilst I was big in clamour, came there a man,
Who, having seen me in my worst estate,
Shunn'd my abhorr'd society; but then, finding
Who 'twas that so endur'd, with his strong arms
He fastened on my neck, and bellowed out
As he'd burst heaven; threw him on my father;
Told the most piteous tale of Lear and him
That ever ear receiv'd; which in recounting
His grief grew puissant, and the strings of life
Began to crack. Twice then the trumpets sounded,
And there I left him tranc'd.

speeches.speech: But who was this?
lines: But who was this?

speeches.speech: Kent, sir, the banish'd Kent; who in disguise Followed his enemy king and did him service Improper for a slave.
lines: Kent, sir, the banish'd Kent; who in disguise
Followed his enemy king and did him service
Improper for a slave.

speeches.speech: Help, help! O, help!
lines: Help, help! O, help!

speeches.speech: What kind of help?
lines: What kind of help?

speeches.speech: Speak, man.
lines: Speak, man.

speeches.speech: What means that bloody knife?
lines: What means that bloody knife?

speeches.speech: 'Tis hot, it smokes. It came even from the heart of- O! she's dead!
lines: 'Tis hot, it smokes.
It came even from the heart of- O! she's dead!

speeches.speech: Who dead? Speak, man.
lines: Who dead? Speak, man.

speeches.speech: Your lady, sir, your lady! and her sister By her is poisoned; she hath confess'd it.
lines: Your lady, sir, your lady! and her sister
By her is poisoned; she hath confess'd it.

speeches.speech: I was contracted to them both. All three Now marry in an instant.
lines: I was contracted to them both. All three
Now marry in an instant.

speeches.speech: Here comes Kent.
lines: Here comes Kent.

speeches.speech: Produce their bodies, be they alive or dead. [Exit Gentleman.] This judgement of the heavens, that makes us tremble Touches us not with pity. O, is this he? The time will not allow the compliment That very manners urges.
lines: Produce their bodies, be they alive or dead.
[Exit Gentleman.]
This judgement of the heavens, that makes us tremble
Touches us not with pity. O, is this he?
The time will not allow the compliment
That very manners urges.

speeches.speech: I am come To bid my king and master aye good night. Is he not here?
lines: I am come
To bid my king and master aye good night.
Is he not here?

speeches.speech: Great thing of us forgot! Speak, Edmund, where's the King? and where's Cordelia? [The bodies of Goneril and Regan are brought in.] Seest thou this object, Kent?
lines: Great thing of us forgot!
Speak, Edmund, where's the King? and where's Cordelia?
[The bodies of Goneril and Regan are brought in.]
Seest thou this object, Kent?

speeches.speech: Alack, why thus?
lines: Alack, why thus?

speeches.speech: Yet Edmund was belov'd. The one the other poisoned for my sake, And after slew herself.
lines: Yet Edmund was belov'd.
The one the other poisoned for my sake,
And after slew herself.

speeches.speech: Even so. Cover their faces.
lines: Even so. Cover their faces.

speeches.speech: I pant for life. Some good I mean to do, Despite of mine own nature. Quickly send (Be brief in't) to the castle; for my writ Is on the life of Lear and on Cordelia. Nay, send in time.
lines: I pant for life. Some good I mean to do,
Despite of mine own nature. Quickly send
(Be brief in't) to the castle; for my writ
Is on the life of Lear and on Cordelia.
Nay, send in time.

speeches.speech: Run, run, O, run!
lines: Run, run, O, run!

speeches.speech: To who, my lord? Who has the office? Send Thy token of reprieve.
lines: To who, my lord? Who has the office? Send
Thy token of reprieve.

speeches.speech: Well thought on. Take my sword; Give it the Captain.
lines: Well thought on. Take my sword;
Give it the Captain.

speeches.speech: Haste thee for thy life. [Exit Edgar.]
lines: Haste thee for thy life. [Exit Edgar.]

speeches.speech: He hath commission from thy wife and me To hang Cordelia in the prison and To lay the blame upon her own despair That she fordid herself.
lines: He hath commission from thy wife and me
To hang Cordelia in the prison and
To lay the blame upon her own despair
That she fordid herself.

speeches.speech: The gods defend her! Bear him hence awhile.
lines: The gods defend her! Bear him hence awhile.

speeches.speech: Howl, howl, howl, howl! O, you are men of stone. Had I your tongues and eyes, I'ld use them so That heaven's vault should crack. She's gone for ever! I know when one is dead, and when one lives. She's dead as earth. Lend me a looking glass. If that her breath will mist or stain the stone, Why, then she lives.
lines: Howl, howl, howl, howl! O, you are men of stone.
Had I your tongues and eyes, I'ld use them so
That heaven's vault should crack. She's gone for ever!
I know when one is dead, and when one lives.
She's dead as earth. Lend me a looking glass.
If that her breath will mist or stain the stone,
Why, then she lives.

speeches.speech: Is this the promis'd end?
lines: Is this the promis'd end?

speeches.speech: Or image of that horror?
lines: Or image of that horror?

speeches.speech: Fall and cease!
lines: Fall and cease!

speeches.speech: This feather stirs; she lives! If it be so, It is a chance which does redeem all sorrows That ever I have felt.
lines: This feather stirs; she lives! If it be so,
It is a chance which does redeem all sorrows
That ever I have felt.

speeches.speech: O my good master!
lines: O my good master!

speeches.speech: Prithee away!
lines: Prithee away!

speeches.speech: 'Tis noble Kent, your friend.
lines: 'Tis noble Kent, your friend.

speeches.speech: A plague upon you, murderers, traitors all! I might have sav'd her; now she's gone for ever! Cordelia, Cordelia! stay a little. Ha! What is't thou say'st, Her voice was ever soft, Gentle, and low- an excellent thing in woman. I kill'd the slave that was a-hanging thee.
lines: A plague upon you, murderers, traitors all!
I might have sav'd her; now she's gone for ever!
Cordelia, Cordelia! stay a little. Ha!
What is't thou say'st, Her voice was ever soft,
Gentle, and low- an excellent thing in woman.
I kill'd the slave that was a-hanging thee.

speeches.speech: 'Tis true, my lords, he did.
lines: 'Tis true, my lords, he did.

speeches.speech: Did I not, fellow? I have seen the day, with my good biting falchion I would have made them skip. I am old now, And these same crosses spoil me. Who are you? Mine eyes are not o' th' best. I'll tell you straight.
lines: Did I not, fellow?
I have seen the day, with my good biting falchion
I would have made them skip. I am old now,
And these same crosses spoil me. Who are you?
Mine eyes are not o' th' best. I'll tell you straight.

speeches.speech: If fortune brag of two she lov'd and hated, One of them we behold.
lines: If fortune brag of two she lov'd and hated,
One of them we behold.

speeches.speech: This' a dull sight. Are you not Kent?
lines: This' a dull sight. Are you not Kent?

speeches.speech: The same- Your servant Kent. Where is your servant Caius?
lines: The same-
Your servant Kent. Where is your servant Caius?

speeches.speech: He's a good fellow, I can tell you that. He'll strike, and quickly too. He's dead and rotten.
lines: He's a good fellow, I can tell you that.
He'll strike, and quickly too. He's dead and rotten.

speeches.speech: No, my good lord; I am the very man-
lines: No, my good lord; I am the very man-

speeches.speech: I'll see that straight.
lines: I'll see that straight.

speeches.speech: That from your first of difference and decay Have followed your sad steps.
lines: That from your first of difference and decay
Have followed your sad steps.

speeches.speech: You're welcome hither.
lines: You're welcome hither.

speeches.speech: Nor no man else! All's cheerless, dark, and deadly. Your eldest daughters have fordone themselves, And desperately are dead.
lines: Nor no man else! All's cheerless, dark, and deadly.
Your eldest daughters have fordone themselves,
And desperately are dead.

speeches.speech: Ay, so I think.
lines: Ay, so I think.

speeches.speech: He knows not what he says; and vain is it That we present us to him.
lines: He knows not what he says; and vain is it
That we present us to him.

speeches.speech: Very bootless.
lines: Very bootless.

speeches.speech: Edmund is dead, my lord.
lines: Edmund is dead, my lord.

speeches.speech: That's but a trifle here. You lords and noble friends, know our intent. What comfort to this great decay may come Shall be applied. For us, we will resign, During the life of this old Majesty, To him our absolute power; [to Edgar and Kent] you to your rights; With boot, and such addition as your honours Have more than merited.- All friends shall taste The wages of their virtue, and all foes The cup of their deservings.- O, see, see!
lines: That's but a trifle here.
You lords and noble friends, know our intent.
What comfort to this great decay may come
Shall be applied. For us, we will resign,
During the life of this old Majesty,
To him our absolute power; [to Edgar and Kent] you to your
rights;
With boot, and such addition as your honours
Have more than merited.- All friends shall taste
The wages of their virtue, and all foes
The cup of their deservings.- O, see, see!

speeches.speech: And my poor fool is hang'd! No, no, no life! Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, And thou no breath at all? Thou'lt come no more, Never, never, never, never, never! Pray you undo this button. Thank you, sir. Do you see this? Look on her! look! her lips! Look there, look there!                            He dies.
lines: And my poor fool is hang'd! No, no, no life!
Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life,
And thou no breath at all? Thou'lt come no more,
Never, never, never, never, never!
Pray you undo this button. Thank you, sir.
Do you see this? Look on her! look! her lips!
Look there, look there!                            He dies.

speeches.speech: He faints! My lord, my lord!
lines: He faints! My lord, my lord!

speeches.speech: Break, heart; I prithee break!
lines: Break, heart; I prithee break!

speeches.speech: Look up, my lord.
lines: Look up, my lord.

speeches.speech: Vex not his ghost. O, let him pass! He hates him That would upon the rack of this tough world Stretch him out longer.
lines: Vex not his ghost. O, let him pass! He hates him
That would upon the rack of this tough world
Stretch him out longer.

speeches.speech: He is gone indeed.
lines: He is gone indeed.

speeches.speech: The wonder is, he hath endur'd so long. He but usurp'd his life.
lines: The wonder is, he hath endur'd so long.
He but usurp'd his life.

speeches.speech: Bear them from hence. Our present business Is general woe. [To Kent and Edgar] Friends of my soul, you twain Rule in this realm, and the gor'd state sustain.
lines: Bear them from hence. Our present business
Is general woe. [To Kent and Edgar] Friends of my soul, you
twain
Rule in this realm, and the gor'd state sustain.

speeches.speech: I have a journey, sir, shortly to go. My master calls me; I must not say no.
lines: I have a journey, sir, shortly to go.
My master calls me; I must not say no.

speeches.speech: The weight of this sad time we must obey, Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say. The oldest have borne most; we that are young Shall never see so much, nor live so long.
lines: The weight of this sad time we must obey,
Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say.
The oldest have borne most; we that are young
Shall never see so much, nor live so long.
